```mermaid
    ground1[CHASSIS GROUND] -->|Return Path| battery1
```

### 4.2 Detailed Component-Level Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    operator1[OPERATOR TURNS KEY] --> check1{ALL SAFETY CHECKS PASS}
    check1 -->|Transmission Neutral| check2
    check1 -->|Clutch Pressed| check2
    check1 -->|Operator Seated| check2
    check2 -->|Yes| energize1[Energize Solenoid Coil]
    energize1 --> push1[Pinion Slides Forward]
    energize1 --> close1[Close Main Contacts]
    push1 --> mesh1[Pinion Meshes with Ring Gear]
    close1 --> current1[Current Flows to Starter Motor]
    current1 --> motor1[Armature Rotates]
    motor1 --> crank1[Engine Cranks at 150-200 rpm]
    crank1 --> comb1{Compression Achieved}
    comb1 -->|Yes| fuel1[Fuel Injection Begins]
    fuel1 --> start1[Engine Self-Sustained]
    start1 --> release1[Operator Releases Key]
    release1 --> disengage1[Solenoid De-energizes]
    disengage1 --> retract1[Pinion Retracts]
    motor1 -.->|Speed Rise| backemf1[Back EMF Increases]
    backemf1 -.->|Reduces Current| current1
```

### 4.3 Circuit Schematic of the Starting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BATTERY_BANK[BATTERY SECTION]
        batA[Battery Positive 12V]
        batB[Battery Negative GND]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_CIRCUIT[CONTROL CIRCUIT LOW CURRENT]
        keyA[Ignition Switch OFF-ON-START]
        lampA[Warning Lamp]
        relayA[Solenoid Pull-In Coil]
        relayB[Solenoid Hold-In Coil]
        interA[Clutch Interlock Switch NC]
        interB[Neutral Interlock Switch NC]
    end
    subgraph POWER_CIRCUIT[POWER CIRCUIT HIGH CURRENT]
        solA[Solenoid Main Contacts]
        fieldA[Starter Field Windings Series]
        armA[Starter Armature]
        pinionA[Drive Pinion to Ring Gear]
    end
    batA --> solA
    solA --> fieldA
    fieldA --> armA
    armA --> batB
    keyA --> interA
    interA --> interB
    interB --> relayA
    relayA --> batB
    keyA --> relayB
    relayB --> batB
    keyA --> lampA
    lampA --> batB
    solA -.Controls.-> fieldA
    armA -.Mechanically Coupled.-> pinionA
```

### 4.4 Working Cycle Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> CRANK : Key Turned to START
    CRANK --> SOLENOID_ENERGIZE : Safety Checks Pass
    SOLENOID_ENERGIZE --> PINION_MESH : Plunger Pushes Forward
    PINION_MESH --> MAIN_CONTACT_CLOSE : Plunger Fully Extended
    MAIN_CONTACT_CLOSE --> MOTOR_ROTATE : High Current Flows
    MOTOR_ROTATE --> ENGINE_CRANK : Pinion Drives Ring Gear
    ENGINE_CRANK --> SELF_SUSTAINED : Engine Fires
    SELF_SUSTAINED --> KEY_RELEASE : Operator Releases Key
    KEY_RELEASE --> SOLENOID_DEENERGIZE : Coil Loses Power
    SOLENOID_DEENERGIZE --> PINION_RETRACT : Spring Returns Plunger
    PINION_RETRACT --> OFF : System Returns to Idle
    OFF --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU 2024 Exams:**
> Always label the **two distinct circuits** in your answer sheet diagrams:
> 1. **Control Circuit (Low Current, ~1–2 A):** Ignition switch → Safety interlocks → Solenoid coil → Ground.
> 2. **Power Circuit (High Current, 300–800 A):** Battery → Solenoid contacts → Field → Armature → Ground.
> Mixing these two is a common KTU valuation penalty.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
> **Define the term "Cold Cranking Amps (CCA)" of a tractor battery. State its typical range for a 12V tractor battery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** *Cold Cranking Amps (CCA)* is the maximum current a fully charged battery can deliver for **30 seconds** at a temperature of **−18°C (0°F)** while maintaining a terminal voltage of at least **7.2V** (for 12V battery) or above the minimum cutoff.
- **Typical Range (1 mark):** For modern tractors, CCA ranges from **600 to 1000 amps**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
> **Why is a DC series wound motor used as a starter motor in tractors instead of a DC shunt motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A series motor develops **maximum torque at zero speed** (locked-rotor condition), which is exactly the requirement during engine cranking.
- (1 mark) The torque in a series motor follows $T \propto I_a^2$, providing **very high starting torque** sufficient to overcome diesel engine compression resistance.
- (1 mark) In contrast, a shunt motor has constant flux, develops low starting torque ($T \propto I_a$ only), and would stall without external starting resistance.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of the electric starting system used in modern tractors. Explain the role of the solenoid switch in detail. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 24V tractor starter motor has armature resistance 0.03 Ω and series field resistance 0.02 Ω. The battery has internal resistance 0.01 Ω. Calculate:
> (i) The locked rotor current.
> (ii) The cranking torque if $k_1 k_2 = 0.008$.
> (iii) Comment on the no-load speed of the starter motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

The electric starting system consists of the following components (3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for explanation):

**Diagram:** Draw a labelled diagram showing Battery, Ignition Switch, Safety Interlocks, Solenoid, Starter Motor (Field + Armature), Pinion, Ring Gear, and the complete wiring circuit.

**Working Principle:**
1. (1 mark) Operator turns the ignition key to the START position.
2. (1 mark) The low-current control circuit energizes the solenoid pull-in and hold-in coils after passing through safety interlocks (clutch switch, neutral switch).
3. (1 mark) The solenoid plunger is pulled forward by electromagnetic force, performing **two functions simultaneously**: (i) closing the high-current main contacts between battery and starter motor, and (ii) mechanically pushing the pinion gear into mesh with the flywheel ring gear.
4. (1 mark) Heavy current (300–800 A) flows from the battery through the field windings and armature, causing the starter motor to rotate at high speed. Through the gear reduction (10:1 to 20:1), this rotates the engine crankshaft at 150–200 rpm, achieving compression ignition.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_b = 24$ V
- $R_a = 0.03 \ \Omega$
- $R_{se} = 0.02 \ \Omega$
- $R_{battery} = 0.01 \ \Omega$
- $k_1 k_2 = 0.008$

**(i) Locked Rotor Current (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{locked} &= \dfrac{V_b}{R_{battery} + R_a + R_{se}} \\
&= \dfrac{24}{0.01 + 0.03 + 0.02} \\
&= \dfrac{24}{0.06} \\
\Rightarrow \quad I_{locked} &= 400 \ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cranking Torque (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cranking} &= k_1 k_2 \cdot I_a^2 \\
&= 0.008 \times (400)^2 \\
&= 0.008 \times 160{,}000 \\
\Rightarrow \quad T_{cranking} &= 1280 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Comment on No-Load Speed (1 mark):**

In a series motor, at no-load, the armature current approaches zero, hence field flux $\phi \to 0$. Since $N \propto \dfrac{V_b}{\phi}$, the no-load speed tends to **dangerously high (theoretically infinite) values**. This is why starter motors must **never be operated without mechanical load**, and the pinion must always remain meshed with the ring gear during testing.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain the construction and working of the **Bendix drive** mechanism used in tractor starter motors. Compare it with the **follower-type drive** system. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** List and explain the **safety interlock devices** used in tractor starting systems. Why is the neutral safety switch essential for automatic transmission tractors? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Bendix Drive (4 marks):**
- (1 mark) The Bendix drive consists of a pinion gear mounted on a hollow threaded sleeve that engages with helical splines on the starter motor shaft.
- (1 mark) When the starter motor begins to rotate, the pinion inertia causes it to **screw forward** along the shaft threads until it meshes with the flywheel ring gear.
- (1 mark) Once the engine fires and its speed exceeds the starter speed, the ring gear drives the pinion **backwards along the threads**, disengaging it automatically.
- (1 mark) Limitation: High inertia, engagement shock, not suitable for high-speed starter motors.

**Follower-Type Drive (Barrel Type) — Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Bendix Drive | Follower (Barrel) Drive |
|---------|--------------|------------------------|
| Engagement Method | Inertia screw action | Lever-shift actuator |
| Engagement Smoothness | Slightly jerky | Smooth |
| Pinion Adjustment | Limited | Precise via shift lever |
| Application | Older tractors | Modern high-speed starters |

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Safety Interlock Devices (4 marks for listing):**

1. **Neutral Safety Switch (1 mark):** Ensures engine cannot crank unless transmission is in NEUTRAL (for automatic transmission) or clutch is pressed (manual transmission).
2. **Clutch Safety Switch (1 mark):** Series interlock; engine cranks only if clutch pedal is fully depressed.
3. **Operator Presence Switch (1 mark):** Modern ROPS-equipped tractors require the operator to be seated.
4. **PTO Engagement Interlock (1 mark):** Prevents starting when Power Take-Off is engaged.

**Why Neutral Safety Switch is Essential (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Prevents **sudden lurching** of the tractor when engine fires while in gear, which could cause fatal accidents to operators or bystanders.
- (1 mark) Reduces mechanical stress on the driveline (clutch, gearbox, axle).
- (1 mark) Complies with **ISO 26322 and AIS safety standards** for agricultural tractors — KTU emphasizes safety in the 2024 scheme.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mistakes students make in Starting System questions (costing 2–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw both Control and Power circuits** separately in diagrams. Always label currents in amperes.
> 2. **Mixing up series motor no-load behavior** — saying "it stops at no-load" instead of "it races to dangerously high speed."
> 3. **Using $T = k \cdot I_a$ instead of $T = k \cdot I_a^2$** for series motor. This is a **single-character error costing 2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring battery internal resistance** in numerical problems — KTU examiners specifically test whether you include $R_{battery}$.
> 5. **Not mentioning "30-second crank limit"** — students lose marks when describing starter operation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Starting Systems**

- [x] **System Function:** Convert battery electrical energy into mechanical cranking energy for the engine.
- [x] **Cranking Speed Requirement:** 150–200 rpm for diesel tractor engines.
- [x] **Core Components:** Battery, Ignition Switch, Safety Interlocks, Solenoid, Starter Motor (Series DC), Pinion + Ring Gear.
- [x] **Motor Type:** DC Series Wound Motor (provides maximum torque at zero speed).
- [x] **Key Formula:** $T \propto I_a^2$ (quadratic torque-current relation — **signature of series motor**).
- [x] **Key Formula:** $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ (inverse square-root speed-torque relation).
- [x] **Battery Voltage:** 12V (standard) or 24V (heavy-duty, two batteries in series).
- [x] **Battery CCA Rating:** 600–1000 amps at −18°C.
- [x] **Gear Reduction:** 10:1 to 20:1 between starter pinion and ring gear.
- [x] **Solenoid Function:** Dual role — closes high-current circuit + mechanically engages pinion.
- [x] **Cranking Time Limit:** Maximum 30 seconds, then 2-minute cooling rest.
- [x] **Safety Interlocks:** Neutral, Clutch, Operator Presence, PTO.
- [x] **Bendix Drive:** Inertia-based pinion engagement via helical splines.
- [x] **No-Load Speed:** **Dangerously high** — never test starter without load.
- [x] **Starter Efficiency:** 60–75% typical.
- [x] **Two Circuits to Remember:** Control circuit (low current, ~1–2 A) and Power circuit (high current, 300–800 A).

> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Mnemonic — "S-C-R-A-N-K":**
> **S** — Safety Interlocks (Neutral, Clutch)
> **C** — Control Circuit (Ignition Switch + Solenoid Coil)
> **R** — Ring Gear (Flywheel mounted)
> **A** — Armature + Field (Series Wound)
> **N** — No-Load Speed (dangerous — never test)
> **K** — Kinetic Cranking (150–200 rpm for diesel)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lights and Fuses. Wheels and Tyres in Tractors - Tyre Characteristics and Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT613 - TRACTORS AND FARM EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lights and Fuses. Wheels and Tyres in Tractors - Tyre Characteristics and Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lights in Tractors

### Formal Definition
A **tractor lighting system** is an integrated electrical network of lamps and reflectors designed to provide illumination for safe field operation during low-light conditions, road transport, and to signal the tractor's presence, position, and intent to other road users. The system operates on a **12 V DC** (or in some heavy models, **24 V DC**) supply drawn from the tractor's battery and regulated by the alternator/charging system.

### Components of the Tractor Lighting System

| Light Type | Function | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Headlights (Main Beam)** | Long-range forward illumination for road driving | Front grille / fenders |
| **Work Lights (Field Lights)** | Wide-angle, high-intensity illumination of implement/field area at night | Cab roof, fender extensions |
| **Tail Lights** | Red rear position lamps visible from behind | Rear fenders |
| **Brake / Stop Lights** | Brighten when brake pedal is depressed | Integrated within tail light cluster |
| **Turn Indicators (Flashers)** | Amber flashing lights for direction change | Front and rear corners |
| **Beacon / Rotating Lamp** | Warns of slow-moving vehicle in road traffic | Cab roof |
| **Number Plate Lamp** | White light illuminating rear registration plate | Above number plate |
| **Instrument Panel Lamps** | Backlight for gauges and warning indicators | Dashboard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Tractors used on public roads in India (as per Central Motor Vehicle Rules - CMVR) **must** have headlamps, tail lamps, stop lamps, turn indicators, reflectors, a number plate lamp, and a **triangular slow-moving vehicle (SMV) emblem** at the rear.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the tractor lighting system as the **"eyes and voice"** of the tractor. Just as a person needs eyes to see in the dark and a voice to communicate intentions, the tractor needs lights to see the field/work area and signal its movements (turning, braking) to other drivers and pedestrians. The **beacon light** is the tractor's "shout" saying *"I am slow, please be patient!"*

---

## 1.2 Fuses in Tractors

### Formal Definition
A **fuse** is a sacrificial current-limiting safety device placed in series with an electrical circuit. It contains a calibrated metal wire or strip that **melts and breaks the circuit** when the current exceeds a safe predetermined value, thereby protecting wiring, switches, and components from overcurrent, short circuits, and fire hazards.

### Characteristics of Tractor Fuses
- **Type:** Predominantly **blade-type (ATO/ATC)** or **mini-blade** fuses, colour-coded by current rating.
- **Voltage Rating:** Typically **32 V DC** (well above tractor's 12 V system).
- **Current Ratings (Standard Colours):**
  - **3 A – Violet**
  - **5 A – Tan**
  - **7.5 A – Brown**
  - **10 A – Red**
  - **15 A – Blue**
  - **20 A – Yellow**
  - **25 A – Clear/White**
  - **30 A – Green**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fuses Are Critical in Tractors:** Tractors operate in dusty, vibrating, and moisture-laden environments. Wiring chafing, rodent damage, and water ingress frequently cause short circuits. A fuse rated, for example, at **15 A** will blow in milliseconds if current surges to **30 A**, saving the harness and the alternator.

### Conceptual Analogy
A fuse is the **"weakest link"** of an electrical chain — deliberately engineered to be the part that breaks first. It is like a **pressure relief valve on a water pipeline**: when pressure (current) becomes dangerously high, it opens (blows) to protect the whole system downstream.

---

## 1.3 Wheels and Tyres in Tractors

### Formal Definition
A **tractor wheel-tyre assembly** is the primary ground-engaging support and traction system of a tractor. It transmits engine power to the soil (via tractive force), supports the static and dynamic weight of the tractor, cushions shocks from ground irregularities, and provides steering control. Tractors almost universally use **pneumatic (air-filled) tyres** because of the low ground pressure and high flexibility required for soft soil.

### Fundamental Tyre Functions
1. **Load Support** – Carries axle weight.
2. **Traction** – Converts wheel torque into drawbar pull.
3. **Cushioning** – Absorbs shocks from field undulations.
4. **Steering** – Front tyres guide the tractor.
5. **Flotation** – Distributes weight to minimize soil compaction.

### Conceptual Analogy
The tractor tyre is the **"shoe"** of the tractor. Just as a farmer chooses rubber boots for muddy fields (soft, grippy) versus sports shoes for road running (hard, smooth), a tractor uses **deep-lug rear agricultural tyres** for the field and **rib-pattern front tyres** for steering on the road. The tyre's "shoe size" must match the task and the load.

---

## 1.4 Tyre Characteristics and Specifications

### Formal Definition
**Tyre characteristics** are the engineering attributes that define a tyre's load-bearing capacity, traction efficiency, ground pressure, speed capability, and operational life. **Tyre specifications** are the standardised codes that uniquely identify a tyre's size, construction, ply rating, tread pattern, and service conditions as per **Indian Standard IS 11973** and **E/ECE-R 106** regulations.

### Key Tyre Characteristics
- **Section Width** – The linear distance across the widest part of the inflated tyre (in inches or mm).
- **Overall Diameter** – The total height of the inflated tyre (2 × rolling radius).
- **Rolling Radius (Rᵣ)** – The effective radius from wheel centre to ground during loaded rolling motion.
- **Static Loaded Radius (Rₛ)** – The distance from wheel centre to ground when stationary under load.
- **Section Height** – The radial distance from bead seat to outer tread.
- **Aspect Ratio (AR)** – Ratio of section height to section width × 100.
- **Rim Diameter** – The diameter of the wheel rim on which the tyre is mounted.
- **Ply Rating (PR)** – An index of load-carrying capacity, not the literal number of cotton plies.
- **Tread Pattern** – The lug/rib design on the outer surface (R1, R2, R3, R4).
- **Load Index (LI)** – Numerical code for maximum load at a given inflation pressure.
- **Speed Symbol** – Letter code (e.g., A6 = 30 km/h, A8 = 40 km/h) for max sustained speed.
- **Rolling Circumference (Cᵣ)** – The distance travelled in one wheel revolution.
- **Contact Patch (Footprint)** – The area of tyre in contact with the ground.
- **Inflation Pressure (Pᵢ)** – The internal air pressure, usually in **bar** or **psi (lbf/in²)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A tractor rear tyre of size **12.4-28** is interpreted as: section width **12.4 inches**, hyphens indicate **bias-ply** construction, and **28** is the **rim diameter in inches**. A radial equivalent is written as **12.4R28** (inch) or **320/85R28** (metric, where 320 mm is width and 85 is aspect ratio %).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a tyre specification as a **"passport"**: it tells you everything about who the tyre is, where it comes from (type), what it can carry (load index), how fast it can travel (speed symbol), and what shoes it is wearing (tread pattern). Without the passport (specification), you cannot match the right tyre to the right tractor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Analysis — Lights in Tractors

### Working Principle
Tractor lamps use **incandescent halogen bulbs**, **HID xenon**, or modern **LED clusters**. The lamp body includes:
- **Reflector** – Parabolic mirror that projects bulb light into a directional beam.
- **Lens/Diffuser** – Glass/polycarbonate cover that shapes and protects the beam.
- **Bulb** – The light source (typically H4 dual-filament for combined high/low beam).
- **Wiring & Switch** – Controlled via dashboard switches and relays.

The **high beam (main beam)** projects light straight ahead for maximum distance, while the **low beam (dipped beam)** is angled downward to avoid blinding oncoming traffic.

### Headlight Beam Pattern (Optical Theory)
The beam cut-off is governed by the **parabolic reflector geometry**. For a parabolic surface of focal length *f*, parallel light rays from the bulb at the focal point reflect to infinity. The dipped beam uses an asymmetric cut-off at **~1% (0.6°)** below horizontal on the driver's side to prevent glare.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Work lights on tractors use **flood (wide) beam** patterns with no sharp cut-off because they are not used on public roads, only for stationary or slow field operation.

---

## 2.2 Deep Analysis — Fuses in Tractors

### Operating Principle
A fuse operates on the **I²R heating law**: the fuse element dissipates heat equal to **P = I²R**, where I is the current and R is the element resistance. When the current exceeds the rated value:

$$
P_{\text{dissipated}} = I^2 \times R_{\text{element}} > P_{\text{melt threshold}}
$$

The element temperature reaches the melting point of the alloy (typically a **zinc-tin-lead** or **silver alloy**), and the circuit opens. The **time-current characteristic** is inverse: small overloads take longer to blow, while massive short-circuit currents blow in milliseconds.

### Fuse Selection Logic for Tractor Circuits

| Circuit | Typical Fuse Rating |
|---|---|
| Headlights (each) | **10 A – 15 A** |
| Tail / Brake lights | **5 A – 10 A** |
| Horn | **10 A – 15 A** |
| Instrument cluster | **3 A – 5 A** |
| Work lights (each) | **10 A – 15 A** |
| Starter solenoid | **30 A – 50 A** (often a fusible link) |
| Alternator charge line | **60 A – 100 A** (fusible link) |
| Cab fan / AC | **15 A – 25 A** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never replace a blown fuse with a higher-rated one** or with wire/aluminium foil. This defeats the protection and may cause harness fires.

### Types of Fuses Used in Tractors
1. **Blade Fuses (ATO/ATC)** – Standard colour-coded plastic-body fuses.
2. **Mini-Blade Fuses** – Smaller version for tight fuse boxes.
3. **Maxi-Blade Fuses** – For high-current circuits (50 A–100 A).
4. **Fusible Links** – Wire-type fuses for battery-to-alternator cable.
5. **Resettable PTC Fuses (PolySwitch)** – Self-resetting polymer fuses used in some electronic modules.
6. **Cartridge Fuses** – Glass cylinder type, used in older tractors.

---

## 2.3 Deep Analysis — Wheels in Tractors

### Wheel Components
A tractor wheel consists of:
- **Rim (Disc base)** – The central mounting surface with **drop-centre** for tyre bead seating.
- **Disc (Web/Plate)** – Connects the rim to the hub.
- **Hub** – Mounted on the axle, carries bearings.
- **Bolt Circle** – Pattern of lug bolts/studs (commonly 6 or 8).
- **Wheel Weights** – Cast-iron blocks bolted to the rim to add ballast.
- **Dual Wheels (Optional)** – Extra wheels mounted on the same hub for very heavy traction work.

### Types of Tractor Wheels
1. **Pressed Steel Disc Wheels** – Most common, single-piece stamped steel.
2. **Cast Iron Wheels** – Used in two-wheel drive tractors for the rear, often with adjustable track width.
3. **Adjustable Track Wheels** – Allow the wheel track (centre-to-centre width of rear wheels) to be varied to suit row crops.
4. **Power Adjustable Wheels** – Mechanically adjustable from the driver's seat.

### Wheel Track Adjustment Importance
- For **row crops** (cotton, sugarcane, maize), the rear wheel must run between rows.
- Adjustable track wheels allow: **narrow track** (e.g., 1200 mm) for row crops, **wide track** (e.g., 1800 mm) for general farming.
- Front wheels are also adjustable for steering geometry.

---

## 2.4 Deep Analysis — Tyre Characteristics

### Tyre Construction Types

| Construction | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bias-Ply (Diagonal)** | Cord plies crisscross at 30°–45° to the bead | Strong sidewalls, cheaper, easier repair | More heat, less fuel efficient, stiffer ride |
| **Radial-Ply** | Cord plies run radially (90°) across, with a steel-belted tread band | Larger contact patch, better traction, longer life, lower rolling resistance | Higher cost, sensitive to sidewall damage, more expensive repair |

### Tread Pattern Classification (R-Systems per ASAE/IS Standards)

| Code | Tread Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Regular agricultural (45° lugs, ~70% contact area void) | General dryland farming |
| **R2** | Rice & cane (deep lugs, > 90% void) | Paddy fields, wet/muddy conditions |
| **R3** | Turf (shallow ribs, smooth) | Golf courses, lawns, orchards |
| **R4** | Industrial (multi-block lugs) | Construction, loader work |
| **F2** | Front tractor (multi-rib) | Steering axle of 2WD tractors |
| **F3** | Rib implement | Towed implement wheels |

### Tractive Performance Relationships

1. **Tractive Effort (TE)** = $\mu \times W$, where $\mu$ is the wheel-soil traction coefficient and $W$ is the dynamic weight on the wheel.
2. **Rolling Resistance (RR)** = $\rho \times W$, where $\rho$ is the rolling resistance coefficient (typically 0.08–0.12 for soft soil, 0.02–0.03 for hard soil).
3. **Net Drawbar Pull (DBP)** = TE – RR = $(\mu - \rho) \times W$.
4. **Tractive Efficiency (TEff)** = $\dfrac{\text{DBP}}{\text{TE}} = \dfrac{\mu - \rho}{\mu}$.
5. **Slip (s)** = $\dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100\%$, where $V_t$ is theoretical speed (no slip) and $V_a$ is actual speed.

### Tyre Load–Pressure Relationship
For a given tyre, **higher inflation pressure → higher load capacity**, but also **smaller contact patch → higher ground pressure → more compaction** and **lower traction in soft soil**.

$$
P_g = \frac{W}{A_c}
$$

where $P_g$ is the average ground contact pressure (kPa), $W$ is the wheel load (N), and $A_c$ is the contact patch area (m²).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Specification | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **P = I²R** | Power dissipated in fuse element | W |
| 2 | **TE = μW** | Tractive effort (max pull) | N |
| 3 | **RR = ρW** | Rolling resistance | N |
| 4 | **DBP = (μ − ρ)W** | Net drawbar pull | N |
| 5 | **TEff = (μ − ρ) / μ** | Tractive efficiency | – |
| 6 | **s = (Vₜ − Vₐ) / Vₜ × 100** | Wheel slip | % |
| 7 | **AR = H / W × 100** | Aspect ratio | % |
| 8 | **P_g = W / A_c** | Average ground contact pressure | kPa |
| 9 | **Cᵣ = 2π × Rᵣ** | Rolling circumference | m |
| 10 | **Vₐ = π × D × N / 60** | Actual forward speed | m/s |
| 11 | **D = 2Rᵣ = OD − 2H** | Effective rolling diameter | m |
| 12 | **Tyre size 12.4-28** | 12.4" width, 28" rim, bias | in |
| 13 | **Tyre size 320/85R28** | 320 mm width, 85% AR, radial | mm/% |
| 14 | **LI × kg** | Load Index → kg per tyre | kg |
| 15 | **A6 = 30 km/h** | Speed symbol A6 max speed | km/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Rule:** All absolute values and vertical separators in formulas above are rendered with `\vert` / `\mid` or by rephrasing — never with raw `|` inside table cells. This is a strict rendering requirement.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lighting design** in tractors must comply with **AIS-008** and **CMVR** for homologation. Engineers calculate photometric beam intensity, glare limits, and lens transmittance.
- **Fuse coordination** in tractor electrical design ensures the **smallest upstream fuse** blows first, isolating the fault without disabling critical systems (e.g., engine ECU).
- **Tyre selection** directly affects **fuel consumption**, **soil compaction**, **crop yield** (compacted soil reduces root growth), and **drawbar performance**. Modern precision agriculture uses **Vario/IF (Increased Flexion)** and **VF (Very high Flexion)** tyres that carry **40% more load at the same pressure** than standard radials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tractive Effort from Tyre–Soil Mechanics

### Problem Setup
A 2WD tractor has a **rear axle static weight** of **2,500 kgf**. With ballast and weight transfer, the **dynamic weight on the drive wheels** is **W = 3,200 kgf**. The tyre–soil traction coefficient on medium loam soil is **μ = 0.55**, and the rolling resistance coefficient is **ρ = 0.10**. Determine: (a) the maximum tractive effort, (b) the rolling resistance, (c) the net drawbar pull, (d) the tractive efficiency, and (e) the gross thrust the tyre can deliver in Newtons.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert weight to Newtons.**
Weight on drive wheels in Newtons:
$$
W_N = W \times g = 3{,}200 \text{ kgf} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
W_N = 31{,}392 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Compute maximum tractive effort (TE).**
$$
TE = \mu \times W = 0.55 \times 31{,}392
$$
$$
TE = 17{,}265.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Compute rolling resistance (RR).**
$$
RR = \rho \times W = 0.10 \times 31{,}392
$$
$$
RR = 3{,}139.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Compute net drawbar pull (DBP).**
$$
DBP = TE - RR = (\mu - \rho) \times W
$$
$$
DBP = (0.55 - 0.10) \times 31{,}392 = 0.45 \times 31{,}392
$$
$$
DBP = 14{,}126.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Compute tractive efficiency (TEff).**
$$
TEff = \frac{DBP}{TE} = \frac{\mu - \rho}{\mu} = \frac{0.45}{0.55}
$$
$$
TEff = 0.8182 \text{ or } 81.82\%
$$

**Step 6: Express in kgf for practical use.**
$$
TE_{\text{kgf}} = \frac{17{,}265.6}{9.81} \approx 1{,}760 \text{ kgf}
$$
$$
DBP_{\text{kgf}} = \frac{14{,}126.4}{9.81} \approx 1{,}440 \text{ kgf}
$$

### Final Answers
- **Tractive Effort = 17,265.6 N (≈ 1,760 kgf)**
- **Rolling Resistance = 3,139.2 N (≈ 320 kgf)**
- **Net Drawbar Pull = 14,126.4 N (≈ 1,440 kgf)**
- **Tractive Efficiency = 81.82 %**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tyre Slip and Actual Forward Speed

### Problem Setup
A tractor is fitted with rear tyres of size **18.4-30**. The loaded rolling radius **Rᵣ = 0.75 m**. The engine drives the rear wheels at **N = 1,200 rpm**. The measured actual forward speed is **Vₐ = 5.0 m/s**. Determine: (a) the theoretical speed (no slip), (b) the rolling circumference, (c) the slip percentage, and (d) the actual number of wheel revolutions per minute (slip-corrected).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate rolling circumference.**
$$
C_r = 2 \pi R_r = 2 \times \pi \times 0.75
$$
$$
C_r = 4.7124 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate theoretical speed (no slip) at the given rpm.**
$$
V_t = \frac{C_r \times N}{60} = \frac{4.7124 \times 1{,}200}{60}
$$
$$
V_t = 94.248 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** A real tractor never operates at 1,200 rpm *at the wheel*; the transmission reduces engine rpm. For this problem, we treat 1,200 rpm as the wheel rotational speed.

**Step 3: Calculate slip percentage.**
$$
s = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} \times 100 = \frac{94.248 - 5.0}{94.248} \times 100
$$
$$
s = \frac{89.248}{94.248} \times 100
$$
$$
s = 94.69\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A slip of 94.69 % is physically impossible for a moving tractor. This indicates our given N is unrealistically high or our interpretation of the problem is wrong. In realistic tractor operation, **optimum slip is 8–15 %** for field work, and **slip should not exceed 20 %** for efficient operation.

**Step 4: Realistic reformulation.**
Suppose a tractor travels at **Vₐ = 2.5 m/s** with a wheel rpm of **N = 20 rpm**. Then:
$$
V_t = \frac{4.7124 \times 20}{60} = 1.5708 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
s = \frac{1.5708 - 2.5}{1.5708} \times 100 = -59.1\%
$$

A **negative slip** is impossible and indicates measurement or data error. A correct field condition is more like **Vₐ = 2.0 m/s, N = 18 rpm**:
$$
V_t = \frac{4.7124 \times 18}{60} = 1.4137 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
s = \frac{1.4137 - 2.0}{1.4137} \times 100 = -41.5\%
$$

For a correct slip calculation, **Vₐ must be less than Vₜ** (because of slip, the actual distance is less than no-slip distance). Let us redo with proper data: **Vₐ = 1.35 m/s, N = 20 rpm**:
$$
V_t = \frac{4.7124 \times 20}{60} = 1.5708 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
s = \frac{1.5708 - 1.35}{1.5708} \times 100 = 14.06\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **A slip of ~14 % is within the optimal range for tractor field operation (8–15 %).**

### Final Answer (Realistic Case)
- **Rolling Circumference = 4.7124 m**
- **Theoretical Speed = 1.5708 m/s**
- **Actual Speed = 1.35 m/s**
- **Slip = 14.06 %** (within optimum range)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inflation Pressure and Contact Patch

### Problem Setup
A tractor rear tyre of size **16.9-28** has a section width of **16.9 in** and a recommended inflation pressure of **1.0 bar** for field work and **1.6 bar** for road work. The wheel load is **2,000 kgf**. Estimate the contact patch area at each pressure, assuming the contact patch is approximately rectangular with length = 0.4 × tyre outer circumference and width = section width (in m).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert section width to metres.**
$$
W_{\text{tyre}} = 16.9 \text{ in} \times 0.0254 = 0.4293 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Approximate rolling radius (rule of thumb: Rᵣ ≈ 0.5 × OD, OD ≈ 1.8 × rim diameter + 2 × section height; section height ≈ section width for bias tyres).**

Rim diameter = 28 in = 0.7112 m. Section height ≈ 0.4293 m.
$$
OD = 0.7112 + 2 \times 0.4293 = 1.5698 \text{ m}
$$
$$
R_r = \frac{OD}{2} = 0.7849 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Rolling circumference.**
$$
C_r = 2 \pi R_r = 2 \times \pi \times 0.7849 = 4.932 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Contact patch length (40% of circumference).**
$$
L_c = 0.4 \times 4.932 = 1.973 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Contact patch area.**
$$
A_c = L_c \times W_{\text{tyre}} = 1.973 \times 0.4293 = 0.8470 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Wheel load in Newtons.**
$$
W_N = 2{,}000 \times 9.81 = 19{,}620 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7: Average ground contact pressure (assuming load = pressure × area, but actual ground pressure depends on soil–tyre interaction; for hard surface, ground pressure ≈ inflation pressure).**

For a **rigid surface**:
$$
P_g \approx P_i = 1.0 \text{ bar (field)}, \; 1.6 \text{ bar (road)}
$$

For a **soft soil** (sinkage case), the contact area increases with load and decreases with inflation pressure; we keep $A_c$ as an indicator only.

**Step 8: Maximum load each pressure can carry (using P_g = W/A_c).**

At 1.0 bar (1.0 bar = 100 kPa = 100,000 N/m²):
$$
W_{1.0} = P_i \times A_c = 100{,}000 \times 0.8470 = 84{,}700 \text{ N} \approx 8{,}634 \text{ kgf}
$$

At 1.6 bar (160 kPa):
$$
W_{1.6} = 160{,}000 \times 0.8470 = 135{,}520 \text{ N} \approx 13{,}815 \text{ kgf}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual load a tyre can carry at a given pressure is taken from the **tyre load-inflation table** provided by the manufacturer, not calculated from contact patch geometry. The geometric contact patch estimate is only an **engineering approximation**.

### Final Answer
- **Contact patch area (approximate) = 0.847 m²**
- **Load capacity at 1.0 bar ≈ 8,634 kgf** (geometrical estimate)
- **Load capacity at 1.6 bar ≈ 13,815 kgf** (geometrical estimate)
- **Use manufacturer tables for actual values.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bias vs Radial Tyres

| Parameter | Bias-Ply | Radial-Ply |
|---|---|---|
| Ply angle | 30°–45° | 90° (radial cords) + steel belt |
| Sidewall flexibility | Stiffer | More flexible |
| Contact patch | Smaller, rounder | Larger, flatter |
| Rolling resistance | Higher | **Lower (15–30 %)** |
| Tractive efficiency | Lower | **Higher (5–10 % gain)** |
| Heat build-up | More | Less |
| Speed capability | Lower (≤ 30 km/h) | Higher (up to 50–65 km/h) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Soil compaction | Higher | **Lower** |
| Fuel consumption | Higher | **Lower** |
| Life | Shorter | **Longer (1.5–2 × bias)** |
| Repairability | Easier, cheaper | Difficult, requires specialist |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice: Tyre Size Decoding

### Problem
Decode the following tyre markings and state whether they are bias or radial, the rim size, and the section width in mm:

(a) **12.4-24**
(b) **16.9-30**
(c) **320/85R28**
(d) **18.4R38**
(e) **IF 650/65R38**

### Solution

**(a) 12.4-24**
- Width: 12.4 in = 12.4 × 25.4 = **314.96 mm**
- Construction: Hyphen → **Bias-ply**
- Rim: 24 in = **609.6 mm**

**(b) 16.9-30**
- Width: 16.9 in = **429.26 mm**
- Construction: **Bias-ply**
- Rim: 30 in = **762.0 mm**

**(c) 320/85R28**
- Width: **320 mm**
- Aspect ratio: **85 %** → Section height = 320 × 0.85 = **272 mm**
- Construction: **R → Radial**
- Rim: 28 in = **711.2 mm**
- Overall diameter ≈ 711.2 + 2 × 272 = **1,255.2 mm**

**(d) 18.4R38**
- Width: 18.4 in = **467.36 mm**
- Construction: **R → Radial** (inch designation)
- Rim: 38 in = **965.2 mm**

**(e) IF 650/65R38**
- **IF = Increased Flexion** (carries 20 % more load at same pressure)
- Width: **650 mm**
- Aspect ratio: **65 %** → Section height = 650 × 0.65 = **422.5 mm**
- Construction: **Radial**
- Rim: 38 in = **965.2 mm**

> [!NOTE]
> **Metric Low-Profile Tyres:** Modern high-power tractors use metric low-profile tyres (e.g., 650/65R38) instead of inch-designation tyres (e.g., 18.4R38). The lower aspect ratio (65 % vs ~85 %) gives a wider, flatter contact patch and better traction.

## 4.1 Tractor Electrical Lighting System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery 12V DC] --> B[Ignition Switch and Main Relay]
    B --> C1[Fuse Box 1 Lights and Horn]
    B --> C2[Fuse Box 2 Engine and ECU]
    B --> C3[Fuse Box 3 Cab and Accessories]
    C1 --> D1[Headlight Relay]
    C1 --> D2[Tail Light Relay]
    C1 --> D3[Work Light Relay]
    C1 --> D4[Horn Relay]
    D1 --> E1[LH Headlamp High and Low]
    D1 --> E2[RH Headlamp High and Low]
    D2 --> F1[LH Tail and Brake Lamp]
    D2 --> F2[RH Tail and Brake Lamp]
    D2 --> F3[Number Plate Lamp]
    D3 --> G1[Front Work Light LH]
    D3 --> G2[Front Work Light RH]
    D3 --> G3[Rear Work Light LH]
    D3 --> G4[Rear Work Light RH]
    D4 --> H1[Electric Horn]
    C2 --> I1[Engine ECU]
    C2 --> I2[Alternator Regulator]
    C3 --> J1[Instrument Cluster Lamps]
    C3 --> J2[Cab Roof Beacon Lamp]
    C3 --> J3[AC and Blower]
    C3 --> J4[Radio and Auxiliary]
    I2 --> K1[Alternator Charging Loop Back to Battery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading Guide:** Each fuse box acts as a **current-distribution and protection hub**. A fault in branch G3 (rear work light) only blows the **G3 fuse**, leaving all other branches (headlights, horn, ECU) operational. This modular protection is the core engineering principle of tractor electrical design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```


> [!NOTE]
> The **low-beam cut-off** is a critical safety feature. It is engineered so that the left side of the beam (driver's side in right-hand-drive vehicles) is angled **0.5° to 0.6° below horizontal**, so it does not blind oncoming drivers, while the right side is angled slightly higher to illuminate road signs.
