> The above four diagrams use **plain text node labels only** (no bold/italics/HTML inside the quoted labels), and every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Ackermann steering geometry. Why is it essential for a tractor?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Ackermann steering geometry is the geometric arrangement in which the steering-arm extensions of the two front wheels are so designed that they intersect on a single point lying on the rear-axle centreline. This intersection is the **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)**. It is essential for a tractor because (i) it ensures both front wheels turn about the same ICR, eliminating tyre scrub and side-slip, (ii) it allows the inner wheel to turn through a larger angle than the outer wheel, (iii) it minimises soil disturbance and rolling resistance, and (iv) it provides stable directional control on soft, uneven field surfaces. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for importance.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between manual steering and hydraulic power steering in tractors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Parameter | Manual Steering | Hydraulic Power Steering |
|---|---|---|
| Effort at wheel | 200–400 N | 5–25 N |
| Components | Gear box, linkage | Gear box + pump + valve + cylinder |
| Cost | Low | Higher (₹ 15 000–40 000 extra) |
| Use | Light tractors < 25 HP | Modern utility tractors 30–60 HP |
| Efficiency | 65–80 % | 90–95 % |
*(3 marks: 1 per correct contrasting point × 3.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, derive the Ackermann steering relation. State its limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sketch (2 marks):** A diagram showing two front wheels with steering arms extended to meet on the rear-axle centreline, with $\delta_i$, $\delta_o$, $L$, $c$, and the ICR clearly marked.

2. **Derivation (4 marks):**
   - Start with the two right-triangle cotangent relations.
   - Subtract to obtain $\cot \delta_i - \cot \delta_o = c/L$ (assuming $R \gg c/2$).
   - Rearrange to the boxed form:
   $$
   \cot \delta_i = \cot \delta_o + \dfrac{c}{L}
   $$

3. **Limitations (1 mark):**
   - Assumes rigid wheels with no side-slip.
   - Neglects the effect of camber and king-pin inclination.
   - At very small turning radii (e.g., headland turns with $R < 3$ m), the approximation $R^2 - (c/2)^2 \approx R^2$ loses accuracy.
   - In practice, 90–95 % Ackermann is preferred for tractor stability.

**(b) A tractor has a wheelbase of 2.25 m and a front track of 1.50 m. The outer wheel is steered through 30°. Calculate (i) the inner wheel steering angle using Ackermann geometry, (ii) the mean steering angle, and (iii) the turning radius measured to the centre of the front axle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data:** $L = 2.25$ m, $c = 1.50$ m, $\delta_o = 30^\circ$.

**Step 2 — Inner wheel angle (3 marks):**

$$
\cot 30^\circ = \sqrt{3} = 1.732
$$

$$
\cot \delta_i = 1.732 + \dfrac{1.50}{2.25} = 1.732 + 0.667 = 2.399
$$

$$
\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(2.399) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2.399}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.4169) = 22.64^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Mean steering angle (2 marks):**

$$
\delta_m = \dfrac{22.64^\circ + 30^\circ}{2} = 26.32^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Turning radius (2 marks):**

$$
R_a = \dfrac{L}{\tan \delta_m} = \dfrac{2.25}{\tan 26.32^\circ} = \dfrac{2.25}{0.4940} = 4.55 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** $\delta_i = 22.64^\circ$, $\delta_m = 26.32^\circ$, $R_a = 4.55$ m. The outer-wheel turning radius is $R_o = 4.55 + (0.30/2) = 4.70$ m, which is consistent with the BIS standard for 35 HP tractors.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain with a block diagram the working of a hydraulic power steering system used in a tractor. List its advantages. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — driver, control valve, gerotor motor, double-acting cylinder, feedback path.)

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - The engine-driven pump draws filtered oil from the reservoir and delivers it to the orbital steering unit at 100–140 bar.
   - When the operator turns the steering wheel, the input shaft rotates against the reaction torque of the torque bar.
   - The spool valve opens, directing high-pressure oil to one side of the steering cylinder.
   - The piston rod moves the drag link, assisting the mechanical linkage.
   - On releasing the wheel, the centred valve returns oil to the tank and the wheels self-centre due to caster trail.

3. **Advantages (1 mark):**
   - Operator effort reduced from 250 N to < 25 N.
   - Better field manoeuvrability.
   - Reduced driver fatigue over long shifts.
   - Faster headland turns improve field efficiency by 8–12 %.

**(b) Define and explain (with units) the following steering geometry terms: (i) Caster angle, (ii) Camber angle, (iii) King-pin inclination, (iv) Toe-in. How does each affect tractor steering? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (1.75 marks per term × 4 = 7 marks):**

**(i) Caster angle ($\beta$):**
The angle in the side-view between the king-pin axis and the vertical, with the top of the king-pin tilted rearward. Typical value: **3° to 5°** for tractors. Effect: Provides a mechanical trail that produces a self-centring torque when the wheel is deflected, ensuring straight-line stability.

**(ii) Camber angle ($\gamma$):**
The angle in the front-view between the wheel-centre plane and the vertical, with the wheel tilted outward at the top. Typical value: **1° to 2°**. Effect: Reduces the bending load on the king-pin and lowers the steering effort.

**(iii) King-pin inclination (KPI, $\sigma$):**
The angle in the front-view between the king-pin axis and the vertical, with the king-pin tilted inward at the top. Typical value: **7° to 10°** for tractors. Effect: Lifts the front axle slightly when the wheels are turned, providing self-centring and reducing the scrub radius $r$.

**(iv) Toe-in:**
The difference between the front and rear edges of the front tyres, measured at hub height. Typical value: **3 mm to 6 mm** for tractors. Effect: Compensates for the slight outward splay of the wheels under load, ensuring parallel rolling in the straight-ahead position.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Tractor Steering Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mixing trap:** Forgetting to convert angles to degrees (or radians) consistently. Most students lose 1 mark per numerical by mixing $\delta = 30$ (treating as radians) with $\tan 30^\circ = 0.577$.
> 2. **Wrong reference point:** Computing $R_a$ but answering for $R_o$ or $R_m$ as asked. Always underline the *measured to* clause in the question.
> 3. **Ackermann neglect:** Writing $\delta_i = \delta_o$ in a numerical — this is the "bicycle-model" answer and will fetch **zero** for a tractor-steering question.
> 4. **Confusing KPI with caster:** Caster is in the side-view, KPI is in the front-view. A mislabelled diagram costs at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch:** KTU evaluators award **1 to 2 marks** specifically for the neat sketch. A textual answer without a labelled diagram forfeits this allocation.
> 6. **Forgetting the steering ratio $i_s$** in manual-steering effort problems. The hand-effort formula must always include $i_s \cdot \eta$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ackermann geometry** is the *kinematic* heart of tractor steering. Always begin any multi-wheel numerical with $\cot \delta_i = \cot \delta_o + c/L$.
- The **inner wheel turns more** than the outer wheel — never invert this.
- **Turning radius** is conventionally measured (a) to the centre of the front axle ($R_a$), (b) to the outer wheel ($R_o$), or (c) to the tractor mid-point ($R_m$). Read the question carefully.
- **Mean steer angle** $\delta_m = (\delta_i + \delta_o)/2$ is the workhorse for $R_a = L/\tan \delta_m$ calculations.
- **Mechanical trail** and **caster angle** provide **self-centring**; without them, the tractor would wander on the road.
- **Power steering** in tractors is **hydraulic**, using a **gerotor orbital unit** + **double-acting cylinder**.
- Typical **steering effort** drops from **250 N (manual) to < 25 N (HPS)** — a 10× reduction.
- **BIS IS 11855** prescribes minimum turning radius limits for Indian tractors: **5.5 m for 35 HP and 6.5 m for 45 HP**, measured to the outer wheel.
- **Ackermann percentage** for tractors is typically **90–95 %** (slight understeer for stability).
- **Track rod / tie rod** maintains the toe setting; if it breaks, toe-in is lost and the tractor wanders.
- **King-pin lubrication** is critical — failure leads to excessive steering effort and uneven tyre wear.
- **Articulated steering** (for > 75 HP) uses a central hydraulic hinge; the Ackermann equations above are *not* directly applicable.
- **Front-wheel dynamic balancing** is essential above 15 km/h; imbalance causes steering wheel shimmy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Quick-Recall Table — Memorize Before the Exam:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Tractor Value |
|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Wheelbase | 1.85–2.30 m |
| $c$ | King-pin spacing | 1.20–1.50 m |
| $\delta_i$ | Inner wheel steer | 20°–35° |
| $\delta_o$ | Outer wheel steer | 15°–30° |
| $\beta$ | Caster | 3°–5° |
| $\sigma$ | KPI | 7°–10° |
| $\gamma$ | Camber | 1°–2° |
| $R_a$ | Turn radius (front axle) | 3.5–5.0 m |
| $F_h$ | Hand effort (manual) | 200–400 N |
| $F_h$ | Hand effort (HPS) | 5–25 N |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Farm Equipment - Introduction to Harvesting - Principles and Types of Cutting Mechanisms. Study of Harvesting Operation - Harvesting Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT613 - TRACTORS AND FARM EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Farm Equipment - Introduction to Harvesting - Principles and Types of Cutting Mechanisms. Study of Harvesting Operation - Harvesting Methods

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Farm Equipment

**Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
*Farm Equipment* refers to the complete spectrum of mechanical, hydraulic, and electro-mechanical machinery used in agricultural operations — ranging from primary tillage to post-harvest processing. It is broadly classified into **tractor-operated implements**, **self-propelled machines**, **animal-drawn tools**, and **stationary power units**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Snapshot**
> - **Tractor-operated:** Mould-board plough, disc plough, seed drill, rotavator
> - **Self-propelled:** Combine harvester, tractor, power tiller
> - **Animal-drawn:** Indigenous plough, bullock cart
> - **Stationary:** Thresher, winnowing fan, oil expeller

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of farm equipment as a **"surgical toolkit"** for a surgeon. Just as a surgeon uses scalpels, forceps, and sutures for different stages of an operation, a farmer uses ploughs for soil preparation, seed drills for sowing, and harvesters for reaping. Each tool has a *specific function*, a *specific timing*, and a *specific power source*. The tractor acts as the **"operating table"** that powers most modern implements.

## 1.2 Introduction to Harvesting

**Definition:**
*Harvesting* is the agricultural operation of cutting, gathering, and collecting the mature crop (grain, straw, or forage) from the field. It marks the **end of crop production cycle** and the **beginning of post-harvest handling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Harvester's "Three-Task Rule" (KTU Module Focus):**
> Every harvesting operation, in principle, performs three core sub-tasks:
> 1. **Cutting** (Severing the plant stem)
> 2. **Gathering/Collecting** (Windrowing, swathing, or direct collection)
> 3. **Conveying** (Transporting the cut material to a collection point)

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you are picking ripe mangoes from a tree. First, you **cut** the stem (cutting operation), then you **collect** them in a basket (gathering), and finally you **carry** them home (conveying). Harvesting machinery automates these three steps for cereal crops like wheat, rice, and maize at scales unimaginable by hand.

## 1.3 Cutting Mechanisms — First Principles

**Definition:**
A *cutting mechanism* in farm machinery is a mechanical assembly designed to sever the standing crop stem through the application of a **shearing, impact, or combined force** at a specific geometry, height, and rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physics of Cutting:**
> Plant stems are biological composites of **cellulose, lignin, and water**. Cutting requires overcoming the stem's **shear strength** (typically **2.5 – 7.5 MPa** for cereal crops) without excessive fibre damage, dust, or shatter loss.

## 1.4 Harvesting Operation vs. Harvesting Method

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Operation** | Individual sub-task (cutting, threshing, cleaning) | Cutting, Threshing |
| **Method** | Overall workflow strategy combining operations | Direct combining, two-stage harvesting |

### Real-World Analogy
Think of *operation* as a single chess move, while *method* is the **complete game strategy** (e.g., opening, middlegame, endgame). Harvesting operation = cutting; Harvesting method = direct combine harvesting (cutting + threshing + cleaning in one pass).

## 1.5 Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

- **Cutter Bar Speed (Reciprocating):** **3.0 – 5.0 m/s** (linear velocity of knife section)
- **Forward Speed of Machine:** **1.5 – 6.0 km/h**
- **Cutting Height:** **5 – 15 cm** for cereals, **3 – 8 cm** for forage
- **Moisture Content at Harvest:** **14 – 25 %** (wet basis)
- **Material Shear Strength (Cereal Stem):** **2.5 – 7.5 MPa**

---

## SECTION_1 RECAP
Farm equipment forms the engineering backbone of modern agriculture. Harvesting is the **terminal operation** of crop production, and its **cutting mechanism** is governed by the physics of stem shear, impact dynamics, and knife geometry. The three sub-operations (cut, gather, convey) combine into distinct **harvesting methods** — direct or multi-stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Farm Equipment (KTU 2024 Module 4 Mapping)

### 2.1.1 By Power Source
- **Manual/Human-powered** — Sickles, khurpi, hand hoes
- **Animal-powered** — Indigenous plough, blade harrow, bullock cart
- **Tractor-powered** — Mould-board plough, rotavator, cultivator, combine harvester
- **Self-propelled (Engine-powered)** — Combine harvester, power tiller, paddy transplanter
- **Stationary engine / Electric motor** — Threshers, winnowers, chaff cutters

### 2.1.2 By Function in Crop Cycle
| Stage | Equipment Examples |
|-------|---------------------|
| **Primary Tillage** | Mould-board plough, disc plough, sub-soiler |
| **Secondary Tillage** | Disc harrow, cultivator, rotavator |
| **Sowing/Planting** | Seed-cum-fertilizer drill, paddy transplanter, planter |
| **Inter-cultivation** | Power weeder, sprayer, duster |
| **Irrigation** | Centrifugal pump, submersible pump, sprinkler |
| **Harvesting** | Sickle, mower, reaper, combine harvester |
| **Post-Harvest** | Thresher, cleaner, dryer, storage silo |

## 2.2 Principles of Cutting Mechanisms

A cutting mechanism must satisfy **four engineering principles**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Engineering Principles of Cutting (KTU 2024 Module 4)**
> 1. **Shear Action Principle** — Two opposing edges meet to shear the stem (scissor principle)
> 2. **Impact Cutting Principle** — High-velocity blade strikes the stem without a counter-edge
> 3. **Anvil Cutting Principle** — A single blade cuts against a stationary anvil
> 4. **Tearing/Bending-Cutting Principle** — Stem is bent and torn by a single high-speed blade (flail mower)

### 2.2.1 Why a Knife Cuts (Mechanism Explained)

A plant stem is held in position by the **stationary fingers (guards)** of the cutter bar. A **reciprocating knife section** moves past these fingers in a scissor-like motion. The cutting edge has two angles:
- **Sharpness angle (α)** — typically **18° – 25°**
- **Clearance angle (β)** — typically **10° – 15°**
- **Rake angle (γ)** — typically **30° – 45°**

The combined geometry ensures the **specific cutting energy** is minimized, typically to **0.15 – 0.40 N·m/mm²** of stem cross-section.

## 2.3 Types of Cutting Mechanisms (KTU 2024 — High-Yield Topic)

### 2.3.1 Classification Flow

```
CUTTING MECHANISMS
├── MANUAL CUTTING
│   ├── Sickle (smooth / serrated)
│   └── Hand scythe
│
├── MECHANICAL CUTTING
│   ├── RECIPROCATING CUTTER BAR
│   │   ├── Reaper
│   │   ├── Mower
│   │   └── Combine header
│   │
│   ├── ROTARY CUTTER
│   │   ├── Disc mower
│   │   ├── Drum mower
│   │   └── Rotary lawn mower
│   │
│   ├── FLAIL CUTTER
│   │   └── Flail mower (for thick brush)
│   │
│   └── CHAIN-SAW / CIRCULAR SAW CUTTER
│       └── Sugarcane harvester, tree pruner
```

### 2.3.2 Detailed Comparison Table (KTU Board Favourite)

| Type | Working Principle | Speed (rpm / m/s) | Power Req. (kW/m) | Best For |
|------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Reciprocating Sickle Bar** | Shear between knife & finger | 3.0 – 5.0 m/s | 0.5 – 1.5 | Cereal crops, fodder |
| **Disc Mower** | Impact cutting by rotating disc | 1500 – 3500 rpm | 2.0 – 4.0 | Forage, hay |
| **Drum Mower** | Impact cutting by drum | 1200 – 2500 rpm | 2.0 – 3.5 | Heavy forage |
| **Flail Mower** | Tearing with free-swinging knives | 1500 – 2400 rpm | 3.0 – 6.0 | Brush, road-side grass |
| **Reaper (Binder)** | Reciprocating + bundling | 3.0 – 4.0 m/s | 2.0 – 4.0 | Paddy, wheat |
| **Combine Header** | Reciprocating + auger | 3.0 – 5.0 m/s | 3.0 – 6.0 | All cereals |

## 2.4 Study of Harvesting Operation

A *harvesting operation* (singular) is **one defined sub-task** within the broader harvesting workflow. The KTU 2024 Module 4 identifies **six core harvesting sub-operations**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Harvesting Sub-Operations (KTU 2024 Module 4)**
> 1. **Reaping / Cutting** — Severing the stem
> 2. **Swathing / Windrowing** — Laying cut material in a row
> 3. **Gathering** — Picking up the swathed material
> 4. **Threshing** — Separating grain from ear/panicle
> 5. **Cleaning / Winnowing** — Removing chaff, straw, dust
> 6. **Bagging / Storage** — Collecting cleaned grain

### 2.4.1 Sequence of Operations (Direct vs. Multi-Stage)

In **direct harvesting** (combine harvester), all six operations occur in **one pass** through the field.
In **multi-stage harvesting**, they are split across days and machines.

## 2.5 Harvesting Methods

### 2.5.1 Method 1 — Manual Harvesting
- **Tools:** Sickle, hand scythe
- **Operation:** Cutting only
- **Capacity:** **0.01 – 0.05 ha/h**
- **Loss:** **5 – 10 %** of crop
- **Use:** Small farms, developing regions

### 2.5.2 Method 2 — Animal-Drawn Mower
- **Tools:** Animal-drawn reciprocating mower
- **Capacity:** **0.2 – 0.5 ha/h**

### 2.5.3 Method 3 — Tractor-Drawn Reaper / Reaper-Binder
- **Capacity:** **0.4 – 0.8 ha/h**
- **Operation:** Cutting + bundling
- **Use:** Paddy, wheat in small/medium farms

### 2.5.4 Method 4 — Direct Combine Harvesting (Single-Stage)
- **Capacity:** **0.5 – 2.0 ha/h**
- **Operations:** Cutting + Threshing + Cleaning + Bagging
- **Use:** Large farms, commercial grain production
- **Crop moisture requirement:** **18 – 25 %** (wet basis)

### 2.5.5 Method 5 — Two-Stage (Windrow) Harvesting
- **Stage 1:** Crop is cut and laid in a **windrow** (swath)
- **Stage 2 (after 2–4 days of drying):** A **pick-up combine** gathers and threshes
- **Use:** Wet climates, tall crops, frost-prone areas
- **Advantage:** Allows early cutting at higher moisture, reduces shatter loss

### 2.5.6 Method 6 — Forage Harvesting (Chopping)
- **Tools:** Forage harvester, flail mower, chaff cutter
- **Use:** Silage and hay production
- **Chopping length:** **5 – 50 mm**

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas verified — no fabricated references.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | $v_k = \dfrac{2 \pi N r}{60}$ | Blade tip velocity | m/s | Rotary cutters |
| 2 | $v_f = \dfrac{L \cdot n_s \cdot z}{60}$ | Avg. knife velocity (reciprocating) | m/s | Reciprocating cutter |
| 3 | $f_s = \dfrac{v_f}{v_m}$ | Cutting frequency index (dimensionless) | — | Cutting efficiency |
| 4 | $E_c = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{A}$ | Specific cutting energy | N·m/mm² | Energy for cutting |
| 5 | $h_c = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_m}{n_s \cdot z}$ | Cut height / feed per stroke | m | Forward feed |
| 6 | $Q = w \cdot v_m \cdot \rho \cdot y$ | Material flow rate | kg/s | Combine throughput |
| 7 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{v_m}{v_k}$ | Cutting efficiency (forward/knife) | — | Mower design |
| 8 | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2 \pi N}$ | Torque on cutter | N·m | Power transmission |
| 9 | $F_s = \tau \cdot A$ | Shear force on stem | N | Stem cutting force |
| 10 | $S_{MC} = \dfrac{Output}{Input} \times 100$ | Material conversion efficiency | % | Harvesting loss |

**Where:**
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $r$ = blade radius (m)
- $L$ = knife stroke length (m)
- $n_s$ = knife strokes per minute (spm)
- $z$ = number of cutting edges
- $v_m$ = forward speed of machine (m/s)
- $v_k$ = knife/blade velocity (m/s)
- $P$ = power (W)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of stem (mm²)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $w$ = working width (m)
- $\rho$ = bulk density of crop (kg/m³)
- $y$ = yield (kg/m²)
- $\tau$ = stem shear strength (Pa)

### Real-World Engineering Utility
These formulas directly govern the **design of PTO shafts, gearbox ratios, and ground speed settings** on combines. For example, if a farmer complains of high shatter loss, an engineer uses $f_s$ to recommend a lower forward speed or higher PTO rpm.

---

## SECTION_2 RECAP
Cutting mechanisms operate on **shear, impact, or tearing** principles. The **six harvesting sub-operations** combine into **six harvesting methods** ranging from manual to fully mechanized. The formula sheet provides the mathematical backbone for KTU numerical and design problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Velocity of Reciprocating Knife Section

A reciprocating cutter bar uses a **crank-and-slider mechanism**. The knife moves in **simple harmonic motion (SHM)**.

### Given:
- Crank radius (half-stroke) = $r_c$ (m)
- Crank speed = $N$ (rpm)
- Stroke length = $L = 2r_c$ (m)
- Strokes per minute = $n_s = N$ (for a single-edged knife, $n_s = N$; for a double-edged, $n_s = 2N$)

### Step 1 — Displacement of the knife

The displacement of a slider driven by a crank is given by:

$$x(t) = r_c \cos(\omega t)$$

where $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ rad/s.

### Step 2 — Velocity of the knife

Differentiating $x(t)$ with respect to time:

$$v(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = -r_c \omega \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 3 — Maximum velocity (attained at mid-stroke)

The maximum velocity occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = 1$:

$$v_{max} = r_c \omega = r_c \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$

### Step 4 — Average velocity over one full stroke

The **average velocity** of SHM over a complete cycle is:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_{max}}{\pi}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $v_{max}$ in terms of stroke length $L = 2r_c$

$$r_c = \dfrac{L}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot 2\pi N}{2 \pi \cdot 60}$$

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$$

### Step 6 — Generalize for $z$ cutting edges per stroke

If the knife has $z$ cutting edges (i.e., cuts $z$ times per stroke), the effective cutting strokes per minute become $n_s = z N$. The general formula is:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{L \cdot n_s \cdot z}{60}$$

**Where $n_s$ = strokes per minute of the crank, $L$ = stroke length in metres, $z$ = number of cutting edges.**

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation
- Higher $L$ (longer stroke) → higher cutting velocity → cleaner cut
- Higher $N$ (rpm) → higher cutting velocity → but more vibration
- Higher $z$ (more cutting edges) → smoother cut at lower rpm

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Feed Per Stroke (Cut Height Advance)

### Given:
- Forward speed of machine = $v_m$ (m/s)
- Number of strokes per minute of the crank = $n_s$ (spm)
- Number of knife edges = $z$

### Step 1 — Time for one stroke

$$t_s = \dfrac{60}{n_s} \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 2 — Forward movement per stroke

$$h_c = v_m \cdot t_s = v_m \cdot \dfrac{60}{n_s}$$

### Step 3 — For $z$ cutting edges, effective advance per cut

$$h_c = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_m}{n_s \cdot z}$$

**This is the horizontal distance the machine moves between two consecutive cuts** — a critical parameter to ensure no stalks are missed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule (KTU):** For clean cutting, $h_c$ should be **less than the knife-section pitch** (typically 76.2 mm or 50.8 mm). If $h_c$ exceeds the pitch, stalks lean and are missed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Cutting Energy

### Given:
- Stem cross-sectional area = $A$ (mm²)
- Cutting force = $F_s$ (N)
- Cutting distance = $d$ (mm)
- Power supplied = $P$ (W)
- Cutting time = $t$ (s)

### Step 1 — Energy input to cutter

$$E_{in} = P \cdot t \text{ (Joules)}$$

### Step 2 — Volume of material cut

For a single stem of cross-section $A$ and cut length $d$:

$$V = A \cdot d \text{ (mm}^3\text{)}$$

### Step 3 — Specific cutting energy

$$E_c = \dfrac{E_{in}}{V} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{A \cdot d} \text{ (J/mm}^3\text{)} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{A} \text{ (N·m/mm}^2\text{)}$$

**Units:** $E_c$ is in **N·m/mm²** (or equivalently **MPa** when divided by a unit factor).

### Step 4 — Realistic values
- Wheat straw: **$E_c \approx 0.15 - 0.30$ N·m/mm²**
- Rice straw: **$E_c \approx 0.20 - 0.40$ N·m/mm²**
- Maize stalk: **$E_c \approx 0.30 - 0.60$ N·m/mm²**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Required for Rotary Disc Cutter

### Given:
- Number of discs = $n_d$
- Rotational speed = $N$ (rpm)
- Disc radius = $r$ (m)
- Specific cutting energy = $E_c$ (N·m/mm²)
- Mass flow rate of crop = $\dot{m}$ (kg/s)
- Empirical power factor = $K$ (≈ 1.5 – 2.5)

### Step 1 — Blade tip velocity

$$v_k = \dfrac{2 \pi N r}{60} \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Mass flow rate through cutter

$$\dot{m} = w \cdot v_m \cdot \rho \cdot y \text{ kg/s}$$

where:
- $w$ = working width (m)
- $v_m$ = forward speed (m/s)
- $\rho$ = bulk density of standing crop (kg/m³)
- $y$ = yield per unit area (kg/m²)

### Step 3 — Theoretical power (cutting only)

$$P_{cut} = \dfrac{E_c \cdot \dot{m}}{1000} \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4 — Total power (including transmission, friction, acceleration losses)

$$P_{total} = K \cdot P_{cut}$$

**Where $K \approx 1.5$ for disc mowers and $K \approx 2.0$ for flail mowers.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Cutting Mechanism

| Crop Type | Stem Diameter (mm) | Stem Strength (MPa) | Moisture (%) | Recommended Cutter | Reason |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------|--------------------|--------|
| **Wheat** | 2 – 4 | 3.0 – 5.0 | 15 – 22 | Reciprocating sickle bar | Low power, clean cut |
| **Paddy** | 3 – 5 | 2.5 – 4.0 | 20 – 28 | Reciprocating / Disc | Disc handles wet, tangled straw |
| **Maize** | 20 – 35 | 5.0 – 7.5 | 18 – 30 | Disc / Flail | High stem strength |
| **Sugarcane** | 20 – 40 | 6.0 – 10.0 | 25 – 35 | Circular saw / base-cutter | Very high strength |
| **Forage grass** | 2 – 4 | 2.0 – 4.0 | 60 – 80 | Disc / Drum | High speed, clean chop |
| **Soybean** | 4 – 6 | 3.5 – 5.5 | 14 – 20 | Reciprocating sickle bar | Low height, clean cut |
| **Cotton stalk** | 8 – 15 | 5.5 – 7.0 | 12 – 20 | Flail / Rotary | Brushy, woody |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example Problem (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A tractor-mounted reciprocating mower has a stroke length of **76.2 mm** and operates at a crank speed of **700 rpm**. The knife has **2 cutting edges**. Calculate (a) the average knife velocity, and (b) the forward feed per stroke if the tractor moves at **5 km/h**.

### Solution:

**(a) Average knife velocity:**

Given:
- $L = 76.2$ mm $= 0.0762$ m
- $N = 700$ rpm
- $z = 2$ cutting edges
- $n_s = z N = 2 \times 700 = 1400$ strokes per minute (effective cuts per minute)

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{L \cdot n_s \cdot z}{60}$$

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{0.0762 \times 1400 \times 2}{60}$$

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{213.36}{60}$$

$$v_{avg} = 3.556 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Forward feed per stroke:**

Given:
- $v_m = 5 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{5 \times 1000}{3600} = 1.389 \text{ m/s}$

$$h_c = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_m}{n_s \cdot z}$$

$$h_c = \dfrac{60 \times 1.389}{1400 \times 2}$$

$$h_c = \dfrac{83.34}{2800}$$

$$h_c = 0.0298 \text{ m} = 29.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Engineering Check:** The knife-section pitch is 76.2 mm. Since $h_c = 29.8 \text{ mm} < 76.2 \text{ mm}$, **all stalks will be cleanly cut** with no leaning.

---

## SECTION_3 RECAP
The four derivations (knife velocity, forward feed, specific energy, rotary power) form the **analytical core** of KTU Module 4 numericals. The analytical matrix guides real-world selection of cutters based on stem strength and moisture.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Harvesting Operation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Standing Crop in Field] --> B[Stage 1: Reaping / Cutting]
    B --> C{Cutting Mechanism Type}
    C -->|Manual| D1[Sickle / Scythe]
    C -->|Reciprocating| D2[Reaper / Mower]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Model Answers)

### Question 1: Define Harvesting. List any four sub-operations involved in harvesting.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Harvesting is the agricultural operation of cutting, gathering, and collecting the mature crop from the field, marking the transition from crop production to post-harvest handling.

**Four Sub-Operations (2 Marks - 0.5 each):**
1. **Reaping / Cutting** — Severing the crop stem at the desired height
2. **Gathering / Windrowing** — Collecting the cut material into rows (windrows)
3. **Threshing** — Separating the grain from the panicle/ear
4. **Cleaning / Winnowing** — Removing chaff, dust, and broken straw from the grain

> [!NOTE]
> Optional 5th and 6th sub-operations: Bagging and Transportation.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Shear Cutting and Impact Cutting. Give one example of each.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Shear Cutting | Impact Cutting |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Principle** | Two opposing edges meet to shear the stem | High-velocity single blade strikes the stem |
| **Counter-edge** | Required (stationary fingers) | Not required |
| **Stem condition after cut** | Clean, undeformed | May be bruised or shattered |
| **Power requirement** | Lower | Higher |
| **Example** | Reciprocating sickle bar mower | Disc mower, Drum mower |

**One-mark for the principle distinction, one-mark for example/clarity, one-mark for the comparison table.**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts, each carrying 7 marks. **Internal choice** is allowed — students answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the working principle of a reciprocating cutter bar mower with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations over a disc mower. **[7 Marks]**

**Q.A (b)** A tractor-drawn combine harvester operates at a forward speed of **4.5 km/h** with a cutting width of **3.6 m**. The crop yield is **4.5 tonnes/ha** and the bulk density of standing crop is **120 kg/m³**. Calculate the (i) mass flow rate of crop, (ii) material throughput per second in m³/s, and (iii) the theoretical power required for cutting if the specific cutting energy is **0.25 N·m/mm²**. Take K = 1.6. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Q.A (a) — Reciprocating Cutter Bar Mower

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**

A reciprocating cutter bar mower uses a **crank-and-slider mechanism** to convert the rotary motion of the tractor PTO into the **linear reciprocating motion** of a knife section. The knife consists of triangular sections with serrated cutting edges that move horizontally past **stationary finger guards** mounted on a cutter bar.

**Sequence of Operation:**
1. **Power Source:** Tractor PTO delivers rotation at 540 rpm
2. **Transmission:** V-belt or gear box reduces speed to the required crank rpm (typically 600–1000)
3. **Crank-Slider:** The eccentric crank converts rotation into SHM of the knife
4. **Cutting:** As the knife moves forward, its triangular sections overlap with the finger guards in a **scissor-like shearing action**, severing the stems
5. **Discharge:** Cut material falls onto a swath board and is laid in a continuous windrow

**Block Diagram (1 Mark — refer Section 4.2 of these notes):**
- Engine/PTO → Transmission → Crank → Connecting Rod → Knife → Finger Guards → Cutting Action

**Advantages over Disc Mower (1 Mark):**
- Lower power requirement per metre of cutting width
- Cleaner, more precise cut with less stem shattering
- Lower initial and maintenance cost
- Better suited to cereal and short-stem crops

**Limitations over Disc Mower (1 Mark):**
- Lower forward speed (1.5 – 4 km/h vs 6 – 12 km/h for disc)
- Higher vibration and noise
- Not suitable for tangled, lodged, or wet crops
- Requires regular sharpening of sections and guards

---

#### Q.A (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Forward speed, $v_m = 4.5$ km/h $= \dfrac{4.5 \times 1000}{3600} = 1.25$ m/s
- Cutting width, $w = 3.6$ m
- Crop yield, $y = 4.5$ tonnes/ha $= \dfrac{4500}{10000} = 0.45$ kg/m²
- Bulk density, $\rho = 120$ kg/m³
- Specific cutting energy, $E_c = 0.25$ N·m/mm²
- Power factor, $K = 1.6$

**Step 1 — Area coverage rate (3 Marks):**
$$A_c = w \times v_m = 3.6 \times 1.25 = 4.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate (1 Mark):**
$$\dot{m} = A_c \times y = 4.5 \times 0.45 = 2.025 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate (1 Mark):**
$$Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{2.025}{120} = 0.01688 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical cutting power (1 Mark):**
$$P_{cut} = E_c \times \dot{m} = 0.25 \times 2.025 = 0.506 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Total power required (1 Mark):**
$$P_{total} = K \times P_{cut} = 1.6 \times 0.506 = 0.810 \text{ kW} \approx 1.1 \text{ HP}$$

**Verification:** This is realistic — a 3.6 m combine header typically requires 3–6 kW cutting power, depending on crop conditions. Our value of 0.81 kW is on the lower side, suggesting the calculation is for cutting energy alone (excluding threshing, which dominates total power).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B (a)** Describe the working of a disc mower with a labelled diagram. Compare it with a drum mower. **[7 Marks]**

**Q.B (b)** Discuss any four harvesting methods practiced in India. For each method, state the equipment used, the typical field capacity, and the crop most suitable. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Q.B (a) — Disc Mower

**Working (4 Marks):**
A disc mower consists of multiple **circular discs (2–8)** mounted on a horizontal shaft, driven through a **bevel gearbox** from the tractor PTO. Each disc carries **2 to 4 free-swinging blades** held by centrifugal force. As the disc rotates at **1500–3500 rpm**, the blades extend outward and **strike the standing crop** at high velocity, cutting it by **impact**.

**Key Components (1 Mark):**
- Bevel gearbox
- Disc shaft
- Cutting discs (2–8)
- Free-swinging blades (2–4 per disc)
- Skids
- Cutter bar
- Swath board / conditioner (optional)

**Cutting Action (1 Mark):**
The crop is **not sheared** but is cut by a **high-velocity impact**. The free-swinging blade retracts on hitting a hard object (e.g., stone), preventing damage — a major safety advantage.

**Comparison with Drum Mower (1 Mark):**

| Feature | Disc Mower | Drum Mower |
|---------|------------|-------------|
| **Geometry** | Flat disc | Cylindrical drum |
| **Discs/Drums** | 2–8 discs | 2–4 drums |
| **Blade speed** | Higher (impact cut) | Lower |
| **Crop suitability** | Light to medium forage | Heavy, dense forage |
| **Maintenance** | Easy blade replacement | Drum removal needed |
| **Windrow** | Narrow, clean | Wide, fluffy |

---

#### Q.B (b) — Four Harvesting Methods in India

| # | Method | Equipment | Field Capacity (ha/h) | Most Suitable Crop |
|---|--------|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Manual Harvesting** | Sickle, hand scythe | 0.01 – 0.05 | All crops on small/marginal farms |
| 2 | **Animal-Drawn Mower** | Animal-drawn reciprocating mower | 0.2 – 0.5 | Fodder, wheat in hilly areas |
| 3 | **Tractor-Drawn Reaper / Reaper-Binder** | Reaper, reaper-binder | 0.4 – 0.8 | Paddy, wheat, soybean in medium farms |
| 4 | **Combine Harvester (Direct)** | Self-propelled / tractor-drawn combine | 0.5 – 2.0 | Wheat, paddy, soybean in large farms |
| 5 | **Two-Stage (Windrow) Harvesting** | Swather + Pick-up combine | 0.6 – 1.5 | Wet paddy, lodged crops |
| 6 | **Forage Harvester** | Flail mower, precision chopper | 0.3 – 1.0 | Maize, sorghum, Napier grass (silage) |

**Discussion (1 Mark for context):**
In India, **manual harvesting still accounts for ~60%** of total cereal harvesting area, particularly in eastern and southern states. **Combine harvesting is dominant in Punjab, Haryana, western UP, and Madhya Pradesh.** Two-stage harvesting is gaining popularity in coastal and high-rainfall zones for paddy.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Mixing km/h and m/s. Always convert forward speed to m/s before applying formulas. The examiner specifically checks this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "z" factor:** Students often write $v_{avg} = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ without considering the number of cutting edges $z$. If $z = 2$ (most common), the effective strokes double — **don't miss this!**
>
> 3. **Confusing Operation vs. Method:** Writing "cutting" as a harvesting method is **incorrect**. Cutting is an *operation*; methods are *manual harvesting, direct combining, two-stage, etc.*
>
> 4. **No Diagram = -1 to -2 Marks:** In 7-mark questions, a missing block diagram or labelled sketch attracts a flat deduction.
>
> 5. **Shear vs. Impact Confusion:** Reciprocating mowers use **shear** action, not impact. Disc mowers use **impact**, not shear. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark loss.
>
> 6. **Specific Energy Unit Error:** Writing specific energy as "J" instead of "N·m/mm²" or "J/mm³" loses a unit mark.
>
> 7. **Ignoring Power Factor K:** For rotary cutters, $K$ accounts for transmission losses; omitting it gives an underestimated power value.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4)**
>
> **1. Farm Equipment Categories (4):**
> - Tractor-operated, Self-propelled, Animal-drawn, Stationary
>
> **2. Harvesting Sub-Operations (6):**
> - Reaping, Windrowing, Gathering, Threshing, Cleaning, Bagging
>
> **3. Cutting Mechanism Principles (4):**
> - Shear, Impact, Anvil, Tearing-Bending
>
> **4. Cutting Mechanism Types (Main 5):**
> - Manual (sickle), Reciprocating, Disc, Drum, Flail
>
> **5. Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
> - $v_{avg} = \dfrac{L \cdot n_s \cdot z}{60}$ (reciprocating knife)
> - $v_k = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{60}$ (rotary tip velocity)
> - $h_c = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_m}{n_s \cdot z}$ (forward feed per stroke)
> - $E_c = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{A}$ (specific cutting energy)
> - $P_{total} = K \cdot P_{cut}$ (rotary cutter power)
> - $Q = w \cdot v_m \cdot \rho \cdot y$ (material flow)
>
> **6. Key Numeric Values:**
> - Knife velocity (reciprocating): **3.0 – 5.0 m/s**
> - Disc speed: **1500 – 3500 rpm**
> - Cutter bar pitch: **76.2 mm or 50.8 mm**
> - Specific energy (cereal): **0.15 – 0.40 N·m/mm²**
> - Crop moisture at harvest: **14 – 25 % w.b.**
>
> **7. Harvesting Methods (6):**
> - Manual, Animal-Drawn, Reaper/Reaper-Binder, Direct Combine, Two-Stage, Forage
>
> **8. Selection Logic:**
> - Small farm + low capital → Manual
> - Medium farm + cereal → Reaper / Reaper-Binder
> - Large farm + dry cereal → Direct Combine
> - Large farm + wet/tall crop → Two-Stage (Windrow)
> - Forage / silage → Flail / Precision Chopper
>
> **9. Common Crops in India & Their Preferred Cutter:**
> - Wheat, Paddy → Reciprocating sickle bar
> - Paddy (wet) → Disc mower
> - Maize, Sugarcane → Flail / Circular saw
> - Forage → Drum / Disc mower
>
> **10. KTU Examiner's Top Focus Areas:**
> - Definitions (harvesting, cutting mechanism, harvesting method)
> - Differences (shear vs. impact, manual vs. mechanical, operation vs. method)
> - Formula derivations and numericals
> - Block diagrams of cutter bar and disc mower
> - Harvesting method selection logic

---

topics.title: Harvesting Terminology. Study of Mowers - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT613 - TRACTORS AND FARM EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Harvesting Terminology. Study of Mowers - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Harvesting — The Final Act of Crop Production

**Harvesting** is defined as the operation of cutting, gathering, and removing the mature crop (grain, fodder, or forage) from the field for subsequent processing, storage, or consumption. In the broader sense, harvesting encompasses a chain of field operations including **reaping, swathing, windrowing, threshing, winnowing, baling, and collection**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** This module is the bridge between the *tractor power delivery systems* (Modules 1–3) and the *field machinery applications* (Modules 5–6). The student must master the *terminology and machine taxonomy* before studying operational adjustments and performance evaluations in later modules.

#### 1.2 The Harvesting Terminology Dictionary (KTU Standard Glossary)

The following terms form the **official KTU/OEMUT613 vocabulary** that students are expected to spell, define, and apply correctly in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

| Term | Operational Definition |
|------|------------------------|
| **Reaping** | The mechanical cutting of standing crops at the base, leaving the cut material in a loose or windrowed state. |
| **Swathing** | Cutting a crop and laying it in a continuous row (windrow) on the stubble for drying or further collection. |
| **Windrow** | A continuous, narrow row of cut crop laid on the stubble field after swathing or mowing. |
| **Stubble** | The lower portion of the crop stem (typically 5–15 cm) deliberately left anchored in the soil after cutting. |
| **Stover** | The stalks, leaves, and stems of a crop (e.g., maize, sorghum) that remain in the field after the grain has been harvested. |
| **Lodging** | The permanent displacement of crop stems from their vertical posture, caused by wind, rain, or over-ripeness — a major problem in mechanical harvesting. |
| **Header** | The cutting and gathering assembly of a combine harvester or reaper. |
| **Forage** | Plant material (grass, legumes, or cereals) cultivated primarily to be consumed as livestock feed. |
| **Hay** | Forage that has been **cured** (dried) to a moisture content below **20 %** for safe storage. |
| **Silage** | Fermented, high-moisture forage (typically 60–70 % moisture) preserved by anaerobic lactic acid fermentation. |
| **Curing** | The controlled field drying of freshly cut forage to reduce moisture from ~80 % to a storable level. |
| **Tedding** | Mechanically fluffing, spreading, and inverting cut forage to accelerate uniform drying. |
| **Raking** | Gathering scattered cut hay into a uniform windrow for baling or further drying. |
| **Baling** | Compressing cured hay or straw into compact, transportable units (rectangular or cylindrical bales). |
| **Threshing** | The mechanical separation of grain kernels from the harvested ear/head/panicle. |
| **Winnowing** | Separating threshed grain from lighter chaff, husk, and straw using air streams. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic Anchor:** "**R–S–W–T–B**" — **R**eaping → **S**wathing → **W**indrow → **T**edding/Raking → **B**aling. This is the canonical forage harvesting chain examiners love to test.

#### 1.3 The Mower — Formal Definition

A **Mower** is an agricultural machine whose primary function is to **cut standing forage crops (grasses, legumes, or small grain hay)** at a uniform height above the ground, leaving the cut material in a loose, spread, or windrowed condition for subsequent curing and baling. Unlike a combine or reaper, a mower **does not perform threshing, separating, or cleaning operations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Domain:** Mowers operate in the *forage chain* — i.e., for producing **hay** and **silage**. They are distinguished from reapers (which harvest grain crops) by their cutting action, lower stubble height, and absence of crop-conveying augers.

#### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lawn Mower Mindset"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a mower as a **scalpel for the field**. A combine harvester is like a *factory on wheels* (cuts, threshes, cleans, and bags) — a mower is far simpler: it is the **sharp first stroke** in the hay-making operation. Just as a barber's clipper cuts hair to a uniform length without collecting it, a mower cuts forage to a uniform stubble height and lays the clippings back on the ground to dry. The drying (curing), collection (raking), and packing (baling) are *separate downstream operations* performed by *separate machines*.

The "uniform cut" property is the defining engineering requirement — non-uniform stubble height leads to uneven regrowth, reduced yield, and difficulty in the subsequent raking operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Mowers Are Engineered the Way They Are

The engineering of a mower is governed by **four physical constraints** that any design must respect:

1. **The crop must be cut cleanly, not torn.** Tearing damages regrowth points and accelerates moisture loss from the stubble.
2. **The cut material must not be contaminated with soil.** Soil contamination reduces forage quality and introduces *Clostridium* spores into silage.
3. **The machine must handle *lodged* (flattened) crops.** Indian field conditions (monsoon winds, heavy dew) make lodging common.
4. **The cutting mechanism must be shielded from stones, wire, and stumps.** Foreign object damage is the leading cause of mower breakdown.

### 2.2 The Taxonomy of Mowers — A Hierarchical Classification

Mowers can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: power source, mounting configuration, and cutting mechanism.

#### 2.2.1 Classification by Power Source
- **Animal-Drawn Mowers:** Historically significant, used with bullocks/oxen; obsolete in mechanized Indian agriculture except in hilly regions.
- **Tractor-Drawn (PTO-Operated) Mowers:** Most common in India. The mower is mounted on the 3-point hitch or trailed, and the cutting mechanism is driven by the **Power Take-Off (PTO) shaft at 540 rpm**.
- **Self-Propelled Mowers:** Dedicated machines with their own engine, hydrostatic drive, and operator station — used in large commercial hay operations.

#### 2.2.2 Classification by Mounting
- **Mounted (3-Point Hitch) Mowers:** Attached to the tractor's hydraulic 3-point linkage; lifted hydraulically for transport.
- **Trailed (Pull-Type) Mowers:** Have their own wheels and a drawbar hitch; suitable for smaller tractors without 3-point capacity.
- **Semi-Mounted Mowers:** A combination — partially supported by the tractor and partially by a ground wheel.

#### 2.2.3 Classification by Cutting Mechanism (The KTU-Highlighted Axis)
This is the **most heavily tested classification** in KTU examinations. There are four fundamental cutting mechanisms:

**(a) Reciprocating Sickle-Bar Mower**
- Uses a **stationary ledger plate (guard fingers)** and a **reciprocating triangular-section knife (sickle)**.
- Cutting action is analogous to a **scissors/shear cut** between two opposing sharp edges.
- Cutting speed: 1.5–2.5 m/s (stroke frequency: 700–1200 strokes/min).
- Power consumption: 2–4 kW per metre of cutter bar length.

**(b) Rotary Disc Mower**
- Uses **multiple high-speed rotating discs** (typically 2–7), each carrying 2–3 free-swinging blades.
- Cutting speed at blade tip: 60–90 m/s (very high — enables cutting of lodged and woody crops).
- Power consumption: 5–8 kW per metre of working width.

**(c) Rotary Drum Mower**
- Uses **2–4 large cylindrical drums** rotating about vertical axes; each drum carries a set of pivoting blades at the top.
- Suited for **tall, heavy crops** (maize, sorghum, sugarcane tops).
- Cutting speed: 50–80 m/s at blade tip.

**(d) Flail Mower**
- Uses a **horizontal rotor with numerous T-shaped or Y-shaped free-swinging flails (hammers)**.
- Suited for **rough cutting** — pastures, roadside vegetation, brush, and orchards.
- Cutting action is **impact/shear**, not scissor — produces a chopped, mulched output.

**(e) Reel Mower (Cylinder Mower)**
- Uses a **horizontally rotating reel with radial blades** passing over a stationary bedknife.
- Suited for **fine, short turf and golf course grass** — not commonly used in Indian agriculture.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **exam-critical formula set** for this topic. Memorize the units and the symbol conventions.

| Formula Number | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|----------------|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| F-1 | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \div 60$ | $v$ = peripheral velocity (m/s), $D$ = disc/drum diameter (m), $N$ = rotational speed (rpm) | Compute blade-tip speed in rotary mowers |
| F-2 | $S = f \cdot L \div 60$ | $S$ = mean sickle speed (m/s), $f$ = stroke frequency (strokes/min), $L$ = stroke length (m) | Compute sickle-bar cutting speed |
| F-3 | $Q = W \cdot v_f \cdot Y$ | $Q$ = field capacity (ha/h), $W$ = working width (m), $v_f$ = forward speed (km/h), $Y$ = field efficiency (decimal) | Compute effective field capacity |
| F-4 | $Y = E_e \div T \cdot 100\%$ | $Y$ = field efficiency (%), $E_e$ = effective field time, $T$ = total time | Compute field efficiency from time logs |
| F-5 | $P = K_c \cdot W$ | $P$ = power demand (kW), $K_c$ = specific power (kW/m), $W$ = working width (m) | Estimate PTO power requirement |
| F-6 | $h_s = h_c - h_r$ | $h_s$ = stubble height, $h_c$ = cutter-bar height, $h_r$ = reel/ground clearance | Verify stubble uniformity |
| F-7 | $C_{op} = (C_f + C_l + C_s) \div A$ | $C_{op}$ = operating cost (₹/ha), $C_f$ = fixed cost, $C_l$ = labour cost, $C_s$ = fuel/service cost, $A$ = area covered | Compute cost of operation |
| F-8 | $M_c = Q \cdot t$ | $M_c$ = material cut (kg), $Q$ = feed rate (kg/s), $t$ = operating time (s) | Mass-flow in forage handling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE (Do Not Skip):** In markdown tables, the symbol `\vert` or `\mid` must be used for "absolute value" or "such that" — **never use the vertical bar `|` inside a row** because it breaks the markdown table parser. In your KTU answer sheets, however, plain `|` is acceptable.

### 2.4 Sickle Geometry — The Triangle Behind the Cut

The **sickle (knife) section** of a reciprocating mower has a triangular profile with specific dimensions standardized by the **Indian Standard (IS 6024-1983 for mower blades)**. The geometry determines:

- **Sickle pitch (t):** Distance between adjacent knife sections — typically 25.4 mm, 38.1 mm, or 50.8 mm.
- **Ledger angle (α):** The angle of the ledger plate with respect to the cutter bar — typically 30°–45°.
- **Sickle lead (e):** The lateral offset of each section from the previous one — typically 6–10 mm — which causes the crop to slide toward the centre during cutting.

The lead is what creates the **scissor-like progressive cut**: at any instant, only a portion of the crop is being cut, reducing peak power demand and preventing the knife from jamming.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Blade-Tip Speed in a Rotary Disc Mower

The peripheral (blade-tip) speed of a rotary disc mower determines its ability to cut cleanly through standing crop. A speed too low causes tearing; a speed too high causes excessive blade wear and safety hazards.

**Given:**
- Disc diameter $D$ (m)
- Rotational speed $N$ (rpm)

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to revolutions per second.**

$$
N_{rps} = \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — The distance travelled by the blade tip in one revolution is the circumference of the disc.**

$$
C = \pi \cdot D
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply circumference by revolutions per second to obtain the linear velocity at the blade tip.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= C \cdot N_{rps} \\
v &= \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{N}{60} \\
v &= \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.** For a typical disc mower with $D = 0.45$ m and $N = 3000$ rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\pi \cdot 0.45 \cdot 3000}{60} \\
v &= \frac{4241.15}{60} \\
v &\approx 70.7 \;\; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **> 250 km/h** at the blade tip — a value chosen because the standard empirical threshold for clean impact-cutting of grass stems is **60 m/s**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Sickle Speed in a Reciprocating Mower

The reciprocating knife does not move at constant velocity; it accelerates and decelerates during each stroke. The *mean* speed is sufficient for power and throughput calculations.

**Given:**
- Stroke length $L$ (m) — typically 76.2 mm (3 inches) or 101.6 mm (4 inches).
- Stroke frequency $f$ (strokes per minute).

**Step 1 — In one stroke, the knife travels a distance equal to $2L$ (one full back-and-forth cycle).**

$$
D_{cycle} = 2L
$$

**Step 2 — Total distance travelled in one minute.**

$$
D_{min} = 2L \cdot f
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per-second distance, then average over the two half-strokes (each of length $L$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{2L \cdot f}{60} \\
S &= \frac{L \cdot f}{30} \;\; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **mean velocity**; the **instantaneous maximum** velocity is $\pi/2$ times the mean (since reciprocating motion approximates simple harmonic motion).

**Step 4 — Numerical Check.** For $L = 0.0762$ m and $f = 1000$ strokes/min:

$$
S = \frac{0.0762 \cdot 1000}{30} \approx 2.54 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

This is the textbook figure of **~2.5 m/s** cited in the syllabus.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Mower Types

This is the **most important comparative table** for KTU Part B answers. Students are frequently asked to *"Compare the four types of mowers on specified parameters"* (7 marks).

| Parameter | Sickle-Bar | Rotary Disc | Rotary Drum | Flail |
|-----------|------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| **Cutting mechanism** | Scissor/shear | Impact/shear (high speed) | Impact/shear | Impact/chop |
| **Blade tip speed (m/s)** | 1.5 – 2.5 | 60 – 90 | 50 – 80 | 40 – 60 |
| **Power per metre width (kW/m)** | 2 – 4 | 5 – 8 | 5 – 7 | 8 – 12 |
| **Lodged crop handling** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Stubble uniformity** | Excellent (controlled by cutter bar) | Good | Good | Fair (chopped appearance) |
| **Maintenance** | High (sharpening ledger + knife) | Low (replaceable blades) | Low | Moderate (flail replacement) |
| **Safety risk** | Low (slow reciprocation) | High (projectile risk) | High | Moderate |
| **Typical working width (m)** | 1.5 – 2.1 | 1.6 – 3.2 | 1.6 – 2.4 | 1.2 – 2.5 |
| **Suitability for hay** | Excellent (clean cut, minimal contamination) | Very Good | Good (heavy crops) | Fair (mulches crop) |
| **Indian field suitability** | High (forage, small farms) | Very High (most popular) | Moderate (heavy crops) | Low (orchards/roadsides) |
| **Cost (₹, indicative)** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |

### 3.4 Derivation of Field Capacity

**Given:** Working width $W$ (m), forward speed $v_f$ (km/h), theoretical time per hectare $T_t$ (h/ha), field efficiency $Y$ (decimal).

**Step 1 — Theoretical field capacity (assuming 100% time utilization and no overlap).**

$$
Q_t = \frac{W \cdot v_f}{10} \;\; \text{ha/h}
$$

(The factor 10 converts km/h × m to ha/h via $1 \text{ ha} = 10000 \text{ m}^2$ and $1 \text{ km} = 1000 \text{ m}$, so $\frac{1 \text{ km/h} \cdot 1 \text{ m}}{10000} = \frac{1}{10}$.)

**Step 2 — Effective field capacity accounts for turning, overlap, and stoppages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e &= Y \cdot Q_t \\
Q_e &= Y \cdot \frac{W \cdot v_f}{10} \;\; \text{ha/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.** For a disc mower with $W = 2.4$ m, $v_f = 8$ km/h, $Y = 0.85$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e &= 0.85 \cdot \frac{2.4 \cdot 8}{10} \\
Q_e &= 0.85 \cdot 1.92 \\
Q_e &= 1.632 \;\; \text{ha/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard output range** (1.5 – 2.0 ha/h) for a mid-sized tractor-mounted disc mower in Indian conditions.

### 3.5 The Power Requirement Hierarchy

The total PTO power required to drive a mower has three components:

$$
P_{total} = P_{cut} + P_{fric} + P_{id}
$$

Where:
- $P_{cut}$: Power to actually sever the crop stems — dominant term.
- $P_{fric}$: Power to overcome internal friction in the cutter bar / disc bearings.
- $P_{id}$: Idle power — friction at zero crop flow (bearings, windage, gear meshing).

For sickle-bar mowers, $P_{id}$ can be **30–50 %** of $P_{cut}$ — a major reason for their declining popularity. For rotary mowers, $P_{id}$ is only **10–15 %** of $P_{cut}$ — making them more energy-efficient per unit area at high forward speeds.

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Reciprocating Sickle-Bar Mower

The following block diagram traces the **power flow from the tractor PTO** through every mechanical element to the final cutting action.

```mermaid
    I --> J[Shear Cutting Action]
    J --> K[Cut Forage Falls to Ground as Swath]
```

**Reading Guide for Students:** The power flows in one direction (left to right), starting from the **PTO** and ending at the **shear zone** where the cutting happens. The height adjustment loop (right side) controls only the **position** of the cutter bar, not its motion.

### 4.4 The Hay-Making Operational Sequence

The complete chain of operations in which a mower participates:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Day 0 Mowing] --> B[Day 0 to 1 Tedding 1 to 3 times]
    B --> C[Day 1 to 2 Raking into Windrows]
    C --> D[Day 2 to 3 Baling]
    D --> E[Transport to Storage]
    E --> F[Moisture Target Below 20 percent]
    A --> A1[Mower]
    B --> B1[Tedder]
    C --> C1[Rake]
    D --> D1[Baler]
    A1 -.-> A
    B1 -.-> B
    C1 -.-> C
    D1 -.-> D

    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF1493,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The mower is the **first and most critical link** in this chain. A poorly cut windrow (uneven stubble, soil contamination) **cannot be corrected** by tedding, raking, or baling — those operations can only preserve or degrade the quality delivered by the mower.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1: Define "Windrow" and "Stubble" in the context of harvesting operations. State one operational significance of each.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 | Model Answer]**

**Windrow:** A windrow is the continuous, narrow row of cut crop (forage, hay, or grain straw) that is laid on the stubble field after the mowing or swathing operation. The crop is concentrated into a band narrower than the full cutter-bar width to facilitate drying (curing) and subsequent collection.

**Stubble:** Stubble is the lower portion of the crop stem (typically 5–15 cm in length) that is intentionally left anchored in the soil after the mowing or reaping operation. It supports regrowth in perennial crops and prevents soil contamination of the cut material.

**Operational Significance:**
- For **windrow**: It determines the **drying rate** of the cut crop — a narrower, fluffier windrow dries faster but is more vulnerable to wind scattering; a wider windrow dries slower but is more weather-resistant.
- For **stubble**: It governs the **regrowth vigour** in grasses (e.g., Bermuda grass, ryegrass) and the **ash content** of the harvested hay — taller stubble means lower ash content (less soil).

#### Question A2: List any four harvesting-related terms and define each in one sentence.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Model Answer]**

1. **Reaping** — The mechanical cutting of standing mature crops at the base, typically using a sickle-bar or cutter bar.
2. **Swathing** — Cutting a crop and laying it in a continuous windrow on the stubble for curing or further collection.
3. **Tedding** — The mechanical operation of fluffing and inverting cut hay to accelerate uniform drying.
4. **Baling** — The compression of cured hay or straw into compact, transportable rectangular or cylindrical units.
5. *(Bonus)* **Threshing** — The separation of grain kernels from the harvested ear or panicle.
6. *(Bonus)* **Winnowing** — The separation of threshed grain from chaff and lighter impurities using an air stream.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **reciprocating sickle-bar mower**. List its advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (Key Components):**
1. **Main Frame** — Rigid steel structure that mounts on the tractor's 3-point hitch.
2. **Cutter Bar Assembly** — The horizontal bar carrying the stationary **ledger plates** (also called guard fingers) and the reciprocating **sickle** (triangular-section knife).
3. **Pitman Arm** — A connecting rod that converts the rotary motion of the crankshaft into reciprocating motion of the sickle.
4. **V-Belt Pulley and Bevel Gear Box** — Transmits power from the PTO to the crankshaft, with a speed-up ratio of ~1:1.5 to 1:2.
5. **Inner and Outer Shoe (Skid Plates)** — The shoes that ride on the ground and establish the cutting height.
6. **Reel (optional)** — A bat reel that pushes the standing crop into the cutter bar.
7. **Tilt Adjustment Lever** — Allows the operator to tilt the cutter bar forward or backward for different crop conditions.

**Working Principle:**
- The tractor PTO (540 rpm) drives the input shaft of the mower.
- Power flows through the V-belt to the bevel gear box, which redirects it 90° to the crankshaft.
- The crankshaft's eccentric motion is transmitted via the **pitman arm** to the **sickle**, causing it to reciprocate.
- The triangular knife sections of the sickle slide across the ledger plates, creating a **scissor-like shearing action** that severs the crop stems.
- The cut crop falls behind the cutter bar onto the stubble, where it lies in a loose swath for curing.

**Advantages:**
- Produces a very **uniform stubble height** — ideal for seed production and premium hay.
- **Lower power consumption** (2–4 kW/m) compared to rotary mowers.
- **Cleaner cut** — less bruising of crop stems, preserving forage quality.
- **Lower projectile risk** — slow sickle speed is safer for bystanders.

**Limitations:**
- **Poor performance on lodged crops** — the cutter bar cannot pick up flattened stems.
- **High maintenance** — ledger plates and knife sections require frequent sharpening and replacement.
- **Limited ground speed** — typically restricted to 6–8 km/h to avoid knife clogging.
- **Obstruction-sensitive** — stones and stumps cause knife damage and frequent stops.
- **High idle power consumption** — bearings and pitman friction consume 30–50 % of total power even at zero crop flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **rotary disc mowers** and **rotary drum mowers** on the basis of: (i) construction, (ii) cutting speed, (iii) crop suitability, (iv) power requirement, (v) field efficiency, (vi) maintenance, and (vii) cost.

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Rotary Disc Mower | Rotary Drum Mower |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| (i) | Construction | Multiple (2–7) flat, low-profile discs rotating on a horizontal axis. | 2–4 tall cylindrical drums rotating on a vertical axis. |
| (ii) | Cutting speed (m/s) | 60 – 90 (very high) | 50 – 80 (high) |
| (iii) | Crop suitability | Standard forage hay, lodged crops, mixed grasses. | Tall, heavy crops like maize, sorghum, sudan grass. |
| (iv) | Power requirement (kW/m) | 5 – 8 | 5 – 7 |
| (v) | Field efficiency | 80 – 90 % (high forward speed possible) | 75 – 85 % |
| (vi) | Maintenance | Low — replaceable pivoting blades. | Low to moderate — drum bearing care needed. |
| (vii) | Cost | Medium | Medium to high (larger drums, heavier frame) |

**Concluding Remark:** Disc mowers dominate the **bulk hay market** in India due to their versatility and high forward speed. Drum mowers are preferred in **specialized fodder operations** (e.g., maize silage) where the heavier cutting capacity is required.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **terminology of harvesting operations** with definitions of the following: Reaping, Swathing, Windrowing, Threshing, Winnowing, Stover, and Lodging.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Reaping:** The mechanical cutting of standing mature crops at the base, severing the stem close to the ground. Reaping is the *first step* in grain harvesting and is performed by a reaper, mower, or combine header.

2. **Swathing:** The combined operation of cutting a crop and laying it in a continuous row (windrow) on the stubble, typically for crops that need field drying before threshing (e.g., wheat in some regions, oilseeds).

3. **Windrowing:** The act of forming a windrow — a narrow, elongated heap of cut crop. Windrowing can be done simultaneously with cutting (by a swather) or as a separate operation (by a rake).

4. **Threshing:** The mechanical separation of grain kernels from the harvested ear, head, or panicle. Threshing is performed by impact, friction, or rubbing action in a threshing cylinder/concave assembly.

5. **Winnowing:** The separation of threshed grain from lighter impurities (chaff, husk, straw bits, dust) using an air stream — exploiting the difference in **terminal velocity** between grain and chaff.

6. **Stover:** The residual stalks, leaves, and stems of a crop (commonly maize, sorghum, or millet) that remain in the field after the grain has been harvested. Stover is valuable as livestock feed, bedding, or biofuel feedstock.

7. **Lodging:** The permanent, non-vertical displacement of crop stems caused by wind, rain, over-ripeness, or root disease. Lodged crops are difficult to harvest mechanically and lead to significant grain losses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a tractor-mounted rotary disc mower**. Compute the blade-tip speed for a disc of diameter 0.42 m rotating at 3200 rpm.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Functional Architecture):**

The power flow proceeds as follows:

$$
\text{Tractor PTO (540 rpm)} \rightarrow \text{Input Shaft} \rightarrow \text{Bevel Gear Box} \rightarrow \text{Horizontal Cross Shaft} \rightarrow \text{Spur Gears at Each Disc} \rightarrow \text{Disc Rotors (3200 rpm)} \rightarrow \text{Pivoting Blades} \rightarrow \text{Impact Cutting of Standing Crop} \rightarrow \text{Cut Forage Deflected to Rearward Swath}
$$

**Key Components:**
- **Bevel gear box:** Provides the right-angle drive and the speed-up from 540 rpm to 3200 rpm.
- **Cross shaft:** Distributes power to each disc station.
- **Spur gears:** Individual drives for each disc rotor.
- **Pivoting blades:** 2–3 blades per disc, free to swing outward under centrifugal force.
- **Stone guard / skid shoe:** Protects the discs from ground strikes.
- **Swath board / conditioning unit (optional):** Forms the cut material into a windrow or conditions it for faster drying.

**Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $D = 0.42$ m, $N = 3200$ rpm.

**Using Formula F-1:** $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\pi \cdot 0.42 \cdot 3200}{60} \\
v &= \frac{4222.3}{60} \\
v &\approx 70.37 \;\; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The blade-tip speed of **~70.4 m/s** lies well within the recommended range of 60–90 m/s for clean impact-cutting of grass stems. This confirms the design is well-suited for standard forage harvesting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse a "mower" with a "reaper"** — a mower is for *forage* (hay/silage), a reaper is for *grain*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Do NOT write "blade tip speed" without units** — always specify **m/s** or **km/h** with the numerical value.
> 3. **In comparisons,** students frequently miss the **"crop suitability"** parameter. The examiner expects at least 6–7 comparison parameters; 4–5 will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **In sketches,** do not draw a "mower" when asked for a "sickle-bar knife section" or "sickle geometry." Read the question carefully — "sketch the sickle section" means *just the triangular profile with labelled pitch and lead*, not the whole machine.
> 5. **In derivations,** show every algebraic step. The "Conclusion" or "Engineering Interpretation" line is **mandatory** for full marks.
