### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Harvesting** = Cutting + Gathering + Removing the crop from the field.
- [ ] The **forage chain** is: **Mowing → Tedding → Raking → Baling.**
- [ ] A **mower** cuts forage; a **reaper** cuts grain; a **combine** does both + threshing + cleaning.
- [ ] **Windrow** = continuous row of cut crop; **Stubble** = portion of stem left in field; **Stover** = stalk residue after grain harvest.
- [ ] **Lodging** = flattened crop = harder mechanical harvest = the *single biggest agronomic problem* addressed by rotary mowers.
- [ ] **Four primary mower types**: Sickle-bar, Rotary Disc, Rotary Drum, Flail. (Reel mower is for turf, rarely tested in OEMUT613.)
- [ ] **Sickle-bar** = scissor action, low power, clean cut, poor on lodged crops.
- [ ] **Rotary disc** = impact action, high blade speed (60–90 m/s), most popular in India.
- [ ] **Rotary drum** = impact action, suited for *tall, heavy* crops like maize.
- [ ] **Flail** = chop/impact, for *brush, pasture, roadside* — not for premium hay.
- [ ] **Key Formula 1:** $v = \pi D N / 60$ → **blade-tip speed.**
- [ ] **Key Formula 2:** $S = L f / 30$ → **mean sickle speed.**
- [ ] **Key Formula 3:** $Q_e = Y \cdot W \cdot v_f / 10$ → **effective field capacity in ha/h.**
- [ ] **PTO speed for Indian tractors is standardised at 540 rpm** (and 1000 rpm for higher-power machines).
- [ ] **Sickle pitch** (25.4 / 38.1 / 50.8 mm) and **sickle lead** (6–10 mm) determine the geometry of the reciprocating cut.
- [ ] **Field efficiency** of a mower is typically **80–90 %** — higher than most other field machines (plough, harrow) because turning time is small relative to long passes.
- [ ] **Standard working width** of an Indian tractor-mounted mower: **1.5 – 2.4 m.**
- [ ] **Recommended stubble height** for hay crops: 5–10 cm (taller → lower ash, better regrowth).
- [ ] **Mnemonic for mower selection:** "**F-L-T**" → **F**ine grass → Reel; **L**ight hay → Sickle/Disc; **T**all crop → Drum; Brush/Orchard → Flail.
- [ ] **The mower is the FIRST and most CRITICAL link in hay quality** — downstream operations (tedding, raking, baling) cannot fix a poor cut.
- [ ] **For ESE:** Always draw a **labelled block diagram** whenever the question says "with the help of a diagram" — a text-only answer loses 2–3 marks even if the content is perfect.
- [ ] **For numerical problems:** Always state the given data, the formula, the substitution step, and the final answer with units. Omitting units = -0.5 mark per occurrence.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Study Strategy for this Module:**
> 1. Memorize the **formula sheet (Section 2.3)** — three formulas cover 80 % of numerical questions.
> 2. Memorize the **comparative matrix (Section 3.3)** — it directly answers any "compare" question.
> 3. Practice drawing the **block diagrams (Section 4)** — at least the sickle-bar and rotary disc architectures.
> 4. Learn the **harvesting terminology dictionary (Section 1.2)** — definitions are tested in Part A *every* year.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT613 - TRACTORS AND FARM EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Constructional Details** of a tractor refer to the systematic engineering description of the geometric, mechanical, and material specifications of every major sub-assembly that constitutes a modern farm tractor. This encompasses the engine block, cylinder head, piston-crankshaft assembly, valve train, fuel injection equipment, cooling and lubrication circuits, transmission gearbox, clutch assembly, differential and final drive, hydraulic three-point hitch, Power Take-Off (PTO) shaft, steering mechanism, braking system, and the chassis/frame.

In the KTU 2024 OEMUT613 syllabus, Module 4 specifically analyses the **functional anatomy** of these systems — i.e., *how each component is built, what material it is made from, how its parts are assembled, and how the geometry supports its working duty cycle*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The module focuses on the **constructional features** of the tractor engine (multi-cylinder, vertical, water-cooled, 4-stroke diesel), transmission, hydraulic system, and the working attachments (PTO, hitch, drawbar). Students must be able to *sketch, label, and functionally explain* each assembly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tractor as a "Living Organism"

Imagine a tractor as a **large mechanical animal** working on a farm. Each sub-system is an "organ":

| Tractor System | Biological Analogy | Function |
|----------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Diesel Engine** | Heart | Pumps energy (in the form of rotating crankshaft) into the driveline |
| **Fuel Injection System** | Lungs & Arteries | Delivers metered diesel + air to the combustion chambers |
| **Cooling System (Radiator, Water Pump, Fan)** | Skin & Sweat Glands | Removes excess heat to maintain operating temperature (~85 °C) |
| **Lubricating Oil System** | Blood Circulation | Distributes oil to bearings and pistons to reduce friction |
| **Clutch & Transmission** | Joints & Tendons | Transmits torque and selects speed/torque ratio |
| **Differential & Final Drive** | Hips & Legs | Permits wheel-speed differential during turns |
| **Hydraulic System & 3-Point Hitch** | Muscles & Hands | Lifts and positions implements |
| **Steering System** | Brain-Neck Reflex | Directs the front wheels |
| **Brakes** | Stop Reflex | Converts kinetic energy into heat to halt motion |
| **PTO Shaft** | Power Outlet Socket | Delivers engine power to external implements |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box:**
> - **Bore (D):** Internal diameter of the engine cylinder. Unit: **mm**.
> - **Stroke (L):** Distance travelled by the piston between TDC and BDC. Unit: **mm**.
> - **Compression Ratio (r):** Ratio of total cylinder volume to clearance volume. Typical tractor diesel: **17 : 1 to 22 : 1**.
> - **Displacement Volume (Vd):** Swept volume of one cylinder. Unit: **cc or L**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Operating Metrics of a Modern KTU-Reference Tractor (≈ 45 HP Class)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Engine type | 4-stroke, vertical, water-cooled, direct-injection diesel |
| Number of cylinders | 3 or 4 |
| Bore × Stroke | **110 mm × 116 mm** |
| Total displacement | ≈ **2.73 L (3-cyl)** or **3.64 L (4-cyl)** |
| Rated engine speed | **2200 rpm** |
| Compression ratio | **18.5 : 1** |
| Cooling system capacity | ≈ **8–10 L** of coolant |
| Engine oil capacity | ≈ **7–9 L** |
| Hydraulic pump flow | ≈ **30 L/min** at **180 bar** (≈ **18 MPa**) |
| PTO speed | **540 rpm @ 2200 engine rpm** (standard) |
| Battery voltage | **12 V DC**, typically **88–110 Ah** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Standard Used:** IS 5994 (Indian Standard for Agricultural Tractors – Test Code), IS 4468 (Three-Point Linkage), ISO 730-1 (PTO), SAE J1170 (Hydraulic Couplers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Engine Constructional Details — Block-by-Block Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Cylinder Block (Crankcase)
- **Material:** Close-grained **cast iron** (Grade FG 260) or **aluminium alloy** for light-duty tractors.
- **Function:** Houses the cylinders, crankshaft, camshaft, and provides mounting for the head, sump, and gearbox housing.
- **Construction:** Mono-block (single casting) for 3/4-cylinder tractors. Cylinder liners (wet-type) are press-fitted — coolant directly contacts the liner outer surface for superior heat dissipation.
- **Key features:**
  * Machined bearing saddles for the main bearings (typically 4 for a 4-cylinder engine).
  * Gallery passages for lubrication oil.
  * Water-jacket cavities surrounding the cylinder bores.

### 2.1.2 Cylinder Head
- **Material:** Cast iron (heavy-duty) or aluminium alloy with cast-iron valve seat inserts.
- **Construction:** Houses intake/exhaust ports, valve guides, spark-plug-less injector bore (diesel), and rocker-arm shaft.
- **Head Gasket:** Multi-layer steel (MLS) with elastomer coating. Seals combustion, oil, and coolant simultaneously.

### 2.1.3 Piston Assembly
- **Material:** Aluminium-silicon alloy (eutectic Al-Si, ≈ **12 % Si**) for high strength-to-weight ratio and good thermal conductivity.
- **Construction:**
  * **Crown** (top) – may have a **bowl-in-piston** cavity for direct-injection diesel.
  * **Ring belt** – accommodates **3 rings**: top compression ring (chrome-plated), second compression ring (taper-faced), and oil control ring (spring-expander).
  * **Skirt** – elliptical profile (barrel-shaped) to allow thermal expansion without scuffing.
- **Clearance:** Piston-to-bore ≈ **0.10 – 0.15 mm** at room temperature.

### 2.1.4 Connecting Rod
- **Material:** Drop-forged **carbon steel** (e.g., EN8 / 45 C8), heat-treated and shot-peened.
- **Construction:** I-section shank (for high buckling resistance), small-end with bronze bushing for gudgeon pin, big-end with removable cap held by **two** high-tensile bolts (≈ **M10 × 1.25**).
- **Length-to-stroke ratio:** ≈ **1.6 – 1.8** for diesel tractors (limits side thrust on cylinder walls).

### 2.1.5 Crankshaft
- **Material:** Forged **carbon steel** (e.g., EN15, EN16) or spheroidal graphite (SG) iron; induction-hardened bearing journals (≈ **55–60 HRC**).
- **Construction:** Has main journals (Ø ≈ **70 mm**), crank pins (Ø ≈ **60 mm**), and counterweights to balance reciprocating mass.
- **Bearings:** Thin-shell, steel-backed, lead-bronze or aluminium-tin overlay.

### 2.1.6 Camshaft & Valve Train
- **Drive:** Helical spur gear from crankshaft (gear ratio 1 : 2).
- **Valves:** Poppet-type, typically **2 valves/cylinder** (1 inlet + 1 exhaust); modern tractors use **4 valves/cylinder** for better breathing.
- **Valve seat angle:** **45°** (standard) — promotes swirl and reduces detonation.

### 2.1.7 Flywheel
- **Material:** Cast iron rim with steel hub.
- **Function:** Stores rotational kinetic energy to smooth out the power pulses of the 4-stroke cycle (single firing per cylinder every 2 revolutions).
- **Mounting:** Bolted to rear flange of crankshaft; carries the ring gear for the starter motor pinion.
- **Ring gear teeth:** ≈ **120 – 140 teeth** for reliable starter engagement.

---

## 2.2 Transmission System Construction

### 2.2.1 Clutch
- **Type:** Single-plate, dry-friction, diaphragm-spring type (most modern).
- **Friction disc lining material:** **Ceramic-reinforced phenolic resin** or woven asbestos-free organic.
- **Disc diameter:** ≈ **280 – 330 mm** for 45 HP class.
- **Actuation:** Mechanical (linkage) on economy tractors; hydraulic-assist on premium models.

### 2.2.2 Gearbox
- **Type:** Constant-mesh, sliding-mesh synchromesh (on-road tractors); partial synchromesh on economy models.
- **Gears:** Helical-cut (quiet operation). Number of forward speeds: typically **8F + 2R** or **9F + 3R**.
- **Material:** Case-hardened alloy steel (e.g., **20MnCr5**) with ground teeth.

### 2.2.3 Differential
- **Type:** Open, with crown wheel and pinion.
- **Ratio:** ≈ **3.5 : 1 to 4.5 : 1**.
- **Locking:** Mechanical differential lock (foot-operated) for traction in mud.

### 2.2.4 Final Drive
- **Type:** Epicyclic (planetary) reduction at each rear wheel hub.
- **Ratio:** ≈ **4 : 1 to 6 : 1**.

---

## 2.3 Hydraulic System Construction

### 2.3.1 Hydraulic Pump
- **Type:** External gear pump (most common) or axial-piston pump on higher-HP tractors.
- **Capacity:** ≈ **30 L/min @ 180 bar** (≈ **18 MPa**).

### 2.3.2 Three-Point Hitch (3-Point Linkage)
- **Standards:** IS 4468 / ISO 730-1 — Cat-I (≤ 35 HP), Cat-II (35–100 HP).
- **Components:** Lower links (2), top link (1), lift arms (2), lift rods (2), rocker shaft, hydraulic cylinder.
- **Lift capacity:** ≈ **1500 kgf** at the hitch balls for a 45 HP class.

### 2.3.3 Draft Control Sensor
- **Function:** Measures the horizontal pull force on the top link via a strain-gauge or mechanical deflection link.
- **Output:** Modifies hydraulic valve position to maintain constant implement draft (depth).

---

## 2.4 Power Take-Off (PTO) Shaft

- **Standard speeds:** **540 rpm @ 2200 engine rpm** (standard), **540E @ 1700 rpm** (economy), **1000 rpm @ 2200 rpm** (large tractors).
- **Shaft diameter:** **34.9 mm (1⅜ in)** 6-spline for 540 rpm; **44.4 mm** for 1000 rpm.
- **Direction:** Clockwise viewed from rear of tractor.

---

## 2.5 Steering System

- **Type:** Recirculating-ball mechanical steering (economy) or **hydrostatic power steering** (HPS) on ≥ 35 HP tractors.
- **Turning radius:** ≈ **3.0 – 4.5 m** (without brakes).
- **Steering wheel turns lock-to-lock:** ≈ **4 – 5**.

---

## 2.6 Braking System

- **Type:** **Oil-immersed disc brakes** (mandatory in modern tractors — IS 11858).
- **Actuation:** Mechanical (via rods) or hydraulic-assist.
- **Parking brake:** Independent, hand-lever operated, acts on transmission input shaft.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are dimensionally consistent. Use SI units unless stated. **Never use the vertical pipe character inside a table cell** — use the explicit words *absolute value of* or the LaTeX `\vert` symbol within math mode.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Compression Ratio (r)** | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume | **17 – 22** (diesel) |
| 2 | **Swept Volume of one cylinder** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke | ≈ **1.1 L** |
| 3 | **Total Engine Displacement** | $V_d = N \cdot V_s$ | $N$ = number of cylinders | **3.27 L** (3-cyl) |
| 4 | **Mean Effective Pressure** | $p_{me} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_d \cdot n_c}$ | $W_{net}$ = work per cycle, $n_c$ = cycles/s | **7 – 9 bar** |
| 5 | **Indicated Power (kW)** | $P_i = \dfrac{p_{mi} \, L \, A \, n_c \, N}{60}$ | $p_{mi}$ = mean indicated pressure, $A$ = piston area, $n_c$ = cycles/min | computed |
| 6 | **Brake Power (kW)** | $P_b = \dfrac{2 \pi \, T \, N_e}{60\,000}$ | $T$ = torque in N·m, $N_e$ = engine rpm | **33.5 kW (45 HP)** |
| 7 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_b}{P_i}$ | ratio of brake to indicated power | **0.85 – 0.90** |
| 8 | **Specific Fuel Consumption** | $\text{bsfc} = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | $m_f$ = fuel mass flow in kg/h, $P_b$ = brake power in kW | **0.25 – 0.30 kg/kWh** |
| 9 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{a,act}}{m_{a,th}}$ | actual vs theoretical air mass | **0.80 – 0.90** |
| 10 | **Hydraulic Pressure (Force)** | $F = p \, A$ | $p$ = pressure, $A$ = piston area | computed |
| 11 | **Lift Capacity (3-pt hitch)** | $W = \dfrac{p \, A \, \eta_{hyd}}{n}$ | $\eta_{hyd}$ = hydraulic efficiency, $n$ = mechanical leverage ratio | ≈ **1500 kgf** |
| 12 | **PTO Power** | $P_{pto} \approx 0.85 \, P_b$ | PTO-to-engine power factor | ≈ **38 HP** (45 HP engine) |
| 13 | **Tractive Efficiency** | $\eta_t = \dfrac{\text{Drawbar Power}}{P_b}$ | ratio of drawbar to brake power | **0.55 – 0.65** |
| 14 | **Wheel Slippage** | $s = \dfrac{N_{nl} - N_l}{N_{nl}} \times 100$ | no-load vs loaded wheel rpm | **10 – 15 %** |
| 15 | **Ground Speed (m/s)** | $V = \dfrac{2 \pi r_w N_w}{60}$ | $r_w$ = wheel radius, $N_w$ = wheel rpm | computed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Formulas:**
> - Formulas **1–9** govern engine selection and matching implements to tractor HP.
> - Formulas **10–11** are essential for hydraulic lift calculations (rotavator weight, plough weight).
> - Formulas **12–15** are field-performance indicators (NEFT/NCRT tests as per IS 5994).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Swept Volume, Compression Ratio and Power

### 3.1.1 Swept Volume of One Cylinder

Consider a single cylinder of a tractor engine. Let:
- $D$ = bore diameter = **110 mm = 0.110 m**
- $L$ = stroke length = **116 mm = 0.116 m**

The piston sweeps a cylindrical volume of cross-sectional area $A$ over stroke length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= A \cdot L \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad V_s &= \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} \\[4pt]
V_s &= \frac{\pi \times (0.110)^2 \times 0.116}{4} \\[4pt]
V_s &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0121 \times 0.116}{4} \\[4pt]
V_s &= \frac{0.004408}{4} \\[4pt]
V_s &= 0.001102 \, \text{m}^3 \\[4pt]
V_s &= 1.102 \, \text{L (per cylinder)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Total Engine Displacement (3-Cylinder)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= N \times V_s \\[4pt]
V_d &= 3 \times 1.102 \\[4pt]
V_d &= 3.306 \, \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the displacement of a typical 3-cylinder tractor engine (≈ **3.3 L**).

### 3.1.3 Compression Ratio

Given clearance volume $V_c$ corresponding to compression ratio of **18.5 : 1**, the clearance volume is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} \\[4pt]
\frac{V_s}{V_c} &= r - 1 = 18.5 - 1 = 17.5 \\[4pt]
V_c &= \frac{V_s}{17.5} \\[4pt]
V_c &= \frac{1.102 \, \text{L}}{17.5} \\[4pt]
V_c &= 0.06297 \, \text{L} \\[4pt]
V_c &\approx 62.97 \, \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Indicated Power from Mean Indicated Pressure

Assume $p_{mi}$ = **8 bar = 800 kPa = 800 000 Pa**. Engine runs at $N_e$ = **2200 rpm**. Number of cylinders **N = 3**. Cycles/min $n_c = N_e/2 = 1100$ (for 4-stroke).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= \frac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_c}{60 \, \times \, 1000} \, (\text{kW}) \\[4pt]
L &= 0.116 \, \text{m}, \quad A = \frac{\pi (0.110)^2}{4} = 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2 \\[4pt]
P_i &= \frac{800000 \times 0.116 \times 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 \times 1100}{60 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
P_i &= \frac{800000 \times 0.116 \times 0.009503 \times 3300}{60000} \\[4pt]
P_i &= \frac{2\,908\,567.68}{60000} \\[4pt]
P_i &= 48.48 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Brake Power using Mechanical Efficiency

Take $\eta_m = 0.88$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= \eta_m \times P_i \\[4pt]
P_b &= 0.88 \times 48.48 \\[4pt]
P_b &= 42.66 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to horsepower (1 HP = 0.7457 kW):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HP} &= \frac{42.66}{0.7457} \\[4pt]
\text{HP} &\approx 57.2 \, \text{HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **gross indicated engine output**; the **net PTO power** after subtracting fan, alternator, and water-pump losses (≈ 12–15 %) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pto} &= P_b \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
P_{pto} &= 42.66 \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
P_{pto} &= 36.26 \, \text{kW} \approx 48.6 \, \text{HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the manufacturer's rated **45 HP PTO power** for a 45-HP-class tractor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Hydraulic Lift Capacity

The tractor hydraulic system uses an external gear pump delivering **30 L/min** at **180 bar**. The lift cylinder has bore $D_c = 75 \, \text{mm}$ and rod diameter $d_c = 40 \, \text{mm}$. Mechanical leverage from cylinder to hitch balls = **6 : 1**. Hydraulic-mechanical efficiency $\eta_{hm} = 0.70$.

**Step 1 — Effective cylinder area (annular side, push stroke):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eff} &= \frac{\pi}{4}(D_c^2 - d_c^2) \\[4pt]
D_c &= 0.075 \, \text{m}, \quad d_c = 0.040 \, \text{m} \\[4pt]
A_{eff} &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075^2 - 0.040^2) \\[4pt]
A_{eff} &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.005625 - 0.001600) \\[4pt]
A_{eff} &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.004025) \\[4pt]
A_{eff} &= 3.161 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hydraulic force on piston rod:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{hyd} &= p \cdot A_{eff} \\[4pt]
p &= 180 \, \text{bar} = 18 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} \\[4pt]
F_{hyd} &= 18 \times 10^6 \times 3.161 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
F_{hyd} &= 56\,898 \, \text{N} \\[4pt]
F_{hyd} &\approx 5801 \, \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Lift capacity at hitch balls (after mechanical leverage and losses):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{F_{hyd} \times \eta_{hm}}{6} \\[4pt]
W &= \frac{5801 \times 0.70}{6} \\[4pt]
W &= \frac{4061}{6} \\[4pt]
W &= 676.8 \, \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **lower-link-end lift capacity at standard pressure**. Most 45-HP tractors quote ≈ **1500 kgf** at the hitch balls using a **double-acting external cylinder** and a slightly higher pump pressure (up to **200 bar**). The analytical procedure remains identical.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Specification Summary

| Sub-System | Component | Material | Key Dimension | Function |
|------------|-----------|----------|---------------|----------|
| Engine | Cylinder block | Cast iron FG-260 | Bore 110 mm | Houses cylinders |
| Engine | Piston | Al-Si alloy (12 % Si) | Crown, 3 rings | Converts pressure to motion |
| Engine | Connecting rod | Forged EN8 | I-section, length 230 mm | Transfers force |
| Engine | Crankshaft | Forged EN15 | Main journal Ø 70 mm | Converts reciprocating → rotary |
| Engine | Flywheel | Cast iron + steel hub | Mass ≈ 35 kg | Stores kinetic energy |
| Engine | Valve (intake) | Heat-resistant steel (SUH35) | Stem Ø 8 mm, 45° seat | Admits air |
| Engine | Valve (exhaust) | Nimonic / Inconel-coated | Stem Ø 8 mm, 45° seat | Expels exhaust |
| Lubrication | Oil pump | Cast iron body, steel gears | 30 L/min @ 4 bar | Pressurises oil |
| Cooling | Radiator | Aluminium core + brass tanks | 4-row, 8 L capacity | Dissipates heat |
| Cooling | Water pump | Centrifugal, cast iron body | Flow 60 L/min | Circulates coolant |
| Transmission | Clutch disc | Ceramic-reinforced phenolic | Ø 305 mm | Torque transfer |
| Transmission | Gear (input) | 20MnCr5 case-hardened | Module 2.5, 18 teeth | Speed reduction |
| Differential | Crown wheel | SG iron | Module 4.0, 41 teeth | 90° torque turn |
| Final Drive | Planetary sun gear | EN36 case-hardened | Module 2.0, 17 teeth | Hub reduction |
| Hydraulic | Pump (gear) | Steel gears, Al body | 30 L/min @ 180 bar | Pressure source |
| Hydraulic | Lift cylinder | Honed steel tube | Ø 75 mm bore | Force generation |
| Steering | Steering cylinder | Steel tube, honed | Ø 50 mm | Steering force |
| PTO | PTO shaft | EN8 medium-carbon steel | Ø 34.9 mm, 6-spline | Power outlet |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Modern Tractor (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Engine 4-stroke 3-cylinder] --> B[Clutch Single-plate dry]
    B --> C[Gearbox Constant-mesh 8F 2R]
    C --> D[Differential Crown wheel and pinion]
    D --> E[Final Drive Epicyclic at rear hubs]
    E --> F[Rear Wheels 16.9 x 28 type]
    C --> G[Power Take-Off 540 rpm at 2200 rpm]
    C --> H[Hydraulic Pump External gear 30 L/min]
    H --> I[Control Valve Draft and Position]
    I --> J[3-Point Hitch Cat-I or Cat-II]
    A --> K[Cooling System Radiator and Fan]
    A --> L[Lubrication System Oil pump and galleries]
    A --> M[Fuel System Injection pump and injector]
    A --> N[Air Intake Turbocharger and intercooler]
    F --> O[Steering System Hydrostatic]
    F --> P[Brake System Oil-immersed disc]
```

## 4.2 Engine Block Construction (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Cylinder Block Cast iron FG-260] --> A2[Wet-type Cylinder Liner]
    A2 --> A3[Piston Al-Si alloy]
    A3 --> A4[Piston Rings 3 rings]
    A3 --> A5[Gudgeon Pin]
    A5 --> A6[Connecting Rod Forged steel I-section]
    A6 --> A7[Crankshaft Forged EN15]
    A7 --> A8[Main Bearings Steel-backed babbitt]
    A7 --> A9[Flywheel Cast iron]
    A1 --> A10[Camshaft Helical gear driven]
    A10 --> A11[Valve Tappet]
    A11 --> A12[Push Rod]
    A12 --> A13[Rocker Arm]
    A13 --> A14[Intake Valve 45 degree seat]
    A13 --> A15[Exhaust Valve Nimonic]
    A1 --> A16[Oil Sump Cast aluminium]
    A16 --> A17[Oil Pump Gear type]
    A17 --> A18[Oil Galleries in block]
    A18 --> A8
    A1 --> A19[Water Jacket around cylinders]
    A19 --> A20[Water Pump Centrifugal]
    A20 --> A21[Thermostat 82 degree C]
    A21 --> A22[Radiator 4-row aluminium core]
```

## 4.3 Hydraulic System & 3-Point Hitch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Engine Crankshaft] --> H2[Hydraulic Pump External gear]
    H2 --> H3[Pressure Relief Valve 200 bar]
    H3 --> H4[Control Valve Block Position plus Draft]
    H4 --> H5[Double-acting Lift Cylinder]
    H5 --> H6[Rocker Shaft]
    H6 --> H7[Lift Arms Two]
    H7 --> H8[Lift Rods Two]
    H8 --> H9[Lower Links Two]
    H6 --> H10[Top Link Single]
    H9 --> H11[Hitch Balls Cat-I or Cat-II]
    H10 --> H11
    H4 --> H12[External Hydraulic Coupler SCV]
    H12 --> H13[Tipping Trailer or Remote Cylinder]
    H4 --> H14[Position Sensor Linkage]
    H4 --> H15[Draft Sensor Top-link strain gauge]
    H14 --> H4
    H15 --> H4
```

## 4.4 Transmission Power-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Crankshaft] --> T2[Clutch Disc Single-plate]
    T2 --> T3[Clutch Flywheel-side]
    T2 --> T4[Transmission Input Shaft]
    T4 --> T5[Constant-mesh Gear Train]
    T5 --> T6[Sliding Synchromesh Hub]
    T6 --> T7[Countershaft Gears]
    T7 --> T8[Output Shaft of Gearbox]
    T8 --> T9[Bevel Pinion]
    T9 --> T10[Crown Wheel Differential]
    T10 --> T11[Half Shafts Left and Right]
    T11 --> T12[Planetary Final Drive Sun Gear]
    T12 --> T13[Planet Gears 3 per side]
    T13 --> T14[Ring Gear Fixed to axle housing]
    T13 --> T15[Planet Carrier]
    T15 --> T16[Rear Axle Hub]
    T16 --> T17[Rear Tyre 16.9 x 28]
    T8 --> T18[PTO Clutch Separate]
    T18 --> T19[PTO Shaft 6-spline 540 rpm]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Note:** Each numbered node represents a physical component. Arrows denote the *direction of mechanical power flow*, not fluid flow. This is the **canonical KTU examiner-expected functional block diagram** for Module 4 questions worth 7 to 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Model answers are concise — 3-mark answers should be 80–120 words with a labelled sketch wherever possible.*

### Question 1 — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any six constructional features of a multi-cylinder diesel engine used in modern tractors."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Vertical in-line configuration** of cylinders for compact frontal area and low centre of gravity.
2. **Water-cooled** with a centrifugal water pump and 4-row radiator.
3. **Wet-type cylinder liners** made of centrifugally cast iron, replaceable without disturbing the block.
4. **Direct-injection combustion chamber** with a bowl-in-piston design and a multi-hole injector.
5. **Forged steel crankshaft** with induction-hardened journals and counterweights for balance.
6. **Overhead valve (OHV) gear-driven camshaft** operating poppet valves through push-rods and rocker arms.
7. **(Optional)** Aluminium-silicon piston with three rings and a barrel-shaped skirt.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse OHV with OHC. Tractors predominantly use **OHV (push-rod)** — not OHC (overhead camshaft). Writing "OHC" will cost full marks.

---

### Question 2 — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the constructional difference between a gear pump and a vane pump as used in tractor hydraulic systems."**

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | External Gear Pump | Vane Pump |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Moving parts** | Two intermeshing gears (drive and driven) | Rotor with sliding vanes in a slotted stator |
| **Displacement per rev** | Fixed by gear geometry | Fixed by vane eccentricity |
| **Pressure capability** | Up to **200 bar** | Up to **175 bar** |
| **Typical efficiency** | Volumetric **92 – 95 %** | Volumetric **85 – 90 %** |
| **Noise level** | Higher (gear meshing frequency) | Lower (sliding contact) |
| **Application** | Most 35–55 HP tractors | Premium 60 HP+ tractors |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

The **gear pump** is preferred in tractors for its simplicity, robustness against dirty oil, and ability to deliver high flow at moderate pressures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is split into sub-parts. Module internal choice is provided.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **constructional details of a 4-stroke diesel engine block** used in tractors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-cylinder, 4-stroke tractor diesel engine has bore **95 mm** and stroke **110 mm**. The engine runs at **2400 rpm** with a mean effective pressure of **7.5 bar**. The mechanical efficiency is **88 %** and the bsfc is **0.28 kg/kWh**. Calculate:
1. Brake Power in kW and HP
2. Total fuel consumption in kg/h
3. Volumetric efficiency if the air-fuel ratio is 18 : 1 by mass. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Details of 4-Stroke Diesel Engine Block (7 marks):**

The engine block is the **main structural casting** of the tractor diesel engine. Constructional features:

1. **Material** – Close-grained cast iron (Grade FG 260) for high rigidity and vibration damping; aluminium-alloy blocks are used on light commercial tractors only.
2. **Cylinder arrangement** – Vertical, in-line configuration (3 or 4 cylinders) with a single mono-block casting.
3. **Wet cylinder liners** – Centrifugally cast iron sleeves press-fitted into the block; coolant circulates around them. Liners are replaceable.
4. **Water-jacket** – Cored passages around each cylinder bore and above the exhaust ports allow coolant to circulate from the water pump → cylinder sleeves → cylinder head → thermostat → radiator.
5. **Main bearing saddles** – Machined supports for the crankshaft main journals; typically four bearings for a 4-cylinder engine.
6. **Cam-shaft tunnel** – On OHV engines, an additional tunnel on the block side houses the camshaft driven by helical gears from the crankshaft at 1 : 2 ratio.
7. **Oil galleries** – Internal drillings deliver pressurised oil from the oil pump to the main bearings, big-end bearings, camshaft bearings, and valve gear.
8. **Mounting faces** – Front face carries the timing-gear cover; rear face mates with the flywheel housing; bottom face carries the oil sump; top face mates with the cylinder-head gasket.

A labelled cross-section (refer Section 4.2 of these notes) must accompany this answer in the exam.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of cylinders $N = 3$
- Bore $D = 95 \, \text{mm} = 0.095 \, \text{m}$
- Stroke $L = 110 \, \text{mm} = 0.110 \, \text{m}$
- Engine speed $N_e = 2400 \, \text{rpm}$
- Mean effective pressure $p_{me} = 7.5 \, \text{bar} = 7.5 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$
- bsfc = $0.28 \, \text{kg/kWh}$
- Air–fuel ratio $= 18 : 1$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of piston:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.095)^2}{4} \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{\pi \times 0.009025}{4} \\[4pt]
A &= 7.088 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Brake Power directly (using $p_{me}$ which already accounts for mechanical losses if used as brake MEP, but here it is mean effective pressure at indicated level; we compute $P_i$ first):**

For 4-stroke, cycles per second = $N_e / (2 \times 60) = 2400 / 120 = 20 \, \text{cycles/s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= p_{me} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_c \\[4pt]
P_i &= 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.110 \times 7.088 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 \times 20 \\[4pt]
P_i &= 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.110 \times 7.088 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \\[4pt]
P_i &= 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.046781 \\[4pt]
P_i &= 35\,085.6 \, \text{W} = 35.09 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Brake Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= \eta_m \times P_i = 0.88 \times 35.09 = 30.88 \, \text{kW} \\[4pt]
\text{HP} &= \frac{30.88}{0.7457} = 41.42 \, \text{HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_f &= \text{bsfc} \times P_b = 0.28 \times 30.88 \\[4pt]
m_f &= 8.646 \, \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric efficiency:**

Theoretical air mass at ambient (assume $T = 305 \, \text{K}$, $p = 1.013 \, \text{bar}$, $\rho_a = 1.165 \, \text{kg/m}^3$):

Total displacement $V_d = N \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L = 3 \times 7.088 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.110 = 2.339 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$

Volumetric flow of air intake = $V_d \times (N_e / 2 \times 60) = 2.339 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = 0.04678 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

Theoretical air mass flow = $0.04678 \times 1.165 = 0.05450 \, \text{kg/s}$

Actual air mass flow = Fuel mass flow × AFR = $\frac{8.646}{3600} \times 18 = 0.002402 \times 18 = 0.04324 \, \text{kg/s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_v &= \frac{m_{a,act}}{m_{a,th}} = \frac{0.04324}{0.05450} = 0.793 \\[4pt]
\eta_v &\approx 79.3 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Brake Power | **30.88 kW (41.42 HP)** |
| Fuel consumption | **8.65 kg/h** |
| Volumetric efficiency | **≈ 79.3 %** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes in (b):**
> 1. Students confuse *mean effective pressure* with *brake mean effective pressure*. If the problem says $p_{me}$ without specifying, you must compute $P_i$ first then apply $\eta_m$. If the problem gives **bmep**, the formula directly yields $P_b$.
> 2. **Unit trap:** pressure must be in **Pa** (not bar) when working in SI. Multiply bar by $10^5$.
> 3. The factor **N · $n_c$** in the power formula already accounts for all cylinders and cycles; double-multiplying is a frequent error.
> 4. **Air-fuel ratio direction:** by mass, AFR = (mass of air) / (mass of fuel). Students sometimes invert it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of the three-point hitch system** in a tractor. List its main components and state the relevant Indian Standard. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tractor hydraulic pump delivers **28 L/min** at a system pressure of **175 bar**. The lift cylinder has bore **70 mm** and stroke **120 mm**. Mechanical leverage from cylinder to hitch balls is **5 : 1** and overall efficiency is **0.65**. Calculate:
1. Hydraulic force developed at the piston
2. Useful lift capacity at the hitch balls (in kgf)
3. Time required to fully extend the cylinder (in seconds) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working of Three-Point Hitch (7 marks):**

The **three-point hitch** (also called 3-point linkage) is the standard rear-mounted implement-attachment system on every modern tractor. It is governed by **IS 4468** in India (and ISO 730-1 internationally).

**Main Components:**

1. **Lower Links (Two)** – Forged steel arms pivoted on the tractor body at their front ends. Each carries a hitch ball at its rear end.
2. **Top Link (One)** – Adjustable-length link connecting the implement's top bracket to the tractor's top-link mounting point. Used for depth control in mounted ploughs.
3. **Lift Arms (Two)** – Cranked arms actuated by the hydraulic rockershaft; they lift the inner ends of the lower links through lift rods.
4. **Lift Rods (Two)** – Adjustable-length tension rods between the lift arms and the lower links; allow levelling of the implement laterally.
5. **Rocker Shaft (or Lift Shaft)** – A heavy transverse shaft rotated by the hydraulic lift cylinder; its arms raise or lower the lift arms.
6. **Lift (Hydraulic) Cylinder** – Double-acting cylinder receiving pressurised oil from the control valve; it rotates the rocker shaft.
7. **Draft-Control Sensor** – A strain-gauge / mechanical-deflection sensor in the top-link mounting; it senses horizontal pull on the implement and signals the control valve to maintain constant draft (depth).
8. **Position-Control Linkage** – Mechanical linkage to set and hold the implement at a desired vertical position irrespective of draft.

**Working Principle:**

The tractor's external-gear hydraulic pump draws oil from the transmission sump and delivers it through a relief valve to the **control valve**. The control valve meters oil to the lift cylinder based on inputs from the **draft sensor** and the **position lever**. When the operator lifts the lever, oil enters the cylinder bore, extending the piston, which rotates the rocker shaft, raising the lift arms, which (through the lift rods) raise the lower links — thus lifting the attached implement.

**Indian Standard:** **IS 4468** (Agricultural Tractors — Three-Point Linkage). Categories are Cat-I (≤ 35 HP) and Cat-II (35 – 100 HP).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Pump flow $Q = 28 \, \text{L/min} = 28 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{min} = \dfrac{28 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 4.667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Pressure $p = 175 \, \text{bar} = 17.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$
- Bore $D_c = 70 \, \text{mm} = 0.070 \, \text{m}$
- Stroke $L_c = 120 \, \text{mm} = 0.120 \, \text{m}$
- Mechanical leverage = 5 : 1
- Overall efficiency $\eta_o = 0.65$

**Step 1 — Piston area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D_c^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.070)^2}{4} \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0049}{4} \\[4pt]
A &= 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hydraulic force on piston:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{hyd} &= p \cdot A \\[4pt]
F_{hyd} &= 17.5 \times 10^6 \times 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
F_{hyd} &= 67\,340 \, \text{N} \\[4pt]
F_{hyd} &\approx 6866 \, \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Useful lift capacity at hitch balls:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{F_{hyd} \times \eta_o}{\text{leverage ratio}} \\[4pt]
W &= \frac{6866 \times 0.65}{5} \\[4pt]
W &= \frac{4462.9}{5} \\[4pt]
W &= 892.6 \, \text{kgf}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to extend cylinder:**

Volume of oil required per stroke = $A \times L_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oil} &= 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.120 \\[4pt]
V_{oil} &= 4.618 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Pump volumetric delivery rate = $4.667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{V_{oil}}{Q} = \frac{4.618 \times 10^{-4}}{4.667 \times 10^{-4}} \\[4pt]
t &= 0.989 \, \text{s} \approx 1.0 \, \text{second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Hydraulic force | **67 340 N (≈ 6866 kgf)** |
| Lift capacity at hitch balls | **≈ 893 kgf** |
| Time for full extension | **≈ 1.0 s** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes in (b):**
> 1. **Leverage ratio direction:** leverage *amplifies* force at hitch balls but *reduces* lift speed. Students often invert the ratio.
> 2. **Oil flow vs piston speed:** flow rate gives *piston velocity*, not *force*. Don't confuse them.
> 3. **kgf vs N:** $1 \, \text{kgf} = 9.81 \, \text{N}$. Use the correct conversion unless the problem requests N.
> 4. **Pump pressure vs system pressure:** if a pressure drop across the control valve is given (e.g., 10 bar), subtract it before computing force.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Constructional Details**

- [x] Modern tractor engine: **4-stroke, vertical, water-cooled, direct-injection diesel**, typically 3 or 4 cylinders.
- [x] **Bore** = 95 – 110 mm; **Stroke** = 110 – 116 mm; **Compression ratio** = 17 – 22 : 1.
- [x] **Cylinder block** — cast iron FG-260 with **wet-type liners** and water-jacket.
- [x] **Piston** — Al-Si (12 %) alloy with **3 rings** and barrel-shaped skirt.
- [x] **Crankshaft** — forged EN15/EN16, induction-hardened journals, with flywheel mass ≈ 30 – 40 kg.
- [x] **Valve gear** — OHV (push-rod), poppet valves, **45° seat angle**, gear-driven camshaft at 1 : 2 ratio.
- [x] **Clutch** — single-plate, dry, diaphragm-spring type, ceramic-reinforced phenolic lining.
- [x] **Gearbox** — constant-mesh + synchromesh, helical-cut gears, **8F + 2R** or **9F + 3R** typical.
- [x] **Differential** — crown-wheel/pinion, ratio ≈ 3.5 – 4.5 : 1, mechanical lock available.
- [x] **Final drive** — epicyclic (planetary) reduction, ratio ≈ 4 – 6 : 1.
- [x] **Hydraulic pump** — external gear type, **30 L/min @ 180 bar** typical.
- [x] **3-point hitch** — Cat-I / Cat-II per **IS 4468**; lift capacity ≈ 1500 kgf.
- [x] **PTO** — **540 rpm @ 2200 engine rpm** (standard), **34.9 mm 6-spline**.
- [x] **Steering** — hydrostatic power steering on ≥ 35 HP tractors.
- [x] **Brakes** — **oil-immersed disc brakes** (mandatory per IS 11858).
- [x] Key formulas: $V_s = \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$; $r = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$; $P_b = \dfrac{2 \pi T N_e}{60\,000}$; $F = pA$; $\eta_t = \dfrac{P_{db}}{P_b}$.
- [x] Always convert bar → Pa and L/min → m³/s before substitution in SI equations.
- [x] Memorise two IS codes: **IS 4468** (3-point linkage) and **IS 11858** (oil-immersed brakes).
- [x] **OEMUT613 specific:** KTU frequently asks constructional sketches of (i) engine block, (ii) hydraulic hitch, (iii) clutch assembly. Practise **labelled** diagrams — unlabelled sketches get zero credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working. Introduction to Threshing Systems - Manual and Mechanical Systems. Types of Threshers - Tangential and Axial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT613 - TRACTORS AND FARM EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working. Introduction to Threshing Systems - Manual and Mechanical Systems. Types of Threshers - Tangential and Axial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Threshing — The Fundamental Operation

**Threshing** is the post-harvest agricultural operation of **separating the grain (seed) from the harvested plant material** — specifically, detaching the kernels from the ear-head, panicle, or pod after the crop has been cut and dried. The harvested plant part is termed the **"sheaf"** (for cereals) or **"windrow"**, and the material after grain removal is called **"straw"** or **"chaff"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Threshing is a mechanical process that involves impact, friction, and rubbing actions applied to the crop mass between a rotating drum (cylinder) and a stationary concave, causing the grains to be detached from the ears without significantly damaging the kernels.*

The **principle of separation** relies on three concurrent mechanical actions:
1. **Impact** — sudden striking force dislodges loosely held grains.
2. **Rubbing / Friction** — shearing force between the rotating rasp bar and the stationary concave shears the grain from the panicle.
3. **Centrifugal action** — the drum rotation flings the threshed mass against the concave perforations, allowing grains to fall through.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Threshing is like..."

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Nut Cracker Principle:**
> Imagine cracking a fistful of peanuts still inside their dry, fibrous shells against a rough stone surface. The "thump-impact" shatters the shell, and the subsequent "rubbing motion" against the textured surface releases the kernel. A threshing machine is essentially an **industrial-scale, continuous nut cracker** — except that the goal is to keep the kernel INTACT and only break the bond between the grain and the panicle. The drum acts as the "fist", the concave as the "stone", and the crop is the "peanut bunch."

## 1.3 Classification of Threshing Systems

Threshing systems are broadly classified into two main categories based on the **power source and degree of mechanization**:

### A. Manual Threshing Systems
| System | Typical Tool | Crop Application | Throughput |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Beating Method** | Wooden flail / stick | Wheat, paddy (small scale) | 0.05–0.10 t/h |
| **Foot Treading** | Animal hooves / human feet | Pulses, millets | 0.03–0.08 t/h |
| **Hand Rubbing** | Stone / log | Sorghum, mustard | 0.02–0.05 t/h |
| **Animal Trampling (Kutcha floor)** | Bullock-driven | Wheat, barley | 0.10–0.20 t/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Threshing Efficiency (η):** A well-designed mechanical thresher must achieve **≥ 98% threshing efficiency** with **grain damage < 2%** and **scatter loss < 1%**. Manual methods rarely exceed 80–85% efficiency.

### B. Mechanical Threshing Systems
These are **power-operated threshers** driven by electric motors, tractor PTO shafts, or standalone engines. They dominate modern commercial agriculture due to their:
- **High throughput** (1–10 tonnes/hour for large axial flow units)
- **Consistent quality** of separation
- **Reduced labor dependency** (1 operator vs. 8–10 manual laborers)
- **Lower grain losses** (5–8% reduction compared to manual beating)

## 1.4 Why Threshing Matters in the Tractor–Farm Equipment Context

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> Modern tractor-mounted threshers (especially **axial flow designs**) are the workhorses of India's wheat-paddy belt. Understanding their working is **mandatory** for any agricultural engineer because:
> - The **tractor PTO (540 rpm / 1000 rpm)** is the standard power input.
> - The **thresher drum speed** must be matched to the crop type to minimize grain breakage.
> - **Safety, throughput, and grain quality** all depend on correct design of the drum-concave assembly.
> 
> This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Illustrate the working of various farm equipment used for harvesting and post-harvest operations."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Mechanical Thresher

The complete threshing process in a mechanical system follows a **sequential four-stage logic**:

### Stage 1 — Feeding (Induction)
The cut/dried crop is fed into the thresher through a **feeding chute/tray** either manually or via a **conveyor auger**. The crop enters the space between the **rotating cylinder (drum)** and the **stationary concave**.

### Stage 2 — Threshing (Detachment)
As the drum (fitted with **rasp bars, pegs, or wire loops**) rotates at high peripheral speed, the crop is subjected to:
- **Impact** from the rasp bars (dominant for paddy and wheat).
- **Rubbing/shearing** as the crop is dragged across the concave wires (dominant for maize and soybean).

The peripheral speed of the drum is critical:

$$v_{peripheral} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

where:
- $v_{peripheral}$ = peripheral speed of drum in m/s
- $D$ = diameter of threshing drum in m
- $N$ = rotational speed of drum in rpm

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical peripheral speeds by crop type:**
> - **Paddy:** 18–22 m/s (lower to avoid husk cracking)
> - **Wheat:** 25–30 m/s (higher to break tight glumes)
> - **Maize:** 10–15 m/s (very gentle, threshed by impact only)
> - **Soybean:** 12–18 m/s (low to prevent kernel splitting)

### Stage 3 — Separation (Concave Perforations)
The **concave** is a curved, perforated screen (round or slotted holes) that wraps the lower half of the drum. Threshed grains fall through these perforations due to **gravity + centrifugal force**, while the long straw continues to travel along the drum axis.

The **concave clearance** (distance between drum rasp bar tip and concave surface) is a critical design parameter:
- **Narrow clearance (5–15 mm)** → high threshing, high breakage
- **Wide clearance (20–40 mm)** → lower threshing, less damage

### Stage 4 — Cleaning (Blower + Sieves)
A **centrifugal blower** generates an airstream that lifts the **light chaff, husk, and straw bits** while the heavier **clean grain** falls onto the sieves. The **oscillating sieves** further separate grain from unthreshed material.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drum peripheral speed** | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | Determines threshing aggressiveness |
| 2 | **Threshing efficiency** | $\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100$ | % | Quality metric — must be ≥ 98% |
| 3 | **Cleaning efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_{gc}}{W_{gc} + W_{uc}} \times 100$ | % | Quality of blower-sieve stage |
| 4 | **Total grain loss** | $L_{total} = L_{scatter} + L_{broken} + L_{unthreshed}$ | % | Combined performance index |
| 5 | **Throughput capacity** | $Q = \dfrac{W_c}{t}$ | t/h (or kg/s) | Operational productivity |
| 6 | **Specific power consumption** | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{input}}{Q}$ | kWh/t | Energy efficiency benchmark |
| 7 | **Concave angle of wrap** | $\theta$ (typical: 90°–180°) | degrees | Determines threshing duration |
| 8 | **Drum index / size** | $K = D \times L$ | m² | Sizing rule: 0.20–0.35 m² per t/h |
| 9 | **Grain damage index** | $D_i = \dfrac{W_{broken}}{W_{total}}$ × 100 | % | Must be < 2% for commercial grade |
| 10 | **Blower air velocity** | $v_{air} = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{A_{blower}}$ | m/s | Must exceed terminal velocity of chaff |

> Where:
> - $W_g$ = weight of grain threshed, $W_u$ = weight of unthreshed grain
> - $W_{gc}$ = weight of clean grain, $W_{uc}$ = weight of unclean grain
> - $W_c$ = total crop weight, $t$ = time, $P_{input}$ = power input
> - $L$ = drum length, $A_{blower}$ = blower outlet area

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Field Engineering:**
> - **Drum sizing** governs whether a 35 HP or 50 HP tractor is needed.
> - **Blower calibration** determines whether grain reaches the market (with < 2% foreign matter) or gets rejected.
> - **Peripheral speed selection** decides whether you process **paddy at 20 m/s** (low breakage) or **sunflower at 30 m/s** (high threshing).
> - **Concave design** (round vs. slotted perforations) is crop-specific: **round holes for wheat/paddy, slotted for soybean/maize**.
> - **Safety** — unguarded rotating drums cause severe injuries; the 2024 KTU syllabus stresses **operator safety interlocks** and **PTO shielding**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Drum Speed for a Given Peripheral Velocity

> **Problem Setup:** A paddy thresher has a drum diameter of **0.60 m**. The required peripheral speed for paddy is **20 m/s**. Determine the **drum rotational speed in rpm** and the **suitable PTO speed** (tractor) to drive it through a standard V-belt pulley ratio.

**Given:**
- Drum diameter $D = 0.60$ m
- Required peripheral speed $v = 20$ m/s
- Standard tractor PTO speeds: $N_{PTO} = 540$ rpm (or 1000 rpm)

**Step 1:** Start with the peripheral speed equation.

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for the drum speed $N$ (in rpm).

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$N = \frac{60 \times 20}{\pi \times 0.60}$$

$$N = \frac{1200}{1.88496}$$

$$N = 636.6 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **V-belt pulley ratio** assuming tractor PTO of 540 rpm.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{N_{drum}}{N_{PTO}} = \frac{636.6}{540} = 1.179$$

> This means a **step-up pulley** (driven pulley slightly larger than driver) is required: a **driver pulley of 254 mm** and **driven pulley of 300 mm** would yield the required speed.

**Step 5:** Verify by substituting back.

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.60 \times 636.6}{60} = \frac{1199.7}{60} \approx 20.0 \text{ m/s} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Throughput Capacity from Drum Geometry

> **Problem:** An axial flow thresher has a drum diameter of **0.70 m** and a drum length of **2.5 m**. The crop feed rate is **0.4 kg per second per square metre of drum area**. Calculate the **theoretical throughput in t/h**.

**Given:**
- Drum diameter $D = 0.70$ m
- Drum length $L = 2.5$ m
- Specific feed rate $q = 0.4$ kg/s per m²

**Step 1:** Calculate the **projected drum area** (cylindrical surface).

$$A_{drum} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$A_{drum} = \pi \times 0.70 \times 2.5 = 5.4978 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Calculate mass flow rate.

$$\dot{m} = q \times A_{drum} = 0.4 \times 5.4978 = 2.199 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert kg/s to t/h.

$$Q = \dot{m} \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 2.199 \times 3.6 = 7.917 \text{ t/h}$$

**Conclusion:** The thresher can theoretically process **≈ 7.9 t/h**, which justifies its application in **medium-to-large commercial paddy operations** and pairs well with a **45–60 HP tractor**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Manual vs. Mechanical Threshing Comparison

| Parameter | Manual Threshing | Mechanical Threshing | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | Human / animal muscle | Tractor PTO / electric motor | Mechanization multiplier |
| **Throughput** | 0.05–0.2 t/h | 1–10 t/h (50–100× higher) | Productivity leap |
| **Threshing efficiency** | 75–85% | 97–99% | Reduces field loss |
| **Grain damage** | 3–8% | 0.5–2% | Marketable grade |
| **Labor requirement** | 8–12 persons | 1–2 persons | Social benefit |
| **Initial cost (₹)** | ₹2,000–10,000 | ₹1,50,000–5,00,000 | Capital investment |
| **Lifespan** | Indefinite (tools) | 8–15 years | Depreciation planning |
| **Scatter loss** | 5–10% | 1–3% | Income protection |
| **Scalability** | Very low | High | Commercial farming |
| **Skill required** | Low (traditional) | Medium (training needed) | Capacity building |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight (2024 Scheme):** The economic breakeven for purchasing a mechanical thresher is typically reached when the **annual throughput exceeds 100–150 tonnes**, which corresponds to a farm size of **4–6 hectares** in India's wheat/paddy belt.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Required to Drive the Threshing Drum

> **Problem:** A thresher drum of mass moment of inertia $I = 12$ kg·m² must be accelerated from rest to operating speed of 600 rpm in 8 seconds. Find the **average power required during startup**.

**Step 1:** Convert the operating speed to angular velocity.

$$\omega_f = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the rotational kinetic energy at operating speed.

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_f^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (62.832)^2$$

$$E_k = 6 \times 3947.84 = 23,687 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the average power over the startup time.

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{t} = \frac{23,687}{8} = 2,961 \text{ W} \approx 2.96 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Add the continuous threshing power (estimated 5–8 kW) for total running load.

$$P_{total} = P_{startup,avg} + P_{continuous} \approx 2.96 + 6 = 8.96 \text{ kW} \approx 12 \text{ HP}$$

> **Conclusion:** A **15 HP electric motor** or a **tractor PTO delivering ~12 kW** is sufficient to drive this thresher. The startup transient explains why a **soft-start VFD or hydraulic coupling** is recommended for electric-driven units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Mechanical Thresher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cut Crop / Sheaf Input] --> B[Feeding Tray Chute]
    B --> C[Threshing Drum with Rasp Bars]
    C --> D[Stationary Concave with Perforations]
    D --> E[Threshed Grain + Chaff Mixture]
    E --> F[Centrifugal Blower Air Stream]
    F --> G[Oscillating Sieves Upper and Lower]
    G --> H[Clean Grain Collection Outlet]
    G --> I[Light Chaff Discharge]
    D --> J[Straw Outlet Rear]
    C -.-> K[PTO Shaft / V-Belt Drive]
    K -.-> C
```

> **Reading Guide:** Crop flows along the **solid arrows** (top-down path from feeding to grain output). The **dashed arrow** represents the **power transmission** path from the tractor PTO to the drum.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Tangential Flow Thresher (Spike-Tooth / Rasp-Bar)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TF1[Tangential Flow Thresher]
        direction TB
        P1[Feeding Chute] --> P2[High-Speed Rasp-Bar Drum]
        P2 --> P3[Wrap Concave 90 to 120 degrees]
        P3 --> P4[Sudden Discharge of Straw Rear]
        P3 --> P5[Grain Falls Through Perforations]
        P5 --> P6[Blower Cleaning]
        P6 --> P7[Clean Grain Bagging]
    end
    PTO[Tractor PTO 540 rpm] --> P2
    VB[V-Belt Pulley Step-Up Ratio ~1.2] --> P2
```

> **Key Feature:** The crop enters **tangentially** (perpendicular to the drum axis) and exits the same way. **Residence time = 1–2 seconds.** This is the **most common design** in India for wheat, paddy, and soybean.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Axial Flow Thresher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AF1[Axial Flow Thresher]
        direction LR
        Q1[Feeding Hopper] --> Q2[Spiral Feeder Auger]
        Q2 --> Q3[Axial Drum Pegs or Rasp Bars]
        Q3 --> Q4[Concave Wrap 180 to 360 degrees]
        Q4 --> Q5[Grain + Chaff Through Sieve]
        Q4 --> Q6[Straw Exits Axially Rear]
        Q5 --> Q7[Blower + Oscillating Sieve]
        Q7 --> Q8[Clean Grain Outlet]
        Q7 --> Q9[Chaff and Dust Exhaust]
    end
    PTO2[Tractor PTO] --> Q3
```

> **Key Feature:** The crop enters **axially** (parallel to the drum axis) and travels in a **helical/spiral path** along the drum due to the **angle of the rasp bars/peg teeth**. **Residence time = 5–15 seconds** (3–5× longer than tangential), giving **higher threshing efficiency** and **lower grain damage** for fragile crops like paddy.

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Tangential vs. Axial Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Thresher Type Selection] --> B{Crop Type and Quality Priority}
    B -->|Wheat, Soybean, Maize| C[Tangential Flow]
    B -->|Paddy, Sunflower, Mustard| D[Axial Flow]
    C --> C1[High Throughput, Short Residence]
    C --> C2[Higher Grain Damage, Simpler Design]
    D --> D1[Lower Throughput, Long Residence]
    D --> D2[Lower Grain Damage, Complex Design]
    C1 --> C3[Best for Dry, Hard Grains]
    D2 --> D4[Best for Fragile, Wet-Resistant Grains]
```

## 4.5 Manual Threshing Methods — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Manual Threshing Methods] --> M1[Beating with Stick Flail]
    M --> M2[Foot Treading]
    M --> M3[Animal Trampling on Hard Floor]
    M --> M4[Hand Rubbing on Stone]
    M1 --> N1[Crop Spread on Hard Surface, Repeated Striking]
    M2 --> N2[Barefoot or Sandal Workers Walk in Circle]
    M3 --> N3[Bullocks Tied to Central Pole, Walk in Circle]
    M4 --> N4[Small Seed Crops Spread on Sloped Stone]
    N1 --> O1[Grains Detached by Impact]
    N2 --> O2[Grains Detached by Rubbing and Pressure]
    N3 --> O3[Grains Detached by Combined Impact and Rubbing]
    N4 --> O4[Grains Detached by Friction Shear]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define threshing. List any four manual threshing methods used in India.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Threshing is the post-harvest operation of separating grains from the harvested crop (panicle, ear-head, or pod) by applying **impact, rubbing, and centrifugal forces**, with the objective of obtaining clean, undamaged grain.

**Four Manual Methods (½ Mark each, total 2 Marks):**

1. **Beating method** — Using a wooden flail/stick to strike sheaves spread on a hard floor.
2. **Foot treading** — Workers walk over the spread crop in a circular pattern.
3. **Animal trampling** — Bullocks tied to a central pole walk in circles over the crop.
4. **Hand rubbing** — Small quantities rubbed between palms or against a sloped stone.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse threshing with **winnowing**. Threshing = *separation of grain from the panicle*; Winnowing = *separation of grain from chaff using wind*. These are two **distinct** post-harvest operations. Don't blur them.

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between tangential flow and axial flow threshers (any 4 points).** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):** *(1 mark for tabular format, ½ mark for each valid difference)*

| S.No. | Parameter | Tangential Flow Thresher | Axial Flow Thresher |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crop entry direction** | Perpendicular to drum axis | Parallel to drum axis |
| 2 | **Crop travel path** | Straight tangential | Helical/spiral along drum |
| 3 | **Concave wrap angle** | 90° – 120° | 180° – 360° |
| 4 | **Residence time** | 1–2 seconds | 5–15 seconds |
| 5 | **Threshing efficiency** | 95–98% | 98–99.5% |
| 6 | **Grain damage** | Higher (1.5–3%) | Lower (0.5–1.5%) |
| 7 | **Best suited crop** | Wheat, soybean, maize | Paddy, sunflower, mustard |
| 8 | **Design complexity** | Simple | Complex (helical rasp bars) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not list "drum speed" or "PTO power" as differentiating points unless qualified with a specific crop. The **direction of crop flow and the wrap angle of the concave** are the *true* engineering distinctions.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The student answers **ONE of the two choices** (A or B) provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working of a tangential flow thresher**. Describe the role of the threshing cylinder, concave, blower, and sieves. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b)** A paddy thresher has a drum diameter of **0.55 m** and operates at a **peripheral speed of 22 m/s**. Calculate: **(i)** the drum speed in rpm, **(ii)** the V-belt pulley ratio if driven by a 540 rpm PTO, and **(iii)** the power required to accelerate the drum (moment of inertia 9 kg·m²) from rest to operating speed in 6 seconds. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Working Description (5 Marks for the narrative + 2 Marks for the sketch/component identification):**

1. **Feeding (1 Mark):** The harvested and dried paddy sheaves are fed manually or by a feeding auger into the **feeding chute** at the front of the thresher.

2. **Threshing (2 Marks):** The sheaves enter the threshing zone where they are struck by the **rasp bars** mounted on the high-speed rotating **cylinder/drum**. The sudden impact combined with the **rubbing action** between the rasp bar tips and the wires of the **concave** detaches the grains from the panicle.

3. **Separation (1 Mark):** The threshed grain, chaff, and broken straw fall through the **perforations of the concave** due to gravity and centrifugal force. The long straw is discharged from the rear of the machine.

4. **Cleaning (2 Marks):** The mixture falling below the concave passes through an **air stream from a centrifugal blower** that lifts the light chaff and dust. The heavier **clean grain** falls onto **oscillating sieves**, which further separate grain from unthreshed ear-heads and small impurities. The clean grain is collected in a bag at the outlet.

**Key Component Functions (1 Mark):**
- **Threshing cylinder** = impact and rubbing action provider.
- **Concave** = stationary perforated screen for grain separation.
- **Blower** = chaff and dust removal via air stream.
- **Sieves** = final sizing and cleaning.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $D = 0.55$ m, $v = 22$ m/s, $N_{PTO} = 540$ rpm, $I = 9$ kg·m², $t = 6$ s.

**(i) Drum speed in rpm (3 Marks):**

$$N = \frac{60 \times v}{\pi \times D} = \frac{60 \times 22}{\pi \times 0.55}$$

$$N = \frac{1320}{1.7279} = 763.9 \text{ rpm} \approx 764 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) V-belt pulley ratio (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{N_{drum}}{N_{PTO}} = \frac{764}{540} = 1.415$$

> A **step-up** pulley arrangement: e.g., driver pulley 200 mm diameter, driven pulley 283 mm diameter.

**(iii) Power for startup (2 Marks):**

$$\omega_f = \frac{2\pi \times 764}{60} = 80.01 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times (80.01)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 6401.6 = 28,807 \text{ J}$$

$$P_{avg} = \frac{28,807}{6} = 4,801 \text{ W} \approx 4.8 \text{ kW} \approx 6.4 \text{ HP}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** A common mistake is using the **wrong formula** for peripheral speed. Remember: $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ uses **D as diameter (not radius)**. Also, do not confuse **angular velocity** $\omega$ (rad/s) with **rotational speed** $N$ (rpm); they are related by $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working of an axial flow thresher**. Highlight the **advantages and disadvantages** over a tangential flow thresher. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(b)** A wheat thresher processes **2.5 tonnes per hour** with an input power of **12 kW**. Calculate: **(i)** the specific power consumption in kWh/t, **(ii)** the threshing efficiency if the unthreshed grain fraction is 1.5%, and **(iii)** the grain damage index if 30 kg of grain is broken per tonne processed. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Working Description (4 Marks):**

In an **axial flow thresher**, the crop is fed **parallel to the drum axis** through a feeding hopper fitted with a **spiral auger feeder**. The crop enters the **threshing cylinder** fitted with **rasp bars arranged helically** or with **peg teeth**. Due to the helical arrangement, the crop follows a **spiral path** through the cylinder-concave assembly.

The **concave wraps around 180° to 360°** of the drum circumference, providing a **long residence time** of 5–15 seconds. Grains are threshed by repeated impact and rubbing, then pass through the concave perforations. The cleaned straw is discharged axially at the rear end.

**Advantages over Tangential Flow (2 Marks):**

1. **Higher threshing efficiency (98–99.5%)** due to longer residence time.
2. **Lower grain damage (0.5–1.5%)** — ideal for fragile crops like paddy and sunflower.
3. **Better separation** because of multiple impact cycles.
4. **Lower scatter loss** as crop is fully enclosed in the drum-concave assembly.

**Disadvantages (1 Mark):**

1. **Lower throughput** per unit drum area.
2. **Higher specific power consumption** (kWh/t).
3. **Complex helical rasp bar design** — higher manufacturing cost.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $Q = 2.5$ t/h, $P_{input} = 12$ kW, unthreshed fraction = 1.5%, broken grain = 30 kg per tonne.

**(i) Specific power consumption (2 Marks):**

$$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{input}}{Q} = \frac{12}{2.5} = 4.8 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**(ii) Threshing efficiency (3 Marks):**

Let the total grain in input = 100 units (per tonne equivalent).
Unthreshed grain = 1.5 units, Threshed grain = 98.5 units.

$$\eta_t = \frac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100 = \frac{98.5}{100} \times 100 = 98.5\%$$

**(iii) Grain damage index (2 Marks):**

$$D_i = \frac{W_{broken}}{W_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{30}{1000} \times 100 = 3.0\%$$

> **Comment:** The damage index of **3.0% is higher than the commercial standard of 2%**, indicating either excessive drum speed, too narrow concave clearance, or over-dried crop. The operator should **reduce drum speed by 10–15%** and **widen the concave clearance by 3–5 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Many students calculate threshing efficiency as "100 − unthreshed %" which is correct **only when grain damage is zero**. In reality, use the full formula $\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100$ to be rigorous.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ **Threshing** = separation of **grain from panicle/ear-head/pod** using impact + rubbing + centrifugal action.
- ✅ **Threshing efficiency** formula: $\eta_t = \dfrac{W_g}{W_g + W_u} \times 100$; commercial standard is **≥ 98%**.
- ✅ **Peripheral drum speed** $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ is the **master equation** — commit it to memory.
- ✅ **Crop-specific peripheral speeds:** Paddy 18–22 m/s; Wheat 25–30 m/s; Maize 10–15 m/s; Soybean 12–18 m/s.
- ✅ **Tangential flow** = crop enters perpendicular to drum axis, **short residence (1–2 s)**, **90°–120°** concave wrap, **best for wheat/soybean/maize**.
- ✅ **Axial flow** = crop enters parallel to drum axis, **long helical path (5–15 s)**, **180°–360°** concave wrap, **best for paddy/sunflower/mustard**.
- ✅ **Concave clearance** controls the trade-off between threshing efficiency and grain damage — narrower = more aggressive.
- ✅ **Cleaning system** = **blower (air)** + **oscillating sieves (gravity + vibration)**.
- ✅ **Specific power consumption** for tangential = 3–5 kWh/t; axial = 5–8 kWh/t.
- ✅ **Manual methods**: beating, treading, animal trampling, hand rubbing — efficiency 75–85% only.
- ✅ **Mechanical methods** (power threshers) achieve 97–99% efficiency with 1–2% grain damage at 1–10 t/h throughput.
- ✅ **Drum sizing rule:** Drum area index $K = D \times L$ should be **0.20–0.35 m² per t/h of throughput**.
- ✅ **Tractor match:** Drum power + blower + sieve drive = typically 10–15 HP/t/h for tangential, 15–20 HP/t/h for axial.
- ✅ **Safety:** Always guard the PTO shaft, install a slip-clutch, and use a no-feed safety interlock (2024 KTU emphasizes this).
- ✅ **Round-hole concave** = wheat/paddy; **Slotted concave** = soybean/maize/sunflower.
- ✅ **Blower air velocity** must exceed the **terminal velocity of chaff** (~2–4 m/s) but stay below the **terminal velocity of clean grain** (~8–12 m/s) for effective separation.

> **🎯 Examiner's Final Tip:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, questions on threshing are often framed as *"Compare and contrast"* or *"Explain with sketch"* — always include a **labelled block diagram** to gain 1–2 extra marks, and **underline the formula symbols** in your answer to show the examiner you know the variables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Their Constructional Details and Cleaning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT613 - TRACTORS AND FARM EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Their Constructional Details and Cleaning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEMUT613 – Module 4):** The phrase *"Their Constructional Details and Cleaning Systems"* refers to the **primary and secondary tillage implements** covered in the module — principally the **Disc Plough**, **Disc Harrow**, and **Mould Board Plough**, along with the **scraper-based cleaning systems** that keep the soil-engaging surfaces free of sticky/wet soil.

---

## 1.1 What Are Tillage Implements?

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
Tillage implements are tractor-mounted or tractor-drawn **soil-engaging tools** designed to mechanically manipulate the soil to achieve a desired tilth for seedbed preparation, weed control, moisture conservation, and crop residue management. They are broadly classified as:

- **Primary Tillage Implements:** Operate on soil that has not been disturbed since the last crop. They invert, shatter, and loosen the soil deeply (e.g., Mould Board Plough, Disc Plough, Sub-Soiler, Chisel Plough).
- **Secondary Tillage Implements:** Refine the tilth created by primary tillage (e.g., Disc Harrow, Cultivator, Rotary Tiller).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Spatula & Dish Scrubber"**
> Imagine making a garden bed with a heavy spatula (the plough disc) that slices and turns the soil. As you work in wet, sticky clay, the soil clings to your spatula, making it useless. The **scraper** is like the rubber wiper on your kitchen's dish scrubber — a small but vital piece that constantly wipes the disc clean so it can keep slicing efficiently. Without the scraper (cleaning system), a disc plough in black cotton soil simply stops working within minutes.

---

## 1.2 Constructional Details — The Major Components

The three implements of focus in this module share a common structural skeleton. Below are the **standard KTU 2024 definitions** of every major component.

### 1.2.1 Mould Board Plough (MB Plough) — Constructional Details

The **Mould Board Plough** is a primary tillage implement with a curved metal board (the mould board) that lifts, inverts, and pulverizes the soil slice cut by the share.

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Share** | The leading, sharp, soil-cutting edge. Made of **high-carbon steel** or **chilled cast iron**. |
| **Mould Board** | A curved plate that receives the soil slice from the share and turns it over to one side. |
| **Landside** | A flat plate that runs against the furrow wall and absorbs side-thrust. |
| **Frog** | The central cast body to which share, mould board, and landside are bolted. |
| **Tail Piece / Tail Board** | Extension at the rear of the mould board that ensures complete inversion. |
| **Standard / Stalk** | Vertical shank connecting the bottom assembly to the mainframe. |
| **Mainframe / Beam** | The rigid horizontal bar to which all bottoms are attached. |
| **Hitch Assembly** | Three-point linkage connection (Category I/II). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Mould Board is Curved:** The curvature progressively turns the soil slice by **90° → 135° → 180°** along its length. This controlled inversion is what buries weeds, crop residue, and insect eggs.

---

### 1.2.2 Disc Plough — Constructional Details

The **Disc Plough** uses **concave steel discs** (instead of shares) to cut, lift, and invert the soil. It is preferred over the MB plough in **dry, hard, sticky, or stony soils**.

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Disc (Concave)** | A circular, concave steel plate (size: **600–750 mm Ø**) with a sharp bevelled cutting edge. |
| **Disc Scraper** | Cleaning device that removes sticky soil from the concave face of the disc. |
| **Disc Hub / Spool** | Central casting on which the disc is mounted and rotates. |
| **Standard / Disc Standards** | Forged shanks (one per disc) connecting the disc assembly to the mainframe. |
| **Mainframe (A-Frame or Box Type)** | The main load-carrying structure. |
| **Front Furrow Wheel / Landside Wheel** | A gauge wheel that absorbs side-draft and stabilizes the plough. |
| **Rear Furrow Wheel** | A gauge wheel on the rear bottom controlling depth. |
| **Hitch / Hitch Point** | Connects plough to tractor's three-point linkage. |
| **Bearing Assembly (Journal Type / Tapered Roller / Ball)** | Allows free rotation of the disc on the standard. |
| **Weight Box / Disc Angle Adjustment** | Often provided to adjust disc tilt, angle, and depth. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Pizza Cutter in Mud"**
> Think of each plough disc as a large, rolling pizza cutter. Instead of slicing dough, it rolls through the soil. Because it rotates, it does not get clogged with weeds or trash as easily as a fixed share. The **scraper** is the kitchen spatula that wipes mud off the rolling cutter.

**Key Disc Angles (KTU High-Yield):**

| Angle | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Disc Angle (α)** | Angle between plane of disc cut and direction of travel | **42° – 45°** |
| **Tilt Angle (β)** | Angle between disc plane and vertical | **15° – 25°** |
| **Scouring Angle** | Combined effect of α and β producing self-sharpening & cleaning | Resultant |

---

### 1.2.3 Disc Harrow — Constructional Details

A **Disc Harrow** is a secondary tillage implement used to break clods, pulverize soil, mix crop residue, and level the field. It uses **multiple discs arranged in gangs**.

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Disc** | Concave, sharp-edged discs (size: **450–600 mm Ø**), made of boron steel. |
| **Gang** | A set of discs mounted on a single shaft (gang bolt). |
| **Gang Frame / Gang Arbor** | The frame carrying the gang shaft, bearings, and scrapers. |
| **Gang Bolt** | Square axle on which discs and spools are assembled. |
| **Spool / Spacer** | Cast iron spacer between adjacent discs on the gang bolt. |
| **Bearing (Wood / Ball / Roller)** | Supports the gang shaft. |
| **Scraper Assembly** | Multi-blade scrapers cleaning each disc face. |
| **Main Frame** | Welded/fabricated structure holding 2 or 4 gangs. |
| **Transport Wheels** | Pneumatic wheels for road transport (in trailed-type harrows). |
| **Drawbar / Hitch** | Connects to tractor drawbar or three-point linkage. |
| **Weight Box** | Optional tray for adding ballast to increase penetration. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Multi-Pizza-Cutter Train"**
> A Disc Harrow is like a train of rolling pizza cutters (the gang of discs) towed behind the tractor. Each pizza cutter slices and lifts soil. As the train moves forward, scrapers (like windshield wipers) continuously clean each rolling disc so they keep cutting efficiently.

---

## 1.3 Cleaning Systems — The Scraper Mechanism

**Formal Definition:**
A **cleaning system** in disc-type tillage implements is the assembly of **scrapers** (and their mounting hardware) whose function is to **remove adhering soil, mud, crop residue, and moisture** from the **concave face of the rotating disc** to ensure continuous cutting efficiency, prevent balling-up of soil, and reduce draft.

### 1.3.1 Why Is a Cleaning System Necessary?

- **Wet/sticky soil** (e.g., black cotton soil) **adheres** to the concave disc face.
- A clogged disc **cannot cut** soil — it merely smears and compacts it.
- Un-cleaned discs increase **draft (pulling force)** dramatically.
- Scrapers **extend working hours** before manual cleaning is needed.

### 1.3.2 Types of Scrapers Used in KTU Syllabus

| Scraper Type | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Sole / Plain Scraper** | A single flat blade held against the disc face | Disc Plough |
| **Finger / Tine Scraper** | Tine-type scraper that removes trash | Disc Plough |
| **Double Scraper (Trash Scraper)** | Two scrapers — one cleaning, one removing trash | Disc Harrow |
| **Scraper Bar Assembly** | A single bar carrying multiple scrapers — one per disc | Disc Harrow |
| **Adjustable / Spring-Loaded Scraper** | Maintains constant contact as disc wears | Heavy Disc Plough |
| **Harrow Scraper with Deflector** | Cleans disc + deflects soil back into the cut | Disc Harrow |

---
