# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Working of a Disc Plough

The disc plough works on the principle of a **rolling coulter combined with a mould board curve**. Each disc is mounted at the bottom of an inclined standard. As the plough is towed, the disc rolls forward, slicing the soil due to its **disc angle (α)** and lifting it up the concave face due to its **tilt angle (β)**.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Penetration Phase:** Weight of the plough + soil reaction on disc edge → disc sinks to set depth.
2. **Cutting Phase:** Due to **disc angle (α = 42°–45°)**, the leading edge cuts a slice of soil.
3. **Lifting Phase:** Tilt angle **β** combined with α raises the soil slice up the concave surface.
4. **Inversion Phase:** The concave shape inverts and throws the slice into the previous furrow.
5. **Self-Cleaning Attempt:** Disc rotation throws some soil outward via centrifugal action — **but insufficient in sticky/wet soils**.
6. **Forced Cleaning Phase:** **Scrapers** remove the residual soil clinging to the concave face.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why doesn't the disc just slide?**
> Disc rotation is **driven by soil reaction** (not by a positive drive). The soil pushing against the concave face creates a moment about the disc axle, causing rotation. This is why **anti-friction bearings** are critical — without them, the disc would not rotate and the plough would not work.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Working of a Disc Harrow

A disc harrow has **two or four gangs** of discs. The gangs are set at an angle (gang angle, **θ**) to the direction of travel.

### Operational Steps

1. **Soil Engagement:** Discs penetrate due to their weight + weight-box ballast.
2. **Cutting:** Leading edge of each disc cuts the soil (cutting depth depends on gang angle).
3. **Pulverization:** The throwing action of the concave discs breaks clods.
4. **Mixing:** Top soil and sub-soil are mixed along with crop residue.
5. **Leveling:** Rear gangs (offset in opposite direction) level the soil.
6. **Cleaning:** Scraper bar assembly cleans each disc continuously.

### Key Concept: Gang Angle (θ)

| Gang Angle (θ) | Effect |
|---|---|
| **0°** | Discs act as rollers — no cutting |
| **10°–15°** | Light pulverization (secondary tillage) |
| **20°–25°** | Aggressive cutting & inversion (deep tillage) |
| **> 25°** | Excessive draft, soil throwing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *Draft of a disc harrow is directly proportional to (1 − cos θ) where θ is the gang angle.* More gang angle → more draft.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Working of the Scraper Cleaning System

### 2.3.1 Mechanics of Scraper Action

A scraper is essentially a **rigid blade** held against the **non-cutting (convex) face of the disc**, near the lower rear quadrant of the disc. As the disc rotates downward and backward (toward the furrow), soil sticks to the concave face. The scraper blade is positioned to:

- **Peel off** the soil layer by layer.
- **Deflect** it down into the furrow bottom.

### 2.3.2 Forces on a Scraper

The scraper must balance two competing requirements:

| Requirement | Consequence if violated |
|---|---|
| **Firm contact** with the disc face | Soil sticks, disc clogs |
| **Light contact** (low normal force) | Scraper wears, disc wears, scraper may break |

This is solved using:
- **Adjustable mounting bolts** (setscrew-loaded scraper).
- **Spring-loaded scraper** (constant contact despite wear).
- **Floating-type scraper** (pivoted to self-align).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all **engineering formulas and design parameters** required for solving numerical and theory questions on this topic in KTU examinations.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Standard Units / Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Diameter of plough disc, D** | $D = 600 \text{ to } 750 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Standard sizes: 600, 650, 700, 750 |
| 2 | **Diameter of harrow disc, Dₕ** | $D_h = 450 \text{ to } 600 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Standard: 450, 500, 560, 600 |
| 3 | **Disc thickness, t** | $t = 3 \text{ to } 6 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Boron steel discs |
| 4 | **Disc concavity depth, h** | $h \approx (0.10 \text{ to } 0.12) \, D$ | mm | Governs soil-lift capacity |
| 5 | **Disc angle (α)** | $\alpha = 42° \text{ to } 45°$ | degrees | Adjustable on plough |
| 6 | **Tilt angle (β)** | $\beta = 15° \text{ to } 25°$ | degrees | Adjustable on plough |
| 7 | **Gang angle of disc harrow (θ)** | $\theta = 10° \text{ to } 25°$ | degrees | Variable adjustment |
| 8 | **Width of cut per disc, Wₐ** | $W_a = D \cdot \sin\alpha$ | mm | Approximation (Kt = 1) |
| 9 | **Total width of cut, W** | $W = n \cdot W_a$ | mm | n = number of discs |
| 10 | **Theoretical field capacity, Cₐ** | $C_a = \dfrac{W \cdot S}{10}$ | ha/h | S = speed in km/h |
| 11 | **Effective field capacity, Cₑ** | $C_e = C_a \cdot \eta_f$ | ha/h | $\eta_f$ = field efficiency (**0.7–0.85**) |
| 12 | **Actual draft, Dₐ (per disc)** | $D_a = K_t \cdot d \cdot w$ | N | Kt = soil resistance, d = depth, w = width |
| 13 | **Total plough draft, F** | $F = n \cdot K_t \cdot d \cdot w$ | N | n = no. of bottoms |
| 14 | **Specific draft (KTU standard)** | $K_t = 25 \text{ to } 80 \text{ kPa}$ | kPa | Varies with soil type |
| 15 | **Soil reaction on disc, R** | $R = \sqrt{F^2 + S_s^2}$ | N | Sₛ = side draft force |
| 16 | **Power required at drawbar** | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot S}{3.6}$ | kW | S in km/h |
| 17 | **Tractor PTO power equivalent (rule)** | $P_{PTO} \approx 1.25 \cdot P_{drawbar}$ | kW | Considering transmission losses |

> **Critical Anti-Pattern Note (No Pipe Symbols):**
> In the formulas above, the absolute / magnitude notation has been avoided in table cells to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 table syntax rules. In handwritten/LaTeX solutions, students may write $\vert x \vert$ without ambiguity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Cleaning Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Significance:**
> - In **black cotton (Vertisol) soils** of central India, scrapers are the **single most important** reason disc ploughs are usable at all. Without scrapers, a disc plough becomes useless within **5–10 minutes**.
> - In **Kerala's laterite and clay soils** (especially during monsoon), scrapers are equally critical because soil sticks aggressively to discs.
> - **Spring-loaded scrapers** reduce operator fatigue and downtime.
> - Modern **precision-farming disc harrows** use **hydraulically actuated scrapers** for automated cleaning.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Width of Cut per Disc (Wₐ)

### Statement
The width of cut per disc of a disc plough is given by the geometric projection of the disc diameter on the direction perpendicular to travel.

### Given
- $D$ = Diameter of the plough disc (mm)
- $\alpha$ = Disc angle (degrees)
- $K_t$ = correction factor (≈ 1 for first approximation)

### Geometric Setup
Consider a disc of diameter $D$ mounted at disc angle $\alpha$ to the direction of travel. The disc plane intersects the ground along a chord. The width of this chord (perpendicular to travel) equals the width of soil slice cut.

### Derivation

The projection of the disc on the horizontal plane (assuming a flat ground and disc angle α between plane of disc cut and direction of travel) yields a chord whose half-length is $R \sin\alpha$, where $R = D/2$.

$$
W_a = 2 R \sin\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \sin\alpha
$$

$$
W_a = D \sin\alpha
$$

Multiplying by the correction factor $K_t$ (accounts for tilt, depth, and slip):

$$
\boxed{W_a = K_t \cdot D \cdot \sin\alpha}
$$

For first approximation, $K_t \approx 1$, so:

$$
W_a \approx D \sin\alpha
$$

**Units:** If $D$ is in metres, then $W_a$ is in metres. Typical values: For $D = 0.65$ m and $\alpha = 45°$:

$$
W_a = 0.65 \times \sin(45°) = 0.65 \times 0.7071 \approx 0.46 \text{ m} = 460 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Width of Cut (W)

### Given
- $n$ = number of discs (or bottoms)
- $W_a$ = width of cut per disc

### Derivation

Each disc cuts an independent slice, so the total width is the linear sum:

$$
W = n \cdot W_a
$$

$$
W = n \cdot K_t \cdot D \cdot \sin\alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU valuation:** In **mounted disc ploughs**, discs are arranged such that their cuts overlap or have a small gap. Some textbooks include an overlap factor $\eta_o \approx 0.95$. For KTU examinations, the simple form $W = n \cdot W_a$ is accepted.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Theoretical Field Capacity (Cₐ) and Effective Field Capacity (Cₑ)

### Given
- $W$ = total width of implement (m)
- $S$ = forward speed of tractor (km/h)

### Derivation

**Theoretical Field Capacity** is the area covered per unit time assuming 100% utilization:

$$
C_a = \frac{\text{Width} \times \text{Speed}}{\text{Unit conversion factor}}
$$

Converting speed from km/h to m/h:

$$
S_{m/h} = S \times 1000
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_a = \frac{W \times S \times 1000}{10^4} \quad \text{(ha/h)}
$$

Because $1 \text{ hectare} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
C_a = \frac{W \cdot S}{10} \quad \text{ha/h}
$$

where $W$ is in **metres** and $S$ is in **km/h**.

**Effective Field Capacity** accounts for time lost in turning, overlapping, refuelling, and operator pauses:

$$
C_e = C_a \times \eta_f
$$

$$
\boxed{C_e = \frac{W \cdot S \cdot \eta_f}{10} \quad \text{ha/h}}
$$

where $\eta_f$ = **field efficiency** (typical: **0.70 – 0.85**).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Draft and Power Requirement

### Given
- $K_t$ = specific draft or soil resistance (kPa)
- $d$ = depth of cut (m)
- $w$ = width of cut per bottom (m)
- $S$ = speed (km/h)
- $\eta_{trans}$ = tractor transmission efficiency ≈ **0.85**

### Derivation (Draft per bottom)

Draft of a single bottom is the product of cross-sectional area of soil cut and the specific soil resistance:

$$
D_a = K_t \cdot d \cdot w
$$

For $n$ bottoms:

$$
F_{total} = n \cdot K_t \cdot d \cdot w
$$

### Derivation (Power at Drawbar)

Power at drawbar (mechanical power needed at the hitch point):

$$
P_{DB} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v}{1000}
$$

where $v$ is forward speed in m/s. Converting $S$ (km/h) to $v$ (m/s):

$$
v = \frac{S \times 1000}{3600} = \frac{S}{3.6}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{DB} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot S}{3.6 \times 1000} \quad \text{(kW)}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\boxed{P_{DB} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot S}{3600} \quad \text{kW, when } S \text{ in m/s}}
$$

### Derivation (PTO Power Required)

Accounting for tractor transmission and wheel slip losses:

$$
P_{PTO} = \frac{P_{DB}}{\eta_{transmission}}
$$

For typical KTU assumptions:

$$
P_{PTO} \approx 1.25 \times P_{DB}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Disc Angle vs. Tilt Angle vs. Gang Angle — Operational Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a **KTU-favourite** comparison table that frequently appears in KTU Part-A (3-mark) questions.**

| Angle | Symbol | Range | Effect on Cutting | Effect on Inversion | Effect on Pulverization | Effect on Draft |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Disc Angle (Plough)** | $\alpha$ | 42° – 45° | Increases with α | Increases with α | Slightly increases | Increases |
| **Tilt Angle (Plough)** | $\beta$ | 15° – 25° | Increases with β | Slightly increases | Increases | Increases |
| **Gang Angle (Harrow)** | $\theta$ | 10° – 25° | Increases with θ | Decreases (mixes instead) | Increases | Increases (proportional to 1 − cos θ) |
| **Scraper Angle** | $\phi$ | 30° – 45° (with disc face) | — | — | — | Decreases soil adhesion |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Problem
A 3-bottom disc plough with disc diameter 600 mm operates at a disc angle of 45° and depth of 200 mm in a soil with specific draft 50 kPa. The tractor forward speed is 5 km/h. Calculate:
(a) Width of cut per disc
(b) Total width of cut
(c) Total draft
(d) Power at drawbar (in kW)
(e) Effective field capacity, assuming field efficiency = 0.8.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $\alpha = 45°$
- $n = 3$
- $d = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $K_t = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $S = 5 \text{ km/h}$
- $\eta_f = 0.8$

**Step (a): Width of cut per disc**

$$
W_a = D \sin\alpha = 0.6 \times \sin(45°) = 0.6 \times 0.7071 = 0.4243 \text{ m}
$$

$$
W_a \approx 0.424 \text{ m} = 424 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step (b): Total width of cut**

$$
W = n \cdot W_a = 3 \times 0.4243 = 1.2728 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{W \approx 1.273 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step (c): Total draft**

$$
F = n \cdot K_t \cdot d \cdot W_a = 3 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.20 \times 0.4243
$$

$$
F = 3 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.08486 = 12{,}729 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 12.73 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step (d): Power at drawbar**

$$
P_{DB} = \frac{F \cdot S}{3.6} = \frac{12{,}729 \times 5}{3.6} = \frac{63{,}645}{3.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{DB} \approx 17{,}679 \text{ W} \approx 17.68 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step (e): Effective field capacity**

$$
C_a = \frac{W \cdot S}{10} = \frac{1.2728 \times 5}{10} = 0.6364 \text{ ha/h}
$$

$$
C_e = C_a \times \eta_f = 0.6364 \times 0.8
$$

$$
\boxed{C_e \approx 0.509 \text{ ha/h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always include units at every intermediate step. Marks are awarded for the **method**, not just the final number.

---

## 3.7 Energy & Force Analysis of a Scraper

### Forces Acting on a Scraper Blade

A scraper pressed against a rotating disc experiences:

| Force | Symbol | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Normal reaction from disc | $N$ | Perpendicular to disc face, pushing scraper away |
| Frictional force | $f$ | Tangential to disc face, opposing relative motion |
| Soil adhesion force | $F_a$ | Pulling scraper along with soil |
| Scraper's reaction to support arm | $R_s$ | Along the support |

**For equilibrium of scraper:**

$$
\sum F_{\parallel} = 0 \Rightarrow f = F_a \quad \text{(soil adhesion balanced by friction)}
$$

$$
\sum F_{\perp} = 0 \Rightarrow R_s = N \quad \text{(support reaction balances disc push)}
$$

The scraper design must ensure $f \geq F_a$ so that soil peels off rather than accumulating.

## 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List the main components of a disc plough and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The main components of a disc plough are:

1. **Disc** (1 mark) — A circular concave steel plate (600–750 mm diameter) with a sharp bevelled cutting edge that cuts, lifts, and inverts the soil.
2. **Standard / Disc Standard** (1 mark) — A forged shank that connects the disc assembly to the mainframe; tilts the disc at the desired angle.
3. **Scraper** (1 mark) — A blade held against the concave face of the disc that removes adhering soil, preventing clogging.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the function of a scraper in a disc plough? Why is it essential in black cotton soil?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Function of Scraper (1.5 marks):** A scraper is a cleaning device mounted against the concave face of the plough disc. It continuously removes sticky soil and crop residue adhering to the disc, ensuring efficient cutting and preventing soil balling.
- **Why essential in black cotton soil (1.5 marks):** Black cotton soil is highly cohesive and clayey. When wet, it strongly adheres to the disc's concave face. Without a scraper, the disc clogs within minutes, draft increases dramatically, and the plough becomes inoperative. Scrapers enable continuous operation in such sticky soils.

---

## 5.3 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module pattern, students answer **either Option A or Option B** in Part B. Below are two complete alternative questions.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, describe the constructional details of a disc plough. Explain the functions of each component. Also discuss the types of scrapers (cleaning systems) used in disc ploughs.**

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the constructional details of a disc plough with a sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

A **disc plough** is a primary tillage implement that uses concave steel discs instead of shares to cut, lift, and invert the soil. It is particularly suitable for **dry, hard, sticky, or stony soils** where mould board ploughs are ineffective.

**Constructional Details (7 marks distributed as below):**

1. **Disc (2 marks):** A circular, concave steel plate of diameter **600–750 mm**, thickness **3–6 mm**, made of **boron steel** or **high-carbon steel**. The cutting edge is bevelled (typically **25°–30°** bevel angle) for self-sharpening. Disc concavity depth = **10–12% of diameter**.

2. **Disc Hub and Bearings (1 mark):** The disc is mounted on a **hub/spool** fitted with **anti-friction bearings** (tapered roller or ball bearings). These allow free rotation of the disc under soil reaction.

3. **Standard / Disc Standard (1 mark):** A heavy forged-steel shank connecting the disc hub to the mainframe. It holds the disc at the required **disc angle (α = 42°–45°)** and **tilt angle (β = 15°–25°)**.

4. **Mainframe (1 mark):** A rigid, welded/fabricated steel structure (box-type or A-frame) to which all standards are bolted. It transfers the pull force from the tractor to the discs.

5. **Hitch Assembly (0.5 marks):** Three-point linkage connection (Category I or II) for mounted ploughs.

6. **Furrow Wheels (0.5 marks):** Front and rear furrow wheels (landsideless or with landside) absorb side-draft and control depth.

7. **Scraper (1 mark):** Held against the concave face of the disc to remove adhering soil. (Detailed in Part b.)

**Sketch (will be drawn by student):**

```
        ┌── Hitch Point
        │
      [A-Frame]
        │
   ═══════════════════ Mainframe
   │    │    │    │
   S1   S2   S3   S4   Standards
   │    │    │    │
  ◯    ◯    ◯    ◯   Concave Discs
  ▓    ▓    ▓    ▓   Scrapers
        │
   ⊙          ⊙      Furrow Wheels
```

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the types of scrapers (cleaning systems) used in disc ploughs.**

**Model Answer:**

The **scraper (cleaning system)** is a critical component that maintains the cutting efficiency of the disc by removing sticky soil. The following types are used:

1. **Sole / Plain Scraper (2 marks):** A single, flat steel blade mounted on a bracket fixed to the standard. It rides lightly against the concave face near the lower rear quadrant. Most common type.

2. **Finger / Tine Scraper (1.5 marks):** Has multiple tines/fingers instead of a solid blade. Effective in trashy soils as it allows residue to pass without balling.

3. **Double / Twin Scraper (1.5 marks):** Two scrapers in tandem — one cleans the disc face, the other deflects cleaned soil back into the furrow.

4. **Spring-Loaded Scraper (1 mark):** Uses a tension spring to maintain constant contact pressure as the disc wears down. Reduces operator adjustment.

5. **Adjustable Scraper (1 mark):** Mounted via slotted bracket allowing angular and proximity adjustment relative to the disc.

**Working Principle:** As the disc rotates, sticky soil is lifted by the concave face. The scraper blade is positioned so its leading edge meets the disc face at an angle of about **30°–45°**, peeling off the soil in a thin layer and deflecting it into the open furrow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget to mention the **scraper's location** (non-cutting convex side, near lower rear quadrant). Lose 1 mark if missing.
> - Students confuse **disc angle (α)** with **tilt angle (β)**. Memorize: α is horizontal, β is vertical.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a) Explain the constructional details of a disc harrow with a labelled diagram. (b) Describe in detail the scraper (cleaning) system used in a disc harrow. Compare the cleaning requirements of a disc plough and a disc harrow.**

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Constructional details of a disc harrow.**

**Model Answer:**

A **disc harrow** is a secondary tillage implement used for pulverizing soil, breaking clods, mixing crop residue, and leveling the field. It consists of two or four gangs of concave discs.

**Constructional Details (7 marks distributed):**

1. **Disc (1.5 marks):** Concave, sharp-edged steel discs of diameter **450–600 mm**, thickness **3–5 mm**, made of **boron steel**. Smaller than plough discs.

2. **Gang (1.5 marks):** A set of **5–13 discs** mounted on a common square axle (gang bolt). Discs are separated by cast-iron **spools/spacers**.

3. **Gang Frame / Arbor (1 mark):** The frame carrying the gang shaft, bearings, and scrapers. Each gang is mounted on the main frame at a **gang angle θ = 10°–25°** from the direction of travel.

4. **Bearings (1 mark):** The gang shaft rotates in **wood bearings, ball bearings, or roller bearings** mounted on the gang frame.

5. **Mainframe (1 mark):** Welded steel structure holding the front and rear gangs. Rear gangs are set at an opposite angle to offset side-thrust.

6. **Hitch and Transport Wheels (0.5 marks):** Drawbar connection for trailed type, or three-point hitch for mounted type. Pneumatic transport wheels for road travel.

7. **Weight Box (0.5 marks):** A ballast tray on the mainframe to add weight for deeper penetration.

**Sketch:**

```
  Tractor Drawbar ──→ ┌─────────────────────┐
                      │     Main Frame      │
                      └──┬──────────────┬───┘
                    Front Gang      Rear Gang
                  (θ = 20° Right)  (θ = 20° Left)
                    ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯       ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯
                    ▓ ▓ ▓ ▓ ▓       ▓ ▓ ▓ ▓ ▓  ← Scrapers
```

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Scraper system in a disc harrow and comparison with disc plough.**

**Model Answer:**

**Scraper System in Disc Harrow (4 marks):**

1. **Scraper Bar Assembly:** A single horizontal bar running parallel to the gang bolt, carrying one scraper blade per disc.

2. **Individual Scrapers:** Each blade is bolted to the bar via a clamp, allowing individual adjustment.

3. **Trash Scrapers:** Wider scrapers placed ahead of the cutting scrapers to remove crop residue before it sticks.

4. **Deflector Scrapers:** Curved scrapers that deflect cleaned soil back into the cut zone.

5. **Spring-Loaded Floating Scrapers:** Pivoted at one end, allowing the scraper to float against the disc as it wears.

**Comparison: Disc Plough vs. Disc Harrow Cleaning Requirements (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Disc Plough | Disc Harrow |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of scrapers** | One per disc | Multiple per disc (bar carries several) |
| **Operating speed** | Slow (3–5 km/h), soil sticking is high | Faster (6–10 km/h), centrifugal force helps cleaning |
| **Soil moisture sensitivity** | Very high (operates in hard/dry/sticky soils) | Moderate (operates in already-tilled soil) |
| **Scraper robustness** | Heavy-duty, high spring force | Lighter duty, simple bar scrapers |
| **Maintenance frequency** | Daily adjustment needed | Weekly or per-season adjustment |
| **Criticality** | Essential — without scrapers, plough fails | Important but less critical due to higher speed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q B:**
> - When drawing the disc harrow, students often forget to show the **opposite gang angles** of front and rear gangs. Lose 1 mark if missing.
> - For the comparison table, students sometimes confuse **gang angle θ** with **disc angle α** (which is for ploughs, not harrows). Gang angle is the relevant parameter for harrows.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

### ✅ Constructional Details — Mould Board Plough
- [ ] Share — cutting edge; chilled cast iron or high-carbon steel.
- [ ] Mould board — curved; inverts soil progressively (90°→180°).
- [ ] Landside — absorbs side-thrust.
- [ ] Frog — central casting joining share, mould board, landside.
- [ ] Standard, mainframe, hitch — load path.

### ✅ Constructional Details — Disc Plough
- [ ] **Disc diameter: 600–750 mm** (memorize the range).
- [ ] **Disc angle α = 42°–45°**; **Tilt angle β = 15°–25°**.
- [ ] Concave disc, bevelled edge (self-sharpening).
- [ ] Standard (forged shank), mainframe (box/A-frame), 3-point hitch.
- [ ] Front + rear furrow wheels for side-draft absorption.
- [ ] Anti-friction bearings (mandatory for free rotation).

### ✅ Constructional Details — Disc Harrow
- [ ] **Disc diameter: 450–600 mm**.
- [ ] **Gang angle θ = 10°–25°** (varies from light to aggressive).
- [ ] Gang = set of 5–13 discs on a square gang bolt with spools.
- [ ] Wood, ball, or roller bearings.
- [ ] Weight box for ballast, transport wheels for trailed models.
- [ ] Front and rear gangs in opposite directions (cancel side-thrust).

### ✅ Cleaning Systems (Scrapers)
- [ ] **Location:** Non-cutting (convex) side of disc, near lower rear quadrant.
- [ ] **Types:** Sole, finger, double, spring-loaded, adjustable, deflector, trash.
- [ ] **Critical for:** Wet, sticky, black cotton, and clay soils.
- [ ] **Working angle of scraper blade:** ~30°–45° to disc face.
- [ ] **Maintenance:** Daily inspection, spring tension check, wear adjustment.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] $W_a = D \sin\alpha$ (width per disc)
- [ ] $W = n \cdot W_a$ (total width)
- [ ] $C_a = \dfrac{W \cdot S}{10}$ ha/h (theoretical field capacity)
- [ ] $C_e = C_a \cdot \eta_f$ (effective field capacity, $\eta_f \approx 0.7–0.85$)
- [ ] $F = n \cdot K_t \cdot d \cdot w$ (total draft)
- [ ] $P_{DB} = \dfrac{F \cdot S}{3.6}$ kW (power at drawbar)
- [ ] $P_{PTO} \approx 1.25 \times P_{DB}$

### ✅ One-Liners for Quick Answer Recall
- [ ] *“Scraper sits on the convex side, near the lower rear quadrant of the disc.”*
- [ ] *“Disc plough is for primary tillage; Disc harrow is for secondary tillage.”*
- [ ] *“Disc angle controls cutting; Tilt angle controls penetration and inversion.”*
- [ ] *“Gang angle controls aggressiveness of disc harrow.”*
- [ ] *“Black cotton soil + no scraper = plough failure in 5 minutes.”*
